name: POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS

topics.title: Basic Post-Harvest Physiology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Post-Harvest Physiology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Post-Harvest Physiology** is the branch of plant/food science that systematically studies the **continuing biological, biochemical, and physical changes** that occur in plant-derived food commodities (fruits, vegetables, flowers, cereals, tubers, and seeds) **after they have been detached from the parent plant** and until they reach the consumer's table or the end of their useful storage life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The study of life processes of harvested plant tissues — including respiration, transpiration, ethylene biosynthesis, ripening, and senescence — and the influence of intrinsic and extrinsic factors on the shelf-life and quality of horticultural produce."*

In precise physiological terms, once a fruit, vegetable, or flower is harvested, it is **separated from its source of water, minerals, and photosynthate**, but it does NOT die immediately. It remains a **living, respiring, transpiring, and metabolically active tissue** that continues to undergo **catabolic (breakdown) and anabolic (synthetic) reactions**, eventually culminating in **senescence and death**.

The three foundational physiological events governing the post-harvest life of any produce are:

| # | Physiological Process | Net Effect on Produce |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Respiration** | Substrate (sugar/starch) catabolism → CO₂ + H₂O + **Energy (ATP)** + Heat |
| 2 | **Transpiration** | Water vapour loss from surface → **Shrivelling, weight loss, turgor loss** |
| 3 | **Ethylene Biosynthesis & Ripening** | Hormonal trigger → **Softening, colour, aroma, sugar accumulation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Post-Harvest Physiology matter in Food Technology?**
> Because **up to 30–40% of harvested produce is lost** between farm and fork in developing countries (FAO estimate). Every successful post-harvest technology — cold storage, MAP, CA storage, edible coatings, 1-MCP treatment — is fundamentally an attempt to **manipulate one of these three physiological processes**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Candle in a Lantern"

Imagine a freshly harvested apple is like a **lit candle inside a closed lantern**:

- The **candle wax** = the **stored carbohydrates (sugars, starch, organic acids)** inside the fruit.
- The **wick burning** = **respiration** (slow combustion of those reserves to keep the fruit alive).
- The **heat of the flame** = the **respiratory heat** that must be removed by refrigeration.
- The **water droplets fogging the glass** = **transpiration** (moisture loss from the produce).
- The **smoke and scent** that escape = **ethylene gas** and **volatile aroma compounds**.
- The moment the **flame finally gutters out** = **senescence and death** of the produce.

> [!TIP]
> **Life lesson for KTU students:** A harvested fruit has a *finite* internal fuel reserve. **Slowing the flame** (lowering respiration by cooling, lowering O₂, or raising CO₂) is the *only* way to extend the lantern's glow. You cannot add new wax once the fruit is detached.

---

## 1.3 Key Physiological Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Fresh Produce) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Respiration rate (low) | Rᵣ | 5 – 10 | **mg CO₂ / kg·h** |
| Respiration rate (moderate) | Rᵣ | 10 – 20 | **mg CO₂ / kg·h** |
| Respiration rate (high) | Rᵣ | 20 – 40 | **mg CO₂ / kg·h** |
| Respiration rate (very high) | Rᵣ | > 40 | **mg CO₂ / kg·h** |
| Ethylene production rate | C₂H₄ | 0.01 – 100 | **µL / kg·h** |
| Transpiration (water loss) rate | Δm | 0.1 – 5 | **% fresh weight / day** |
| Respiratory Quotient (RQ) | RQ | 0.7 – 1.3 (most cases ≈ 1) | **dimensionless** |
| Activation Energy of respiration | Eₐ | ~ 50 – 80 | **kJ / mol** |
| Q₁₀ (temperature coefficient) | Q₁₀ | 2 – 3 (typical for produce) | **dimensionless** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU board favourite:** The **Q₁₀ rule** states that for every **10 °C rise** in storage temperature, the respiration rate of fresh produce **roughly doubles or triples**. This single fact governs the entire cold-chain logistics industry.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Produce by Physiological Behaviour

KTU frequently tests whether the student can categorise produce into the correct physiological group:

| Class | Defining Feature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Climacteric fruits** | Show a **respiratory peak (climacteric rise)** and **autocatalytic ethylene burst** during ripening | Apple, Banana, Tomato, Mango, Papaya, Avocado, Pear |
| **Non-climacteric fruits** | Show **no respiratory peak**; ripen gradually; require ethylene to be present at harvest | Citrus (orange, lemon), Grape, Strawberry, Pineapple, Pomegranate, Cherry |
| **Vegetables (leafy)** | Very high respiration; rapid wilting | Spinach, Lettuce, Cabbage |
| **Vegetables (root/tuber)** | Low respiration; long dormancy | Potato, Carrot, Onion, Beetroot |
| **Flowers (cut)** | Very high respiration; extreme ethylene sensitivity | Rose, Carnation, Jasmine |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Respiration — The Central Engine of Post-Harvest Life

Respiration is the **mitochondrial oxidative breakdown** of organic substrates (primarily glucose, fructose, sucrose, and stored starch) to release **energy (ATP)**, **CO₂**, **H₂O**, and **heat**.

### 2.1.1 Overall Aerobic Respiration Equation

$$ \text{C}_{6}\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_{6} \; + \; 6\,\text{O}_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; 6\,\text{CO}_{2} \; + \; 6\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \; + \; \Delta G \quad (\approx -2870\;\text{kJ/mol}) $$

The released energy is captured in the form of **ATP** (~ 30–32 ATP per glucose), with the balance lost as **respiratory heat (vital heat)** that must be removed by refrigeration.

### 2.1.2 Anaerobic Respiration (Fermentation) — Why It Is Harmful

When O₂ falls below the **fermentative threshold** (typically < 1–3 % O₂ in the tissue), respiration switches to **anaerobic glycolysis**:

$$ \text{C}_{6}\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_{6} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{5}\text{OH} \; + \; 2\,\text{CO}_{2} \; + \; \text{Energy (2 ATP only)} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Anaerobic respiration produces **acetaldehyde and ethanol** that accumulate → off-flavour, off-odour, tissue browning, and ultimately **fermentative spoilage**. This is why Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage uses **O₂ levels > 1.5 %** — below this, the damage exceeds the benefit.

### 2.1.3 The Four Sequential Stages of Respiration

- **Glycolysis** (cytoplasm): glucose → 2 pyruvate + 2 ATP + 2 NADH
- **Pyruvate decarboxylation** (mitochondrial matrix): pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH
- **Krebs / TCA Cycle** (mitochondrial matrix): Acetyl-CoA → 3 CO₂ + ATP + NADH + FADH₂
- **Electron Transport Chain / Oxidative Phosphorylation** (inner mitochondrial membrane): NADH/FADH₂ + O₂ → H₂O + ~26 ATP

The **net stoichiometry** of glycolysis is:

$$ \text{Glucose} + 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{Pyruvate} + 2\,\text{NADH} + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{H}^+ $$

### 2.1.4 Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric — The Big Distinction

| Property | Climacteric | Non-Climacteric |
|---|---|---|
| **Respiratory pattern** | Distinct **climacteric peak** (CO₂ + 2–5×) | Smooth, **monotonic decline** |
| **Ethylene** | **Autocatalytic** burst (> 1 µL/kg·h) | Basal, low, **non-autocatalytic** |
| **Ripening after harvest** | Continues post-harvest (banana) | Must ripen on plant (grape) |
| **Response to exogenous ethylene** | Catalyses its own production | Only speeds up senescence; no autocatalysis |
| **Best harvest stage** | Mature-green (firm) | Fully ripe on plant |

---

## 2.2 Transpiration (Water Loss) — The Silent Spoiler

Transpiration is the **diffusion of water vapour** from the moist internal cells of the produce, through the cuticle/stomata/lenticels, into the surrounding atmosphere. It is the principal cause of **shrivelling, weight loss, and turgor collapse**.

The driving force is the **water vapour pressure deficit (VPD)** between the inside of the fruit and the ambient air.

$$ \text{Water Loss Rate} \; = \; k \cdot A \cdot (\text{VPD}) $$

where $k$ is the **permeability of the cuticle** and $A$ is the **surface area** of the produce.

### 2.2.1 Permeability Hierarchy of Plant Surfaces

| Surface Type | Relative Permeability | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Cuticle + wax bloom** (lowest) | Very low | Apple, Plum, Grape |
| **Cuticle only** | Moderate | Tomato, Banana, Mango |
| **Stomata-bearing skin** | High | Leafy vegetables, Cucumber |
| **Lenticels (root crops)** | High | Potato, Carrot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why refrigerate and humidify storage rooms?**
> Refrigeration lowers temperature (lowers VPD), and humidification (95–98 % RH) reduces the vapour-pressure gap, **drastically slowing transpiration**. Both are KTU classic 14-mark discussion points.

---

## 2.3 Ethylene (C₂H₄) — The Fruit-Ripening Hormone

Ethylene (H₂C=CH₂) is a **simple gaseous plant hormone** (the only gaseous phytohormone) that regulates ripening, abscission, senescence, and stress responses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold of biological action:** As little as **0.1 µL/L (100 ppb)** of ethylene in the storage atmosphere is enough to trigger ripening in climacteric fruit. Hence, **ethylene scrubbers (KMnO₄)** are essential in commercial cold rooms.

---

## 2.4 Senescence — The Final Phase

Senescence is the **final, genetically programmed, deteriorative phase** of the harvested produce, leading to death. It is characterised by:

- **Membrane lipid degradation** (↑ free fatty acids, malondialdehyde)
- **Chlorophyll breakdown** (yellowing)
- **Protein & RNA degradation** (loss of functional enzymes)
- **Cell wall loosening** (softening)
- **Loss of cellular compartmentalisation**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Senescence is **NOT a passive decay**. It is an **active, energy-requiring, gene-regulated process**. The student is often marked down for calling senescence "decay" or "rotting". These are **distinct concepts** — rotting implies microbial action; senescence is intrinsic physiological breakdown.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$ \text{C}_{6}\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_{6} + 6\text{O}_{2} \to 6\text{CO}_{2} + 6\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + E $$ | Aerobic respiration | Define respiration |
| 2 | $$ \text{RQ} = \frac{\text{moles CO}_2\;\text{released}}{\text{moles O}_2\;\text{consumed}} $$ | Respiratory Quotient | Identify substrate being respired |
| 3 | $$ R_{T_2} = R_{T_1} \cdot Q_{10}^{\left( T_2 - T_1 \right) / 10} $$ | Q₁₀ temperature rule | Calculate respiration rate at new T |
| 4 | $$ \text{Water Loss} \propto A \cdot (P_{in} - P_{out}) $$ | Transpiration rate | Explain shrivelling |
| 5 | $$ \text{Methionine} \xrightarrow{\text{MET-SAM-SYN}} \text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{ACS}} \text{ACC} \xrightarrow{\text{ACO}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 $$ | Yang cycle (ethylene pathway) | Describe ethylene biosynthesis |
| 6 | $$ \text{1-MCP} + \text{ETR} \to \text{blocked receptor} $$ | Anti-ethylene action | 1-MCP mechanism |
| 7 | $$ \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2870\;\text{kJ/mol}\;\text{(glucose oxidation)} $$ | Energy yield | Compare aerobic vs anaerobic |

**RQ values to memorise (very high KTU yield):**

| Substrate being respired | RQ value |
|---|---|
| Carbohydrate (sugar / starch) | **1.0** |
| Lipid (fat / oil) | **< 1** (≈ 0.7) |
| Organic acid (malic, citric) | **> 1** (≈ 1.3 – 1.5) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Knowledge

1. **Cold Chain Design:** Engineers use the Q₁₀ equation to size refrigeration load, accounting for respiratory heat.
2. **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage:** Setting O₂ (1–3 %) and CO₂ (0–5 %) to slow respiration.
3. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Polymer films designed to match produce respiration rate to maintain equilibrium O₂/CO₂.
4. **Edible Coatings:** Chitosan, Aloe vera gel, wax coatings — they physically block the cuticle, reducing transpiration.
5. **1-MCP Treatment (SmartFresh™):** Commercial use of a competitive ethylene-receptor blocker in apple storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient Equation

The respiration rate of fresh produce follows an **Arrhenius-type** temperature dependence. We will derive the Q₁₀ formula step by step.

**Step 1 — Start with the Arrhenius equation** for the rate constant *k* of a biological reaction:

$$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$ \ln k = \ln A \; - \; \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

**Step 3 — Write the equation at two different temperatures, $T_1$ and $T_2$:**

$$ \ln k_1 = \ln A \; - \; \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_1} $$

$$ \ln k_2 = \ln A \; - \; \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_2} $$

**Step 4 — Subtract equation (1) from equation (2):**

$$ \ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_2} \; + \; \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_1} $$

$$ \ln \!\left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right) $$

**Step 5 — By definition, $Q_{10} = k_{T+10} / k_T$** (i.e., the ratio of rates when T rises by exactly 10 K).

If we set $T_2 = T_1 + 10$, then:

$$ \ln Q_{10} = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_1 + 10} \right) $$

**Step 6 — Algebraically simplify the bracketed term:**

$$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_1 + 10} \;=\; \frac{(T_1 + 10) - T_1}{T_1(T_1 + 10)} \;=\; \frac{10}{T_1(T_1 + 10)} $$

**Step 7 — Substituting back:**

$$ \ln Q_{10} = \frac{E_a \cdot 10}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot (T_1 + 10)} $$

**Step 8 — Solving for $E_a$ (in case the examiner provides Q₁₀ and asks for activation energy):**

$$ E_a = \frac{R \cdot T_1 \cdot (T_1 + 10) \cdot \ln Q_{10}}{10} $$

**Step 9 — Final usable Q₁₀ form (the version every KTU student memorises):**

$$ R_{T_2} = R_{T_1} \cdot Q_{10}^{\,\Delta T \,/\, 10} $$

where $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example on Q₁₀

> **Problem:** The respiration rate of mangoes at 10 °C is **15 mg CO₂ / kg·h**. If the storage temperature is raised to 25 °C, what is the new respiration rate? Assume $Q_{10} = 2.5$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the known values:**

$$ R_{T_1} = 15 \;\text{mg/kg·h},\;\; T_1 = 10\;°\text{C},\;\; T_2 = 25\;°\text{C},\;\; Q_{10} = 2.5 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T$:**

$$ \Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 25 - 10 = 15\;°\text{C} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Q₁₀ formula:**

$$ R_{T_2} = R_{T_1} \cdot Q_{10}^{\,\Delta T / 10} $$

$$ R_{T_2} = 15 \cdot (2.5)^{15/10} = 15 \cdot (2.5)^{1.5} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $(2.5)^{1.5}$:**

$$ (2.5)^{1.5} = \sqrt{(2.5)^3} = \sqrt{15.625} \approx 3.953 $$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 15:**

$$ R_{T_2} = 15 \times 3.953 \approx 59.3\;\text{mg CO}_2/\text{kg·h} $$

**Interpretation:** A 15 °C temperature rise causes the respiration rate to **quadruple** (from 15 to ~60 mg/kg·h). The mango would now be classified as a **very high respiring** commodity, exhausting its sugar reserves ~4× faster. **This is precisely why the cold chain is non-negotiable.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Respiratory Quotient (RQ) from Mass Balance

The Respiratory Quotient is the **molar ratio of CO₂ produced to O₂ consumed**.

$$ \text{RQ} \;=\; \frac{\text{moles CO}_2\;\text{produced}}{\text{moles O}_2\;\text{consumed}} $$

**Derivation for carbohydrate (RQ = 1):**

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{RQ}_{\text{carb}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.0 \quad \blacksquare $$

**Derivation for a typical lipid (tripalmitin, RQ ≈ 0.7):**

$$ 2\,\text{C}_{51}\text{H}_{98}\text{O}_6 + 145\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 102\,\text{CO}_2 + 98\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{RQ}_{\text{lipid}} = \frac{102}{145} \approx 0.70 \quad \blacksquare $$

**Derivation for malic acid (RQ ≈ 1.33):**

$$ \text{C}_4\text{H}_6\text{O}_5 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{RQ}_{\text{acid}} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33 \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** By experimentally measuring RQ of a stored commodity, a food scientist can **infer the dominant respiratory substrate** — useful in detecting the shift from sugar to lipid respiration during prolonged storage of oilseeds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Yang Cycle for Ethylene Biosynthesis

Ethylene is biosynthesised from the amino acid **L-methionine** through the **Yang cycle (1979)**, the only KTU-mandated pathway in this module.

**Step 1 — Activation of methionine by ATP:**

$$ \text{L-Methionine} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{SAM synthase}} \textbf{SAM} \;(\text{S-adenosyl methionine}) $$

**Step 2 — Conversion of SAM to ACC (1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid):**

$$ \text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{ACS (ACC synthase)}} \textbf{ACC} + 5'\text{-Methylthioadenosine (MTA)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **ACC synthase (ACS)** is the **rate-limiting enzyme** of ethylene biosynthesis. KTU loves asking: *"Which is the rate-limiting step in ethylene biosynthesis?"* — Answer: **ACS-catalysed step**.

**Step 3 — Oxidation of ACC to ethylene by ACC oxidase (ACO):**

$$ \text{ACC} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{ACO (ACC oxidase)}} \textbf{C}_2\textbf{H}_4 \;(\text{ethylene}) + \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCN} $$

**Step 4 — Salvage / Recycling (the "Yang cycle" part):** MTA is recycled back to methionine, so the methionine pool is **conserved** even during high ethylene output.

$$ \text{MTA} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Methylthioribose (MTR)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Methionine (recycled)} $$

**Net effect:** A continuous supply of methionine drives continuous ethylene production without depleting the methionine pool.

---

## 3.5 Action of 1-Methylcyclopropene (1-MCP) on the Ethylene Receptor

**1-MCP (SmartFresh™)** is a **gaseous cyclic olefin** that binds **irreversibly and competitively** to the **ethylene receptor (ETR1 / ETR2)** in plant cells, blocking ethylene perception.

$$ \text{1-MCP}_{(g)} + \text{ETR (receptor)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{1-MCP–ETR complex (permanent, inactive)} $$

Result: even though the plant **continues producing ethylene**, the **signal is not transduced** → ripening is paused.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU pearl:** 1-MCP is used at **extremely low concentrations (~ 0.5–1 µL/L)** and only once, because the receptor binding is essentially **permanent** for the life of the cell.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Storage Temperature on Physiological Rates

| Temperature | Respiration Rate | Transpiration Rate | Ethylene Synthesis | Shelf-life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0 °C (cold)** | Very low | Low (with high RH) | Very low | **Maximum** |
| **10 °C (cool)** | Low | Low–Moderate | Low | Long |
| **20 °C (room)** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Short |
| **30 °C (warm)** | High | High | High | Very short |
| **40 °C (hot)** | Very high | Very high | Peak (chilling injury threshold for some) | Minimal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Chilling Injury Trap (often tested):** Some tropical produce (banana, mango, papaya) suffer **chilling injury** below 12–13 °C. Their storage temperature must therefore be a **compromise** between low enough to slow respiration and high enough to avoid chilling injury — this is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overview of Basic Post-Harvest Physiology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Produce: Fruit, Vegetable, Flower] --> B[Living Tissue: Active Metabolism]
    B --> C[Respiration: Glucose + O2 to CO2 + H2O + ATP + Heat]
    B --> D[Transpiration: Water Vapour Loss through Cuticle / Stomata]
    B --> E[Ethylene Biosynthesis: Yang Cycle from Methionine]
    C --> F[Substrate Depletion: Sugar / Starch Exhaustion]
    C --> G[Respiratory Heat Load on Storage]
    D --> H[Weight Loss and Shrivelling]
    D --> I[Turgor Loss: Visual Quality Decline]
    E --> J[Autocatalytic Ethylene Burst in Climacteric Fruits]
    E --> K[Triggered Ripening: Softening, Colour, Aroma]
    J --> K
    F --> L[Senescence: Final Programmed Phase]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Death: End of Storage Life]
    G --> N[Refrigeration: 0 to 4 degrees Celsius]
    N --> O[Slowed Respiration, Reduced Heat]
    N --> P[Reduced Transpiration under High RH 95 to 98 percent]
    N --> Q[Lowered Ethylene Evolution]
    O --> L
    P --> L
    Q --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Yang Cycle (Ethylene Biosynthesis Pathway)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Methionine: Stored Amino Acid] --> B[SAM Synthase]
    B --> C[SAM: S Adenosyl Methionine]
    C --> D[ACC Synthase: Rate Limiting Enzyme]
    D --> E[ACC: 1 Aminocyclopropane 1 Carboxylic Acid]
    C --> F[MTA: 5 Prime Methylthioadenosine]
    F --> G[Methylthioribose: MTR]
    G --> A
    E --> H[ACC Oxidase: ACO]
    H --> I[Ethylene: C2H4]
    H --> J[CO2: Carbon Dioxide]
    H --> K[HCN: Hydrogen Cyanide Detoxified by Beta Cyanoalanine Synthase]
    I --> L[ETR: Ethylene Receptor on Cell Membrane]
    L --> M[Signal Transduction Cascade]
    M --> N[Ripening Enzymes: PG, PME, Amylase, Chlorophyllase]
    N --> O[Phenotypic Ripening: Colour, Softening, Aroma, Sugar]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Respiratory Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Freshly Harvested Produce] --> B{Classify by Ripening Behaviour}
    B -->|Autocatalytic Ethylene| C[Climacteric: Banana, Apple, Tomato, Mango, Avocado]
    B -->|Basal Ethylene Only| D[Non Climacteric: Grape, Citrus, Pineapple, Strawberry, Pomegranate]
    C --> E[Stage 1: Pre Climacteric Minimum]
    E --> F[Stage 2: Climacteric Peak: CO2 Increases 2 to 5 Fold]
    F --> G[Stage 3: Post Climacteric Decline]
    G --> H[Senescence and Death]
    D --> I[Stage 1: Smooth Respiration Decline]
    I --> J[Stage 2: No Peak, Gradual Ripening]
    J --> K[Senescence and Death]
    F --> L[Apply 1 MCP: Block Ethylene Receptor]
    L --> M[Pause Ripening: Extend Shelf Life]
    H --> N[Apply Low Temperature Storage 0 to 4 degrees Celsius]
    K --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interlinking of the Three Physiological Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Internal Stored Reserves: Sugar, Starch, Organic Acid] --> B[Respiration Releases Energy ATP and Heat]
    B --> C[Respiratory Heat Q must be removed by refrigeration]
    B --> D[CO2 Evolution into Storage Atmosphere]
    E[Internal Water in Vacuole] --> F[Transpiration to Outer Air via Cuticle]
    F --> G[Weight Loss and Shrivelling]
    H[Methionine Pool] --> I[Yang Cycle]
    I --> J[Ethylene C2H4 in Storage Air]
    J --> K[Autocatalytic Ripening in Climacteric]
    B --> L[Substrate Depletion]
    F --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Senescence: Membrane Breakdown, Pigment Change]
    M --> N[Quality Loss and Shelf Life End]
    C --> O[Cold Storage Design: Heat Load Calculation]
    D --> P[CA Storage Design: O2 and CO2 Set Points]
    G --> Q[Humidifier 95 to 98 percent RH Design]
    J --> R[Ethylene Scrubber: KMnO4]
    R --> P
    P --> N
    Q --> N
    O --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** All three physiological processes (respiration, transpiration, ethylene biosynthesis) **converge on senescence** as the common end-point. **Any post-harvest technology** (cold, CA, MAP, 1-MCP, KMnO₄ scrubber, humidification) works by **intervening at one or more of these four coloured arms** of the diagram.
