> 3. **Stating Q₁₀ without units clarification** — Q₁₀ is dimensionless, but the *rate* has units.
> 4. **Missing the role of 1-MCP irreversibility** — reversibility would make it useless for long storage.
> 5. **Ignoring chilling-injury** when recommending low temperatures for tropical produce — examiners specifically look for this nuance.

---

### 5.X Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Respiration rate** = mL CO₂ (or O₂) per kg per hour; **higher rate = shorter shelf-life**.
- ✅ **RQ** ≈ 1 → carbohydrates; > 1 → organic acids; < 1 → lipids.
- ✅ **Q₁₀** ≈ 2–3 for fresh produce; every 10 °C rise ≈ doubles respiration.
- ✅ **Respiration control** targets: **T, pO₂, pCO₂, ethylene, a_w, and membrane integrity**.
- ✅ **Physical methods:** Cold storage, pre-cooling, hypobaric storage, irradiation, heat treatment.
- ✅ **Chemical methods:** 1-MCP, AVG, CO₂ shock, KMnO₄ ethylene scavengers.
- ✅ **Biochemical methods:** Edible coatings (chitosan, wax), CaCl₂ dips, growth regulators.
- ✅ **Packaging methods:** MAP (passive), CAS (active), active & intelligent packaging.
- ✅ **1-MCP** binds ethylene receptors **irreversibly** — 10× affinity, blocks climacteric peak.
- ✅ **Hypobaric storage** operates at ~50–100 mm Hg pressure; reduces ethylene partial pressure and O₂ simultaneously.
- ✅ **Climacteric** = banana, apple, mango, tomato, avocado | **Non-climacteric** = citrus, grape, pineapple.
- ✅ **Heat of respiration** must be added to refrigeration load: $Q_{resp} = m \times R_{CO_2} \times 10.7 \text{ kJ}$.
- ✅ **Conversion rule:** $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$ — never skip this in derivations.

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Respiration Control Methods**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme Aligned | Bloom's Taxonomy: L1–L5*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changes During Post-Harvest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Changes During Post-Harvest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Post-harvest changes* refer to the **biochemical, physiological, physical, chemical, and microbiological alterations** that occur in a harvested food commodity (fruits, vegetables, cereals, milk, meat, fish, etc.) **after separation from its parent plant or animal**, until it reaches the consumer. These changes determine the **shelf-life, quality retention, nutritional value, and marketability** of the food.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, "Changes During Post-Harvest" (Module 1) is the foundational pillar upon which all spoilage, preservation, and storage concepts depend. The changes are classified into **five interrelated domains**:

| S.No | Domain | Brief Nature |
|------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | **Physical** | Moisture loss, wilting, shriveling, texture softening |
| 2 | **Physiological** | Respiration, transpiration, ripening, senescence |
| 3 | **Biochemical** | Starch → sugar conversion, pectin breakdown, chlorophyll degradation |
| 4 | **Chemical** | Lipid oxidation, Maillard browning, enzymatic browning |
| 5 | **Microbiological** | Surface microbial proliferation, decay |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "A Detached Battery"**
> Imagine plucking an apple from a tree is like **removing a fully charged battery from its charger**. The apple still has stored energy (sugars, starches, organic acids), but it is **no longer receiving input from the mother plant**. To keep its cells alive, it must start **consuming its own internal reserves** through *respiration*. Just as a discharged battery eventually dies, the apple loses quality, texture, and nutrients over time.

**A simpler take-away:** A harvested fruit/vegetable is a **living, respiring entity** slowly running out of fuel, losing water, and being attacked by microbes — every second counts from harvest to fork.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

- **Respiration (R):** Oxidative breakdown of carbohydrates to CO₂ + H₂O + Energy (ATP + Heat).
- **Transpiration (T):** Loss of water vapor from the commodity surface.
- **Senescence:** The terminal phase of physiological maturity leading to death of tissue.
- **Climacteric:** A sharp, transient rise in respiration rate during ripening (e.g., banana, apple).
- **Non-Climacteric:** Commodities showing no respiration peak (e.g., citrus, grapes, strawberry).
- **Q₁₀ Value:** The factor by which reaction rate increases for every 10 °C rise in temperature. For post-harvest produce, **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3**.
- **RGR / RG:** Respiration rate commonly expressed in **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**.

## 1.4 Classification of Commodities by Respiration Intensity

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Commodities are graded by respiration rate because **higher respiration = shorter shelf life**.

| Class | Respiration Rate (mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at 5 °C) | Examples | Approx. Shelf Life |
|-------|---------------------------------------------|----------|--------------------|
| **Very Low** | < 5 | Nuts, Dates, Honey | Months–Years |
| **Low** | 5–10 | Apple, Grape, Kiwi | Weeks–Months |
| **Moderate** | 10–20 | Banana, Pear, Peach | Days–Weeks |
| **High** | 20–30 | Strawberry, Cauliflower | 1–3 Days |
| **Very High** | > 30 | Broccoli, Asparagus, Mushroom | < 1 Day |

## 1.5 The Big Picture (Cause-Effect Chain)

```text
Harvest Detachment → Loss of Nutrient Supply
        ↓
Activation of Catabolic Pathways (↑ Respiration)
        ↓
Mobilization of Reserves (Starch → Sugars)
        ↓
Loss of Cellular Integrity (Pectin degradation, Membrane damage)
        ↓
Water Loss + Softening + Pigment Changes
        ↓
Susceptibility to Microbial Spoilage
        ↓
SENESCENCE → DEATH → SPOILAGE
```

This **cause-effect cascade** is the single most important conceptual framework for KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Each Change Category

### 🔬 A. Physical Changes

1. **Water Loss (Transpiration)**
   - Driving force: Water vapor pressure deficit (VPD) between commodity interior and ambient air.
   - Mechanism: Cuticular transpiration (90%) > stomatal transpiration (< 10% after harvest).
   - Visible result: **Shriveling, weight loss, loss of turgidity**.

2. **Texture Changes**
   - Cell wall pectin undergoes enzymatic hydrolysis → middle lamella dissolves.
   - Result: Loss of firmness, mealiness (e.g., over-ripe pear).

3. **Color Changes**
   - Chlorophyll → Pheophytin (green → olive/brown).
   - Carotenoids unmasked when chlorophyll degrades.
   - Anthocyanin biosynthesis in apples, grapes.

4. **Heat Generation**
   - Respiration produces heat; bulk-stored commodities may "heat up" in storage.

### 🔬 B. Physiological Changes

1. **Respiration**
   - **Aerobic (primary):** C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O + Energy (ΔG = -2870 kJ/mol).
   - **Anaerobic (when O₂ < 1–3%):** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ + Energy.
   - **Fermentative metabolites** → off-flavor, tissue death.

2. **Transpiration**
   - Passive process; governed by Fick's law analog: rate ∝ Surface area × VPD / Cuticular resistance.

3. **Ripening & Senescence**
   - Coordinated developmental program involving ethylene, ABA, auxin.

4. **Ethylene Production**
   - Plant hormone; "fruit-ripening hormone"; autocatalytic in climacteric fruits.

### 🔬 C. Biochemical Changes

- **Starch ↔ Sugar Interconversion:** Starch phosphorylase + amylases convert starch → sucrose/glucose/fructose.
- **Pectin Metabolism:** Polygalacturonase (PG), Pectin methyl esterase (PME) → cell wall softening.
- **Organic Acid Turnover:** Malic, citric, tartaric acid levels drop (contribute to sweetness perception).
- **Protein & Amino Acid Changes:** Proteolysis; may generate off-flavors.
- **Lipid Metabolism:** In avocado, oil synthesis; in cereals, lipid hydrolysis → rancidity.

### 🔬 D. Chemical Changes

1. **Enzymatic Browning:** PPO (Polyphenol oxidase) + O₂ + Phenol → Quinones → Melanins (brown pigments).
2. **Non-Enzymatic Browning (Maillard Reaction):** Reducing sugar + Amino acid → Schiff base → Amadori product → Melanoidins (golden/brown).
3. **Lipid Oxidation:** Unsaturated FA + O₂ → Hydroperoxides → Aldehydes/Ketones (rancidity).
4. **Vitamin Degradation:** Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid.

### 🔬 E. Microbiological Changes

- Surface microflora (bacteria, yeasts, molds) utilize leaked nutrients.
- Wounds at harvest accelerate microbial entry.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Print This for Exams**

| S.No | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|------|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Respiration Rate (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\Delta V_{CO_2}}{\Delta t \cdot m}$ | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 2 | **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** | $RQ = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{O_2 \text{ consumed}}$ | Dimensionless; Carbohydrate RQ ≈ 1, Lipid RQ ≈ 0.7, Organic acid RQ ≈ 1.33 |
| 3 | **Heat of Respiration (HR)** | $HR = R \cdot \Delta H_{resp}$ | kJ·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 4 | **Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Dimensionless; ≈ 2–3 for produce |
| 5 | **Arrhenius Equation (Shelf Life)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | k = reaction rate, Eₐ = activation energy (kJ/mol), R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| 6 | **Clausius-Clapeyron (Water Vapor)** | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | For vapor pressure curves |
| 7 | **Fick's Law (Transpiration)** | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_{int} - P_{ext})}{L}$ | A=area, L=cuticle thickness |
| 8 | **Water Loss (Mass Basis)** | $\%\text{ Loss} = \dfrac{W_0 - W_t}{W_0} \times 100$ | W₀ = initial, Wₜ = current weight |
| 9 | **Starch to Sugar Conversion** | $\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\alpha/\beta\text{-amylase}} \text{Maltose} \xrightarrow{\text{maltase}} \text{Glucose}$ | Quality index in banana ripening |
| 10 | **Ethylene Action Threshold** | $C_{eth} < 0.1 \text{ ppm}$ | Sensitive commodities damaged above this |

> **Exam Hack:** For Q₁₀ problems, always confirm the temperature interval is in °C (not K) — the formula works because we take the **difference** which is identical in both scales.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles directly enable:

- **Cold Chain Design:** Calculating refrigeration load = (Sensible heat + Latent heat of respiration × HR).
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Tuning O₂/CO₂ based on RQ to suppress respiration.
- **Shelf-Life Prediction:** Using Arrhenius plots to extrapolate shelf life at storage temperatures.
- **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage:** Apple storage at 1–2% O₂ + 1–2% CO₂ prolongs life by **6–9 months**.

---

## 2.4 Critical Distinctions (Frequently Tested in KTU)

| Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Apple, Banana, Pear, Mango, Tomato, Avocado | Grape, Citrus, Strawberry, Cherry, Pineapple |
| Show respiration peak + autocatalytic ethylene | Low, steady respiration; no ethylene burst |
| Can ripen after harvest | Must ripen on plant |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often wrongly cite **tomato as non-climacteric**. Tomato is a **classic climacteric fruit** and is *the most frequently used example* in KTU questions on ethylene biology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Respiratory Quotient (RQ) from Balanced Equation

**Start:** Glucose oxidation (general carbohydrate):

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O$$

**Step 1:** Identify moles produced vs. consumed.

Moles of CO₂ produced = 6.
Moles of O₂ consumed = 6.

**Step 2:** Apply RQ definition.

$$RQ = \dfrac{\text{Moles of }CO_2 \text{ released}}{\text{Moles of }O_2 \text{ consumed}}$$

$$RQ = \dfrac{6}{6} = 1.0$$

**Interpretation:** RQ = 1 ⇒ substrate is a **carbohydrate** (default assumption for most fruits).

**For Lipid (e.g., Palmitic acid, C₁₆H₃₂O₂):**

$$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + 23\,O_2 \rightarrow 16\,CO_2 + 16\,H_2O$$

$$RQ_{lipid} = \dfrac{16}{23} \approx 0.70$$

**For Organic Acid (e.g., Malic acid, C₄H₆O₅):**

$$C_4H_6O_5 + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$$

$$RQ_{acid} = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.33$$

**Conclusion:** By measuring RQ experimentally, one can deduce the **predominant substrate being respired** at any stage of post-harvest storage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Q₁₀ Equation and Its Application

**Starting Principle:** Reaction rates increase with temperature. For biological systems:

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = Q_{10}^{\,\Delta T / 10}$$

where $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ (in °C or K — difference is identical).

**Step-by-Step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Take logarithms base 10.

$$\log\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta T}{10} \cdot \log(Q_{10})$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the unknown variable.

$$Q_{10} = 10^{\,\dfrac{10}{\Delta T} \cdot \log\left(R_2/R_1\right)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply to a worked KTU-style problem.

**Problem:** A fruit has a respiration rate of 12 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at 5 °C. At 15 °C, the rate rises to 30 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹. Calculate Q₁₀.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = 15 - 5 = 10 \,°C$$

$$Q_{10} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{30}{12} = 2.5$$

Therefore **Q₁₀ = 2.5** (typical for many horticultural commodities).

**Step 4 (Reverse Problem):** Shelf life at 5 °C is 30 days. Estimate shelf life at 15 °C, assuming Q₁₀ = 2.5.

**Logic:** Higher temperature → Higher respiration rate → **Shorter** shelf life. So shelf life is **inversely** proportional to rate.

$$SL_2 = \dfrac{SL_1}{Q_{10}} = \dfrac{30}{2.5} = 12 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** A mere 10 °C temperature rise reduces shelf life by 60%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Shelf-Life Prediction

**Starting Equation:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where:
- $k$ = rate of deterioration (e.g., 1/day)
- $A$ = frequency factor (constant)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 1:** Take natural log of both sides.

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 2:** For two temperatures T₁ and T₂:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R T_2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract.

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Since shelf life $SL \propto 1/k$:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{SL_1}{SL_2}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Numerical Example:**

Tomatoes stored at 10 °C have shelf life = 20 days; at 25 °C it drops to 5 days. Estimate activation energy.

$$T_1 = 283.15 \,K, \quad T_2 = 298.15 \,K, \quad SL_1 = 20, \quad SL_2 = 5$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{20}{5}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.386$$

$$\left(\dfrac{1}{283.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right) = \dfrac{298.15 - 283.15}{283.15 \times 298.15} = \dfrac{15}{84{,}409} = 1.777 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{1.386}{1.777 \times 10^{-4}} = 7{,}800$$

$$E_a = 7800 \times 8.314 = 64{,}850 \,\text{J/mol} \approx 64.85 \,\text{kJ/mol}$$

This value is in the **typical range (40–100 kJ/mol)** for enzymatic deterioration of fresh produce. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Storage Conditions

| Storage Variable | Respiration | Transpiration | Ethylene | Color | Shelf Life |
|------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|-------|------------|
| ↓ Temperature | ↓↓↓ | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | Better | ↑↑↑ |
| ↑ Humidity | = | ↓↓ | = | = | ↑↑ (watch for decay) |
| ↓ O₂ (< 5%) | ↓↓ | = | ↓↓ | = | ↑↑ (anaerobic risk) |
| ↑ CO₂ (5–10%) | ↓ | = | ↓ | = | ↑ (but injury > 15%) |
| ↑ Ethylene | ↑ (climacteric) | = | — | Faster ripening | ↓↓ |
| Light (post-harvest) | Mild ↑ | = | = | Degrades chlorophyll | ↓ |
| Wounding | ↑↑ | = | ↑↑↑ | Browning | ↓↓↓ |

**Engineering Takeaway:** The **golden storage triangle** is **(Low T, High RH, Modified Atmosphere)**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Heat Load from Respiration

**Problem:** 1000 kg of apples are stored at 5 °C. Respiration rate = 8 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹. Heat of respiration = 21 kJ per mole of CO₂. Calculate **respiratory heat load**.

**Step 1:** Total CO₂ produced per hour.

$$8 \, \text{mL/kg/h} \times 1000 \, \text{kg} = 8000 \, \text{mL/h} = 8.0 \, \text{L/h}$$

**Step 2:** Convert L of CO₂ to moles (1 mol ≈ 22.4 L at STP).

$$n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{8.0}{22.4} = 0.357 \,\text{mol/h}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by heat of respiration.

$$Q_{resp} = 0.357 \times 21 = 7.5 \,\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to refrigeration load (1 kJ/h ≈ 0.000278 kW; or ≈ 0.238 kcal/h).

$$Q_{resp} \approx 0.00208 \,\text{kW}$$

> For bulk storage, this is multiplied by thousands of kg — making **respiratory heat a non-negligible design parameter** for cold-room engineers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Master Flow — Post-Harvest Changes Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Commodity] --> B[Detachment from Parent Plant]
    B --> C[Loss of Nutrient and Water Supply]
    C --> D1[Physical Changes]
    C --> D2[Physiological Changes]
    C --> D3[Biochemical Changes]
    C --> D4[Chemical Changes]
    C --> D5[Microbiological Changes]

    D1 --> E1[Water Loss and Shriveling]
    D1 --> E2[Texture Softening]
    D1 --> E3[Pigment Changes]

    D2 --> F1[Respiration Aerobic and Anaerobic]
    D2 --> F2[Transpiration]
    D2 --> F3[Ripening and Senescence]
    D2 --> F4[Ethylene Biosynthesis]

    D3 --> G1[Starch to Sugar Conversion]
    D3 --> G2[Pectin Hydrolysis]
    D3 --> G3[Organic Acid Turnover]
    D3 --> G4[Protein and Lipid Metabolism]

    D4 --> H1[Enzymatic Browning PPO]
    D4 --> H2[Maillard Reaction]
    D4 --> H3[Lipid Oxidation]
    D4 --> H4[Vitamin Degradation]

    D5 --> I1[Surface Microbial Growth]
    D5 --> I2[Decay and Spoilage]

    E1 --> J[Quality Decline]
    E2 --> J
    E3 --> J
    F1 --> J
    F2 --> J
    F3 --> J
    F4 --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    G4 --> J
    H1 --> J
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    H4 --> J
    I1 --> J
    I2 --> J

    J --> K[End of Shelf Life]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Respiration Pathway — Aerobic vs Anaerobic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Glucose] --> B{Available Oxygen}
    B -->|O2 above 3 percent| C[Aerobic Pathway]
    B -->|O2 below 1 to 3 percent| D[Anaerobic Pathway]

    C --> E[Glycolysis produces 2 Pyruvate]
    E --> F[TCA Cycle]
    F --> G[Electron Transport Chain]
    G --> H[Produces CO2 plus H2O plus 38 ATP]

    D --> I[Pyruvate Decarboxylase]
    I --> J[Produces Ethanol plus CO2 plus 2 ATP]

    H --> K[Healthy Energy Production]
    J --> L[Fermentative Spoilage Off flavor and Tissue Death]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric Respiration Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CL["Climacteric Fruits"]
        C1[Stage 1 Pre Climacteric] --> C2[Stage 2 Climacteric Peak Sharp Rise in CO2]
        C2 --> C3[Stage 3 Post Climacteric Senescence]
    end

    subgraph NC["Non Climacteric Fruits"]
        N1[Stage 1 Steady Low] --> N2[Stage 2 Gradual Decline]
        N2 --> N3[Stage 3 Slow Senescence]
    end

    C2 -.Autocatalytic Ethylene.-> C2
    N1 -.No Ethylene Burst.-> N2
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block Architecture of a Cold Storage Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Produce] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Pre Cooling Hydro or Forced Air]
    C --> D[Cold Storage Chamber]
    D --> E1[Temperature Control 0 to 10 C]
    D --> E2[Humidity Control 85 to 95 percent RH]
    D --> E3[Atmosphere Control Low O2 High CO2]
    D --> E4[Ethylene Scrubber KMnO4]

    E1 --> F[Reduced Respiration]
    E2 --> G[Reduced Transpiration]
    E3 --> H[Suppressed Microbial Growth]
    E4 --> I[Delayed Ripening]

    F --> J[Extended Shelf Life]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Enzymatic Browning Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phenol Substrate Tyrosine] --> B[PPO Polyphenol Oxidase]
    B --> C[Oxygen Required]
    C --> D[Quinone]
    D --> E[Polymerization]
    E --> F[Melanin Brown Pigment]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🟦 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between climacteric and non-climacteric fruits with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key pattern):**

**Climacteric Fruits:** Show a **sharp rise in respiration rate** (CO₂ production) and a **burst of autocatalytic ethylene** during ripening. They can ripen even after harvest.

- *Examples:* Banana, Apple, Mango, Tomato, Avocado.

**Non-Climacteric Fruits:** Display **no respiration peak**; respiration gradually declines. Ethylene production is **low and non-autocatalytic**. Ripening essentially stops after harvest.

- *Examples:* Grape, Citrus (orange, lemon), Strawberry, Cherry, Pineapple.

| Parameter | Climacteric | Non-Climacteric |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Respiration peak | Yes, sharp | No |
| Ethylene burst | Yes, autocatalytic | No |
| Ripening after harvest | Possible | Not possible |
| Storage strategy | Use of 1-MCP, CA | Mostly low temperature |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define Respiratory Quotient (RQ). What is its significance in post-harvest physiology?

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key pattern):**

**Definition:** Respiratory Quotient (RQ) is the **molar ratio of CO₂ released to O₂ consumed** during respiration:

$$RQ = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ produced (moles)}}{O_2 \text{ consumed (moles)}}$$

**Significance (3 key points to score full marks):**

1. **Substrate identification:** RQ ≈ 1 → carbohydrate; RQ ≈ 0.7 → lipid; RQ > 1 → organic acid (substrate switching during ripening).
2. **Storage diagnosis:** Sudden RQ rise above 1.3 indicates anaerobic respiration and tissue damage.
3. **Engineering design parameter:** RQ governs CO₂ evolution inside sealed packages, dictating MAP design and O₂/CO₂ ratios.

---

## 🟥 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** of equal marks.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**"Explain in detail the various physiological and biochemical changes that occur in fruits and vegetables after harvest. Discuss respiration, transpiration, ripening, and ethylene biosynthesis."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Physiological Changes after Harvest:**

**(i) Respiration (2.5 marks):**
- Definition: Oxidative catabolism of organic reserves.
- Aerobic: Glucose + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + 2870 kJ/mol.
- Anaerobic: Glucose → 2 Ethanol + 2 CO₂ + 118 kJ/mol (off-flavor, tissue damage).
- Measured as mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹.
- Climacteric rise explained with ethylene autocatalysis.

**(ii) Transpiration (2 marks):**
- Loss of water vapor through cuticle and lenticels.
- Causes: VPD gradient, cuticular resistance, surface area ratio.
- Effects: Shriveling, weight loss, textural loss.

**(iii) Ripening & Senescence (2.5 marks):**
- Coordinated biochemical events; chlorophyll degradation, carotenoid synthesis, flavor volatiles.
- Senescence = terminal phase, programmed cell death.
- Regulatory hormones: Ethylene, ABA.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Biochemical Changes & Ethylene Biosynthesis:**

**(i) Carbohydrate Metabolism (2 marks):**
- Starch → sugars (in banana, mango).
- Increases sweetness, Brix value, taste acceptability.

**(ii) Pectin Metabolism (1.5 marks):**
- PG (polygalacturonase) + PME (pectin methyl esterase) → pectin hydrolysis → softening.
- Tomatoes and papaya are classic examples.

**(iii) Organic Acid & Pigment Changes (1.5 marks):**
- Malic acid declines → sweet/acid ratio increases.
- Chlorophyll → Pheophytin; carotenoids unmask.

**(iv) Ethylene Biosynthesis — Yang Cycle (2 marks):**

$$\text{Methionine} \rightarrow \text{SAM} \rightarrow \text{ACC} \rightarrow \text{Ethylene}$$

(S-adenosyl methionine → 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid → Ethylene)
- Catalyzed by ACC synthase and ACC oxidase.
- Autocatalytic in climacteric fruits; triggers ripening cascade.

**Diagrammatic representation (KTU expects):** Yang cycle flowchart.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**"Discuss the physical, chemical, and microbiological changes occurring in harvested produce. How do these changes affect shelf life and what factors influence them?"**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Physical Changes (3 marks):**
1. Water loss — shriveling, weight loss.
2. Texture changes — pectin degradation → softening.
3. Color changes — chlorophyll degradation, anthocyanin synthesis.
4. Heat from respiration — bulk heating in storage.

**Chemical Changes (2.5 marks):**
1. Enzymatic browning — PPO + phenol + O₂ → melanin (apple cut surface turns brown).
2. Maillard reaction — reducing sugar + amino acid → browning (on heating/drying).
3. Lipid oxidation — rancidity (oily foods, nuts).
4. Vitamin C loss — oxidative degradation.

**Microbiological Changes (1.5 marks):**
- Surface flora (bacteria, yeasts, molds) proliferate using leaked nutrients.
- Wound sites accelerate invasion (Erwinia soft rot, Botrytis gray mold).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Factors Influencing Shelf Life (5 marks):**

| Factor | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| Temperature (Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3) | Most critical; lower T = longer life |
| Relative Humidity | High RH reduces wilting but > 95% invites decay |
| Atmosphere (O₂, CO₂) | Low O₂ + High CO₂ extends shelf life |
| Ethylene exposure | Accelerates ripening in climacteric fruits |
| Mechanical injury | Wounds increase respiration 200–400% |
| Microbial load | Pre-harvest hygiene matters |
| Cultivar, maturity stage | Genotype and harvest maturity determine baseline life |

**Shelf Life Calculation using Arrhenius model (2 marks):**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$

Higher T → higher k → faster deterioration → shorter shelf life.

Practical implication: Cold chain logistics, pre-cooling, and CA storage are essential engineering interventions.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before Writing the Exam):**
> 
> 1. ❌ Writing "RQ = CO₂/O₂" without stating **molar basis** → Lose 0.5 mark.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **climacteric** with **ripening** — all climacteric fruits ripen, but not all ripening is climacteric.
> 3. ❌ Writing "Ethylene is harmful to fruits" — it is **beneficial & necessary** for ripening; only **over-exposure** or **premature exposure** is undesirable.
> 4. ❌ Missing **units** in respiration rate calculation (always mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹).
> 5. ❌ In Q₁₀ problems, writing Q₁₀ = R₂/R₁ **without verifying ΔT = 10**.
> 6. ❌ Calling **tomato a vegetable** in post-harvest context — it is botanically a **fruit**, and a **climacteric** one.
> 7. ❌ Skipping diagrams — KTU examiners **appreciate labeled diagrams** for full credit.
> 8. ❌ Spelling errors: "Pheophytin", "Polygalacturonase", "ACC synthase" — spell correctly.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Use this 60-second recap before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Post-harvest change = anything that happens to food **after detachment** from parent source.
- ✅ Five domains: **Physical, Physiological, Biochemical, Chemical, Microbiological**.
- ✅ Respiration = **primary driver** of all post-harvest deterioration.
- ✅ **RQ** identifies respiratory substrate: 1.0 (CHO), 0.7 (lipid), 1.33 (acid).
- ✅ **Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3** for most produce; every 10 °C doubles/triples respiration rate.
- ✅ **Arrhenius model** is the gold-standard shelf life predictor.
- ✅ **Climacteric** = Respiration peak + Autocatalytic ethylene (Apple, Banana, Tomato, Mango, Avocado).
- ✅ **Non-climacteric** = No peak; ripening only on plant (Grape, Citrus, Strawberry, Cherry).
- ✅ **Ethylene biosynthesis:** Methionine → SAM → ACC → Ethylene (Yang Cycle; ACC synthase is rate-limiting).
- ✅ **Browning:** Enzymatic (PPO, cold/fresh produce) vs. Non-enzymatic (Maillard, during heating).
- ✅ **Pectin degradation:** PG + PME → Softening.
- ✅ **Starch → Sugar:** Conversion tracked as ripening index (Brix).
- ✅ **Transpiration** controlled by cuticle, surface area, VPD, and storage RH.
- ✅ Storage **Triangle:** Low T + High RH + Modified Atmosphere = Maximum shelf life.
- ✅ Heat of respiration **must** be accounted for in bulk cold storage design.

---

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Changes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Changes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Harvest Physical and Chemical Changes** refer to the time-dependent, irreversible (and partially reversible) alterations in the structural, mechanical, and molecular composition of a harvested food commodity that occur *after separation from the parent plant or animal source*. These changes govern the **shelf life**, **sensory quality** (color, texture, flavor), and **nutritive value** of fresh produce, and form the foundational basis of the entire post-harvest technology discipline.

In the context of KTU 2024 PEFTT754, these changes are classified into two master categories:

**A. Physical Changes** — Alterations that do *not* involve a change in the fundamental molecular identity of the constituents. They include **transpiration (moisture loss)**, **wilting**, **shriveling**, **textural softening**, **mechanical injury deformation**, and **color fading** due to pigment redistribution.

**B. Chemical Changes** — Alterations that *do* involve modification of molecular structure, including **respiration (aerobic/anaerobic)**, **ethylene biosynthesis**, **enzymatic browning** (PPO/POD mediated), **lipid oxidation**, **pigment degradation** (chlorophyll, carotenoids, anthocyanins), **starch-to-sugar conversion**, and **vitamin depletion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (KTU Board favorites):**
> - Respiration Rate Unit: **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**
> - Transpiration Rate Unit: **% weight loss·day⁻¹**
> - Q₁₀ (Temperature Coefficient): **dimensionless, usually 2–3 for fresh produce**
> - Storage Temperature Reference: **0 °C (chilling), 10 °C (cool), 25 °C (ambient)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you pluck a ripe tomato from your kitchen garden and leave it on the dining table. Over the next 5 days, you observe:

- It **shrinks** and the skin becomes **wrinkly** → that is **transpiration (physical change)** — water is escaping as vapor, but the sugar, lycopene, and cell wall molecules are still chemically the same.
- It becomes **softer** when you press it → the **pectin** in the middle lamella is being broken down by enzymes like **polygalacturonase** → a **chemical change** in cell wall architecture.
- It **changes color** from green to red → **chlorophyll** is being degraded and **lycopene** (a carotenoid) is being synthesized → a **biochemical/chemical change**.
- It releases a faint **ethylene smell** and nearby fruits ripen faster → **ethylene biosynthesis** → a hormonal **chemical change**.
- Eventually, **mold** appears → **microbial spoilage**, but the *primary* physical and chemical changes preceded it.

**The big picture:** A harvested fruit or vegetable is *not dead* — it is a **living, respiring, transpiring, enzymatically active system** that is *slowly consuming itself* until senescence. Understanding this self-consumption is the heart of post-harvest physiology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Changes — Structured Analytical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Transpiration (Moisture Loss)
- **Why it happens:** After harvest, the commodity has *no replenishment* of water from the soil, but transpiration (cuticular + stomatal water vapor loss) continues.
- **How:** Water moves from the turgid cells (high water potential, Ψ ≈ −0.2 MPa) to the dry surrounding air (low water potential, Ψ ≈ −100 MPa), following the gradient.
- **Consequence:** **Wilting**, shriveling, loss of saleable weight (typically **3–10% loss** makes produce unmarketable).
- **Controlling factors:** Relative humidity (RH), temperature, surface-to-volume ratio, cuticle wax thickness.

#### 2.1.2 Textural Changes (Softening)
- **Why:** Enzymatic hydrolysis of **pectin**, **cellulose**, and **hemicellulose** in the middle lamella and primary cell wall.
- **Key enzymes:** **Polygalacturonase (PG)**, **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)**, **Cellulase (Cx)**, **β-galactosidase**.
- **Outcome:** Loss of firmness, increased susceptibility to mechanical injury and microbial invasion.

#### 2.1.3 Mechanical Damage
- Types: **Bruising**, **Abrasion**, **Puncture**, **Compression**.
- Physical signs: surface lesions, tissue rupture, accelerated moisture loss.
- Triggers **secondary chemical changes** (wound-induced ethylene, browning).

#### 2.1.4 Color (Physical Aspect)
- Loss of surface gloss due to wax removal; structural pigment relocation.

---

### 2.2 Chemical Changes — Structured Analytical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Respiration (The Master Reaction)
- **Definition:** Oxidative breakdown of organic reserves (sugars, organic acids, lipids) to **CO₂ + H₂O + Energy (ATP + heat)**.
- **Aerobic Respiration (dominant):**
  
  C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Energy (≈ 2870 kJ/mol or 686 kcal/mol)
  
- **Anaerobic Respiration / Fermentation (when O₂ is limiting):**
  
  C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ + Energy (≈ 118 kJ/mol)
  
- **Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric:**

| Feature | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
|---|---|---|
| Respiration pattern | Sharp peak (climacteric rise) at ripening | Gradual decline after harvest |
| Ethylene production | **Autocatalytic** (burst) | Low, basal only |
| Examples | Banana, Mango, Tomato, Apple, Avocado | Citrus, Grape, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cherry |

#### 2.2.2 Ethylene Biosynthesis (Yang Cycle)
- **Methionine** → **S-adenosyl methionine (SAM)** → **1-Aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid (ACC)** → **Ethylene (C₂H₄)**.
- Catalyzing enzymes: **SAM synthetase**, **ACC synthase (ACS)**, **ACC oxidase (ACO)**.
- Ethylene is a **plant hormone** that triggers ripening, abscission, and senescence even at concentrations as low as **0.1 ppm**.

#### 2.2.3 Enzymatic Browning
- **Reaction:** Phenols + O₂ → Quinones → Melanins (brown pigments).
- **Enzymes:** **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, **Peroxidase (POD)**, **Catalase**.
- **Substrates:** Tyrosine, chlorogenic acid, catechins.

#### 2.2.4 Lipid Oxidation
- **Mechanism:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- **Products:** Hydroperoxides → Aldehydes, Ketones → Rancidity.
- **Critical in:** Oilseeds, nuts, fatty fish, dairy.

#### 2.2.5 Pigment Changes
- **Chlorophyll → Pheophytin** (acidic, Mg²⁺ loss, green → olive brown).
- **Chlorophyll → Pheophorbide** (loss of phytol chain).
- **Carotenoid unmasking** (chlorophyll loss reveals underlying yellow/orange pigments).
- **Anthocyanin** changes with pH and SO₂ exposure.

#### 2.2.6 Carbohydrate Conversions
- **Starch → Sugars** (in banana, apple, mango) — increases sweetness, decreases firmness.
- **Pectin: Protopectin → Soluble pectin** — softening.

#### 2.2.7 Protein and Amino Acid Changes
- Proteolysis releases free amino acids; can lead to off-flavors (e.g., in stored fish).

#### 2.2.8 Vitamin Changes
- **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** is the most labile — oxidized to **dehydroascorbic acid**.
- **Vitamin A** (carotenoids) and **B-complex** are relatively more stable but decline gradually.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-grade formulas** for Module 1. Memorize units and constants.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Respiration Rate (RR) | $RR = \dfrac{(V_{CO_2} \times M_{CO_2})}{(W \times t)}$ | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 2 | Transpiration Rate (TR) | $TR = \dfrac{(W_i - W_f)}{(W_i \times t)} \times 100$ | % weight loss per day |
| 3 | Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\left(\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}\right)}$ | Dimensionless; typical 2–3 |
| 4 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)}$ | Rate constant vs T (Kelvin) |
| 5 | Respiratory Quotient (RQ) | $RQ = \dfrac{\text{Moles CO}_2 \text{ released}}{\text{Moles O}_2 \text{ consumed}}$ | Substrate indicator: Carbs = 1, Lipids ≈ 0.7, Organic acids > 1 |
| 6 | Heat of Respiration (H) | $H = RR \times 10.7 \text{ (approx for carbs)}$ | mW·kg⁻¹ or W·tonne⁻¹ |
| 7 | Water Vapour Pressure Deficit (WVPD) | $WVPD = e_s(T) - e_a$ | kPa; driver of transpiration |
| 8 | Water Activity (a_w) | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Critical threshold for microbial growth |
| 9 | Fick's First Law (gas diffusion) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Steady-state gas flux through film |
| 10 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (pH control) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | For organic acid / browning control |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL PIPE-CHARACTER SAFETY:** Within the table above, the absolute-value notations (such as those in Q₁₀ expressions) have been replaced with the safer `\vert` or contextual phrasing to prevent the markdown table parser from misinterpreting the vertical bar `|` as a column separator. Use the same convention in your answer scripts.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Cold Storage Design:** Knowing Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3 for a commodity, an engineer calculates that dropping storage temperature from 25 °C to 0 °C slows respiration by **8× to 27×**, directly translating to shelf-life extension.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Setting O₂ < 5% and CO₂ > 3% suppresses respiration and ethylene action in apples.
- **Energy Audit of Cold Rooms:** Heat of respiration must be added to compressor load (Q_total = Q_conduction + Q_respiration + Q_infiltration).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Respiration Rate from Gas-Exchange Data

**Given:** A sealed jar of volume $V_J$ (mL) contains a fruit of mass $W$ (kg). The initial CO₂ concentration is $C_0$ (mL/mL), and after time $t$ (hours), the final concentration is $C_f$. Compute the respiration rate $RR$ in mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Define the CO₂ volume evolved.**

The volume of CO₂ produced inside the jar over the time interval $t$ is the product of the jar's free gas volume and the change in CO₂ concentration. Mathematically:

$$V_{CO_2} = (V_J - V_{fruit}) \times (C_f - C_0)$$

where $V_{fruit}$ is the volume displaced by the commodity (often estimated from mass and density as $V_{fruit} = W / \rho$).

**Step 2 — Express on a mass basis.**

We want the volume per unit mass of commodity. Divide $V_{CO_2}$ by the mass $W$:

$$\dfrac{V_{CO_2}}{W} = \dfrac{(V_J - V_{fruit}) \times (C_f - C_0)}{W}$$

**Step 3 — Express on a time basis.**

Divide further by the elapsed time $t$ to obtain the rate per hour:

$$RR = \dfrac{(V_J - V_{fruit}) \times (C_f - C_0)}{W \times t}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the constants for practical use.**

If the fruit density is close to water (ρ ≈ 1 kg/L = 1000 kg/m³) and $V_{fruit} \ll V_J$, we may approximate $V_{fruit} \approx 0$, giving the working equation:

$$RR = \dfrac{V_J \times (C_f - C_0)}{W \times t}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (worked).**

Suppose $V_J = 1000$ mL, $W = 0.2$ kg, $C_0 = 0.0003$ (300 ppm), $C_f = 0.0013$ (1300 ppm), $t = 1$ h.

$$\Delta C = 0.0013 - 0.0003 = 0.0010$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 1000 \times 0.0010 = 1.0 \text{ mL CO}_2$$

$$RR = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ mL}}{0.2 \text{ kg} \times 1 \text{ h}} = 5.0 \text{ mL CO}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** This commodity exhibits a moderate respiration rate of 5 mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ at the test temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Q₁₀ Relationship

**Definition:** Q₁₀ is the factor by which the rate of a biological/chemical reaction (here, respiration) increases for a **10 °C rise in temperature**.

**Step 1 — Assume the rate follows an exponential (Arrhenius-like) law with temperature.**

Let $R$ denote the rate and $T$ the absolute temperature in Kelvin. Over a small temperature range, the rate is well-approximated by:

$$R = R_0 \cdot e^{k T}$$

**Step 2 — Write the ratio of rates at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.**

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_0 \cdot e^{k T_2}}{R_0 \cdot e^{k T_1}} = e^{k (T_2 - T_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$ (the temperature-sensitivity constant).**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = k (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$k = \dfrac{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}{(T_2 - T_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Specialize to a 10 °C temperature difference.**

If $T_2 - T_1 = 10$ K, then by definition $R_2 / R_1 = Q_{10}$, so:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = 10k \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{\ln(Q_{10})}{10}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the generalized temperature relationship.**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\left(\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical check (worked).**

For a commodity, $R_{25°C} = 20$ mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ and $R_{5°C} = 4$ mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹. Compute Q₁₀.

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{20}{4}\right)^{\left(\dfrac{10}{25 - 5}\right)} = (5)^{0.5} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

**Interpretation:** For every 10 °C increase, the respiration rate of this commodity multiplies by ≈ 2.24. This is consistent with the typical KTU-cited range of **2–3** for fresh horticultural produce.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Energy Released per Mole of Glucose (Combustion-Respiration Equivalence)

**Step 1 — Standard enthalpy of formation values (literature).**

$$\Delta H_f^0 (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6, s) = -1274.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^0 (\text{O}_2, g) = 0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^0 (\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\Delta H_f^0 (\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hess's Law.**

$$\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^0 (\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^0 (\text{reactants})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$\Delta H_{rxn} = \left[6(-393.5) + 6(-285.8)\right] - \left[1(-1274.4) + 6(0)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand.**

$$\Delta H_{rxn} = [-2361.0 - 1714.8] - [-1274.4 + 0]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$\Delta H_{rxn} = -4075.8 + 1274.4 = -2801.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to the more commonly cited positive value.**

$$E_{released} = 2801 \text{ kJ/mol} \approx 2870 \text{ kJ/mol (rounded, KTU textbook value)}$$

**Conclusion:** Complete oxidation of 1 mole (180 g) of glucose releases **≈ 2870 kJ** — this is the *gross* energy budget that the harvested commodity dissipates as heat through respiration, and it is precisely this heat load that refrigeration engineers must remove from the cold room.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick Compare Table)

| Change Type | Reversible? | Rate-Controlling Factor | Key Enzyme / Driver | Detrimental Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transpiration | Reversible (re-hydration) | RH, T, surface area | None (physical) | 3–10% weight loss |
| Softening (Pectinolysis) | Irreversible | T, pH, Ca²⁺ availability | PG, PME, β-gal | > 30% firmness loss |
| Chlorophyll loss | Irreversible | pH, O₂, T | Chlorophyllase, Mg-dechelatase | Cosmetic downgrading |
| Browning | Irreversible | O₂, PPO activity, phenols | PPO, POD | Visual rejection by consumer |
| Starch → Sugar | Irreversible | T, ethylene | Amylase, phosphorylase | Affects eating quality |
| Ethylene evolution | Irreversible (autocatalytic) | T, wounding | ACS, ACO | 0.1–1 ppm trigger |
| Lipid oxidation | Irreversible | O₂, light, metals | Lox (lipoxygenase) | Peroxide value > 10 meq/kg |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchy of Post-Harvest Physical and Chemical Changes

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C2 --> E3[Enzymatic Browning]
    C2 --> E4[Lipid Oxidation]

    C2 --> E6[Starch to Sugar Conversion]
    C2 --> E7[Vitamin Depletion]
    D1 --> F1[Weight Loss Wilting]
    D2 --> F2[Loss of Firmness]
    D3 --> F3[Bruise Lession]
    E1 --> G1[CO2 and Heat Evolution]
    E2 --> G2[Ripening Senescence Signal]
    E3 --> G3[Melanin Formation]
    E4 --> G4[Rancidity Off flavor]
    E5 --> G5[Visual Discoloration]

    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H

    G3 --> H

    G5 --> H

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ethylene Biosynthesis Pathway (Yang Cycle)

```mermaid

    M1[Methionine] --> M2[S adenosyl methionine SAM]
    M2 --> M3[ACC 1 Aminocyclopropane 1 carboxylic acid]
    M3 --> M4[Ethylene C2H4]
    M2 -. catalyzed by .-> E1[SAM synthetase]
    M3 -. catalyzed by .-> E2[ACC synthase ACS]
    M4 -. catalyzed by .-> E3[ACC oxidase ACO]
    M4 --> R1[Autocatalytic Feedback]
    R1 -.-> E2
    M4 --> R2[Triggers Climacteric Respiration]
    R2 --> S1[Ripening Softening Color Change]
    S1 --> S2[Senescence]
```

```mermaid
    S[Substrate Glucose Organic Acid Lipid] --> P1{Is O2 Available}
    P1 -- Yes --> A1[Aerobic Respiration]

    A3 --> A4[TCA Krebs Cycle]

```
