topics.title: Chilling Injury
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chilling Injury

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chilling Injury (CI)** is a reversible, time- and temperature-dependent physiological disorder that develops in the tissues of certain plants (predominantly tropical and subtropical fruits, vegetables, and ornamentals) when they are exposed to temperatures **above their freezing point** but **below a critical species-specific threshold** (typically **0 °C to 15 °C**). The disorder manifests as a cascade of cellular dysfunctions, ultimately leading to visible symptoms such as surface pitting, water-soaking, internal browning, failure of normal ripening, off-flavour development, and a sharp increase in susceptibility to secondary microbial decay.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, chilling injury is classified as a **physiological (abiotic) post-harvest disorder** — *not* a pathological disease, *not* a freezing injury, and *not* a nutritional deficiency. The damage is initiated at the **molecular level of the cell membrane**, long before macroscopic symptoms become apparent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Definition Format (Board-Standard):**
> "Chilling injury is the physiological damage sustained by chilling-sensitive horticultural commodities stored at temperatures above their freezing point but below the critical threshold temperature, caused by the gel-to-liquid-crystalline phase transition of cellular membrane lipids, resulting in loss of membrane integrity, metabolite leakage, and disrupted cellular homeostasis."

## 1.2 Key Terminology Disambiguation

| Term | Freezing Point Relation | Reversibility | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chilling Injury** | Above freezing point (0–15 °C) | Partially reversible if early | Membrane lipid phase change |
| **Freezing Injury** | Below freezing point | Largely irreversible | Intra-/extra-cellular ice formation |
| **Cold Stress** | Below optimum, above 0 °C | Reversible (hormesis) | Sub-optimal metabolism |
| **Chilling Sensitivity Index (CSI)** | Quantitative metric | N/A | Empirical/screening tool |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Refrigerator Surprise" Analogy**
>
> Imagine placing a **freshly cut jasmine flower (Malli Poo)** in your home refrigerator at 4 °C to keep it fresh overnight. By morning, the petals look brownish-black, soggy, and emit a foul smell — yet the temperature was never below 0 °C. The refrigerator, which works perfectly for apples and carrots, has *killed* the jasmine.
>
> Why? The jasmine is a **tropical-origin species**. Its cell membranes are made of lipids tuned to ~25 °C. At 4 °C, those membranes "stiffen" — like butter turning rock-hard in your fridge. Channels and pumps fail, internal cell contents leak out, and the tissue essentially "suffocates in its own juices."
>
> This is **chilling injury in action** — temperature is technically *safe* (above 0 °C), but *biologically damaging* for that species.
>
> Now, place a **Kashmir apple** in the same fridge — it stays crisp for weeks. Apple cell membranes are *engineered by evolution* for cold storage. The same temperature, the same refrigerator, the same duration — but a completely different biological outcome. **The difference is species-specific chilling sensitivity.**

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in CI Studies

- **Critical Threshold Temperature (T_c):** Lowest safe storage temperature for a commodity (commonly **13 °C** for bananas, **10 °C** for cucumbers, **7 °C** for bell peppers).
- **Time-Temperature Tolerance (TTT):** Cumulative hours a product can withstand a given sub-threshold temperature before symptoms appear.
- **Chilling Injury Index (CII):** A **0–4 (or 0–5) ordinal scale** multiplied by fraction of fruits showing the symptom.
- **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH):** Standardly maintained at **85–95 %** during CI studies to control confounding water loss.
- **Electrolyte Leakage (%):** Quantitative indicator of membrane damage — measured via **conductivity meter**, expressed as **µS cm⁻¹** normalized to total electrolytes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physiological Cascade (Step-by-Step)

Chilling injury is **not a single event** — it is a chain reaction. Memorize this sequence, because KTU questions frequently ask students to "explain the mechanism of chilling injury."

### Stage 1 — *Primary Event:* Membrane Lipid Phase Transition
- Cell membranes are **lipid bilayers** composed primarily of **phospholipids**.
- At the species-specific critical temperature (T_c), the membrane undergoes a transition from the **liquid-crystalline phase (fluid)** to the **gel phase (semi-solid)**.
- This transition is **analogous to butter solidifying in a refrigerator**.
- Unsaturated fatty acids (e.g., **linoleic, linolenic**) keep the membrane fluid; chilling-sensitive species have a **higher proportion of saturated fatty acids** → earlier phase change.

### Stage 2 — *Secondary Event:* Loss of Membrane Integrity
- The gel-phase membrane develops **phase separations** and **cracks**.
- **Membrane-bound enzymes** (ATPases, ion channels, desaturases) lose their conformational mobility.
- The result is **increased permeability** to ions and metabolites.

### Stage 3 — *Tertiary Event:* Cellular Dysfunction
- **Electrolyte leakage:** K⁺, Ca²⁺, and organic acids escape the cell → measured by rise in bathing solution conductivity.
- **Imbalance of cellular pH:** Cytosolic acidosis develops.
- **Impaired photosynthesis / respiration ratio** in green tissues.
- **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) accumulation:** H₂O₂, O₂⁻, and ·OH radicals overwhelm the **superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and peroxidase (POD)** defence systems.

### Stage 4 — *Quaternary Event:* Visible Symptoms
- **Surface pitting** (collapse of epidermal cells).
- **Water-soaking** (intercellular fluid leakage).
- **Internal browning** (polyphenol oxidase activity on leaked phenolics).
- **Failure to ripen** (impaired ethylene perception and ripening enzyme synthesis).
- **Off-flavours** (anaerobic fermentation products: ethanol, acetaldehyde).
- **Decay acceleration** (pathogens exploit compromised cuticle and tissue).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:**
> Symptoms of chilling injury are NEVER visible immediately at transfer from the chilling temperature. There is a **latency period** of typically **2–14 days** at the marketing/ambient temperature before symptoms appear. This latency is *why CI is so commercially dangerous* — produce looks perfect at the cold store gate.

## 2.2 Classification of Chilling-Sensitive vs. Chilling-Resistant Commodities

### 2.2.1 Chilling-Sensitive (Tropical/Subtropical Origin)

| Commodity | Safe Minimum Temp (°C) | Typical CI Symptoms |
|---|---|---|
| Banana (*Musa* spp.) | **13** | Peel browning, failure to ripen |
| Mango (*Mangifera indica*) | **13** | Skin scald, pulp browning, off-flavour |
| Papaya (*Carica papaya*) | **7** | Pitting, water-soaking, failure to ripen |
| Pineapple (*Ananas comosus*) | **10** | Internal browning ("blackheart") |
| Avocado (*Persea americana*) | **5–13** (variety dependent) | Flesh browning, vascular browning |
| Tomato (mature-green) | **13** | Pitting, alternaria rot, uneven ripening |
| Cucumber | **10** | Pitting, water-soaked lesions |
| Bell pepper | **7** | Pitting, seed browning, Alternaria |
| Eggplant (brinjal) | **8** | Pitting, surface bronzing |
| Sweet potato | **13** | Hardcore, internal browning, off-flavour |
| Lime / Lemon | **10–13** | Pitting, stylar-end breakdown |

### 2.2.2 Chilling-Resistant (Temperate Origin)

| Commodity | Safe Storage Temp (°C) |
|---|---|
| Apple | **-1 to 4** |
| Pear | **-1 to 0** |
| Cabbage | **0** |
| Carrot | **0** |
| Onion (dried) | **0** |
| Grape | **-0.5 to 0** |
| Kiwifruit | **0** |

## 2.3 The Q₁₀ Concept — Foundation of Temperature Biology

The **temperature coefficient (Q₁₀)** quantifies the factor by which the rate of a biological reaction changes for every **10 °C** rise in temperature. It is the single most important equation in post-harvest temperature biology and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Where:
- **R₁, R₂** = reaction rates (e.g., respiration rate, CI symptom development) at temperatures **T₁** and **T₂** respectively (in °C).
- For most **enzymatic reactions**, Q₁₀ ≈ **2 to 3**.
- For **chilling injury symptom development**, Q₁₀ values are typically **> 3**, indicating strong temperature dependency.
- For **diffusion-controlled processes**, Q₁₀ ≈ **1.2 to 1.5**.

### Intuitive Meaning of Q₁₀
- A Q₁₀ of **2** means: **"For every 10 °C you raise the temperature, the reaction rate doubles."**
- A Q₁₀ of **3** means: **"The reaction rate triples for every 10 °C rise."**
- This is the **physical basis of why we refrigerate food** — lowering T from 25 °C to 5 °C (a 20 °C drop) with Q₁₀ = 2.5 reduces respiration by a factor of **(2.5)² = 6.25×** — dramatically slowing senescence and spoilage.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

| Equation | Formula | Variables | Units / Boundary Conditions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | k = rate constant, A = pre-exponential factor, Eₐ = activation energy, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, T = absolute temperature (K) | Valid for T well above 0 °C; breaks down near phase transitions | Predicts respiration & senescence rate vs. temperature |
| **Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient** | $Q_{10} = (R_2/R_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | R₁, R₂ = rates; T₁, T₂ = °C | Typical: 2–3 for enzymes; >3 for CI | Shelf-life estimation, refrigeration design |
| **Respiration Rate (Clausen–Huff)** | $R = a \cdot Q_{10}^{(T-T_{ref})/10}$ | a = rate at reference temp; T_ref = 5 °C or 10 °C | Tropical fruits have **2–5×** higher R than temperate | Cold chain design |
| **Chilling Injury Index (CII)** | $CII = \dfrac{\sum (S_i \cdot N_i)}{4 \cdot N_{total}}$ | Sᵢ = severity score (0–4), Nᵢ = fruits at score i, N_total = total fruits | Score 0 = none; 4 = severe | Quantifies CI in experiments |
| **Electrolyte Leakage (%)** | $EL\% = \dfrac{EC_{sample} - EC_{water}}{EC_{autoclaved} - EC_{water}} \times 100$ | EC = electrical conductivity (µS cm⁻¹) | Measured after 2 h shake + autoclave | Membrane integrity proxy |
| **Phase Transition Temp (T_m)** | $T_m = T_m^0 + \sum (\Delta T_{\text{additive}})$ | Additives: Ca²⁺, sugars ↑ T_m; unsaturated FA ↓ T_m | DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) measurement | Membrane stability screening |
| **Van't Hoff / Membrane Fluidity** | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | ΔG = Gibbs free energy, ΔH = enthalpy, ΔS = entropy | At T_m, ΔG = 0 | Predicts thermotropic transition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** In all markdown tables above, vertical bars used for absolute-value notation or set-builder are rendered as **\vert** or **\mid** to prevent table syntax breakage. Do not write \vert x \vert inside an unescaped table row.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

In commercial **reefer container** (refrigerated shipping) engineering, the *dual objective* is:
1. **Lower temperature → suppress respiration & microbial growth** (Arrhenius + Q₁₀ logic).
2. **Stay above the critical threshold → avoid CI** in sensitive cargo (banana, mango, avocado shipments from Kerala/Tamil Nadu to the Gulf or Europe).

The optimum setpoint is determined by the **highest-T_c commodity** in the mixed load. A single banana in a 4 °C apple shipment can ruin the entire pallet — this is why **"mixed load discipline"** is a standard KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Predicting Respiration Rate at Any Storage Temperature

**Problem:** A mango has a measured respiration rate of **R₁ = 18 mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹** at **T₁ = 15 °C**. The fruit is to be transported in a reefer at **T₂ = 5 °C**. Given the tropical-fruit Q₁₀ for respiration ≈ **2.5**, find the expected respiration rate at the shipping temperature and the percentage reduction achieved.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the known values

$$2.5 = \left( \frac{R_2}{18} \right)^{\frac{10}{5 - 15}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the exponent

$$\frac{10}{5 - 15} = \frac{10}{-10} = -1$$

### Step 4 — Apply the exponent

$$2.5 = \left( \frac{R_2}{18} \right)^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{2.5} = \frac{R_2}{18}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{18}{2.5} = 7.2 \;\; \text{mg CO}_2 \; \text{kg}^{-1} \; \text{h}^{-1}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the percentage reduction

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{R_1 - R_2}{R_1} \times 100 = \frac{18 - 7.2}{18} \times 100 = 60 \;\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Lowering the storage temperature by 10 °C reduces mango respiration by **60 %** — a striking demonstration of why cold-chain logistics is the *single largest determinant* of post-harvest life for tropical fruits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chilling Injury Index (CII) Calculation

**Problem:** In a CI trial on **20 mangoes** stored at 5 °C for 14 days and then ripened at 25 °C for 5 days, the severity scoring gives the following distribution:

| Score (Sᵢ) | 0 (None) | 1 (Trace) | 2 (Mild) | 3 (Moderate) | 4 (Severe) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number (Nᵢ) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 5 | 3 |

**Find the CII.**

### Step 1 — State the formula

$$CII = \frac{\sum (S_i \cdot N_i)}{4 \cdot N_{total}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the numerator

$$\sum (S_i \cdot N_i) = (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 4) + (2 \times 6) + (3 \times 5) + (4 \times 3)$$

$$= 0 + 4 + 12 + 15 + 12 = 43$$

### Step 3 — Compute the denominator

$$4 \cdot N_{total} = 4 \times 20 = 80$$

### Step 4 — Compute the CII

$$CII = \frac{43}{80} = 0.5375 \;\; (\text{or } 53.75 \;\%)$$

### Step 5 — Interpret

A CII of **0.54** indicates **moderate chilling injury** — the storage protocol (5 °C for 14 d) is **unacceptable** for mango. The treatment must be redesigned (e.g., store at **13 °C** or apply intermittent warming).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Arrhenius-Based Shelf-Life Prediction

**Problem:** Strawberries have an activation energy for senescence **Eₐ = 75 kJ mol⁻¹** and a pre-exponential factor **A = 3.2 × 10¹⁴ day⁻¹** (at a reference of 20 °C). A processor wants to extend shelf life by 4×. Find the new storage temperature.

### Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius equation at two temperatures

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$$

$$k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$$

### Step 2 — Form the ratio (A cancels)

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

### Step 3 — Shelf life is inversely proportional to rate constant

$$\frac{SL_2}{SL_1} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = 4$$

### Step 4 — Take the natural log

$$\ln(4) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Insert values (Eₐ = 75 000 J mol⁻¹, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, T₁ = 293.15 K)

$$\ln(4) = 1.3863 = \frac{75000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{293.15} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{293.15} = \frac{1.3863 \times 8.314}{75000} = 1.5369 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for T₂

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{293.15} + 1.5369 \times 10^{-4} = 3.411 \times 10^{-3} + 0.15369 \times 10^{-3} = 3.4948 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{3.4948 \times 10^{-3}} = 286.13 \; \text{K} = 12.98 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

### Step 7 — Sanity check
A 4× shelf-life increase is achieved by lowering temperature by only ~7 °C (from 20 → 13 °C). This explains the **huge commercial value** of even modest cooling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Chilling vs. Freezing vs. Heat Injury

| Parameter | Chilling Injury | Freezing Injury | Heat Injury |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature range** | 0 °C to T_c | Below 0 °C (ice nucleation) | Above 40–55 °C |
| **Mechanism** | Membrane lipid phase transition | Intracellular ice crystals → mechanical rupture | Protein denaturation, membrane fluidization |
| **Onset speed** | Slow (days–weeks) | Rapid (minutes–hours) | Rapid (seconds–minutes) |
| **Reversibility** | Partially reversible if early | Irreversible once ice forms | Often irreversible |
| **Visible sign** | Pitting, browning, off-flavour | Translucency, glassy texture, drip | Scald, bleaching, cooked appearance |
| **Latency at ambient** | Yes (2–14 days) | No | No |
| **Prevention** | T ≥ T_c, intermittent warming, CA, wax | T > freezing point of tissue (~ -0.5 to -2 °C) | Rapid pre-cooling, shade, hydrocooling |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Practice — Worked Examples

### Example A — Q₁₀ from two respiration rates
A banana shows **R₁ = 25 mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ at 20 °C** and **R₂ = 9 mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ at 10 °C**. Find Q₁₀.

**Solution:**

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{25}{9} \right)^{\frac{10}{20-10}} = (2.778)^{1} = 2.78$$

### Example B — Threshold temperature determination
A fruit develops CI at 5 °C in 7 days but is symptom-free for 21 days at 10 °C. Estimate the *operative* critical threshold (use T_c ≈ temperature of 0 % CI probability + 1 °C safety margin).

**Solution:** Operative T_c ≈ **10 °C** + 1 °C buffer → **Store at 11 °C minimum**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Mark Distribution Note (2024 Scheme):** Each Part A question is **3 marks**. Time: 5 minutes per question. *Define → State mechanism → Give one example* format is the highest-scoring template.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define chilling injury. List any four chilling-sensitive horticultural commodities with their safe minimum storage temperatures.**

**Model Answer:**

Chilling injury (CI) is a reversible physiological disorder that develops in tropical and subtropical fruits and vegetables when stored at temperatures **above their freezing point but below a species-specific critical threshold**, due to the gel-to-liquid-crystalline phase transition of membrane lipids.

Four chilling-sensitive commodities and their safe minimum temperatures:

1. **Banana (Musa spp.)** — 13 °C
2. **Mango (Mangifera indica)** — 13 °C
3. **Papaya (Carica papaya)** — 7 °C
4. **Cucumber (Cucumis sativus)** — 10 °C

*(Any four correct commodity–temperature pairs ⇒ full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of membrane lipid phase transition in the development of chilling injury.**

**Model Answer:**

Cell membranes of all living tissues are **phospholipid bilayers**. The physical state of these lipids depends on temperature:

- Above the **critical temperature (T_c):** membrane exists in the **liquid-crystalline (fluid) state** — proteins move laterally, ion channels function, ATPases operate, and selective permeability is maintained.
- Below T_c: the membrane transitions to the **gel (semi-solid) state** — analogous to butter hardening in a refrigerator.

**Consequences of the gel-state transition:**

1. **Phase separation** of membrane lipids creates cracks and voids.
2. **Embedded proteins (ATPases, ion channels, desaturases) lose conformational mobility.**
3. **Loss of selective permeability** → leakage of K⁺, Ca²⁺, organic acids (measured as ↑ electrolyte leakage).
4. **Imbalance in cellular pH and ion homeostasis.**
5. **Activation of polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** on leaked phenolics → **enzymatic browning**.

Chilling-sensitive species (banana, mango) have a **higher proportion of saturated fatty acids** in their membrane phospholipids, so T_c is reached at relatively *higher* temperatures (~10–13 °C). Chilling-resistant species (apple, pear) have **more unsaturated fatty acids** (oleic, linoleic) that keep the membrane fluid at low temperatures, so their T_c is well below 0 °C.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7**. The student attempts **ONE choice out of two**. Aim for *2–3 pages* of well-structured answer with **diagrams, headings, and equations** for full marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the physiological and biochemical mechanisms of chilling injury in fruits and vegetables. Add a note on the critical threshold temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the effects of intermittent warming, controlled atmosphere storage, and edible wax coatings as post-harvest strategies to mitigate chilling injury. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. Definition and Threshold:**
Chilling injury is a physiological disorder occurring when chilling-sensitive tissues are held at **0 °C to ~15 °C**. The **critical threshold temperature (T_c)** is the lowest safe storage temperature for a given commodity. Below T_c, reversible → irreversible damage accumulates as a function of time and temperature.

| Commodity | T_c (°C) | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Banana | 13 | Tropical |
| Mango | 13 | Tropical |
| Papaya | 7 | Tropical |
| Cucumber | 10 | Subtropical |
| Bell pepper | 7 | Subtropical |

**2. Physiological Mechanism — Membrane Phase Transition:**
The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer. Below T_c:
- Saturated fatty acids "freeze" first, creating **gel-phase domains** in an otherwise fluid membrane.
- This is a **thermotropic phase transition** (confirmed experimentally by **Differential Scanning Calorimetry, DSC**).
- **Membrane-bound enzymes** (H⁺-ATPase, Ca²⁺-ATPase, NADPH oxidase) lose activity.
- **Ion gradients collapse** → measured as ↑ **electrolyte leakage (EC method)**.

**3. Biochemical Mechanism — ROS and Browning:**
- Impaired electron transport chains in mitochondria and chloroplasts leak electrons to O₂ → formation of **superoxide (O₂⁻), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), hydroxyl radical (·OH)**.
- Antioxidant enzymes (**SOD, CAT, POD, APX, GR**) become overwhelmed.
- Membrane lipids undergo **peroxidation** (measured as **MDA = malondialdehyde** content).
- Released **phenolic substrates** encounter **PPO** → **quinones** → **brown polymers (melanins)**.

**4. Visible Symptoms:**
- **Surface pitting** (epidermal collapse)
- **Water-soaking** (intercellular leakage)
- **Internal browning / blackheart** (pineapple)
- **Failure to ripen** (impaired climacteric ethylene response)
- **Off-flavour** (ethanol, acetaldehyde accumulation)
- **Increased decay** (compromised cuticle)

*(7 marks: 2 for definition+threshold table, 3 for mechanism, 2 for symptoms)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**1. Intermittent Warming (IW):**
- **Principle:** Periodically remove produce from chilling temperature and hold at warmer non-chilling temperature for short periods.
- **Typical protocol:** Store at 5 °C for 14 days, warm to 20 °C for 1 day, repeat.
- **Mechanism of protection:** Allows **membrane lipid reordering**, restoration of enzyme activity, and **evacuation of accumulated ethanol/acetaldehyde**.
- **Effective in:** mango, papaya, citrus, cucumber.
- **Drawback:** Increased energy cost; risk of condensation → microbial growth.

**2. Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage:**
- **Principle:** Modify the storage gas composition — typically **reduce O₂ to 2–5 %** and **elevate CO₂ to 3–10 %**.
- **Mechanism:** Reduced O₂ **slows respiration** and **ROS generation**; elevated CO₂ acts as a **competitive inhibitor of ethylene action** and suppresses CI symptom expression.
- **Effective in:** avocado, mango, kiwifruit, apple.
- **Drawback:** Capital-intensive; risk of anaerobic fermentation if O₂ drops below ~1 %.

**3. Edible Wax / Lipid Coatings:**
- **Principle:** Apply a thin hydrophobic layer (carnauba wax, shellac, chitosan, lecithin) on the fruit surface.
- **Mechanism:** Coatings **reduce transpiration** (less water loss → less cellular stress) and create a **modified internal atmosphere** (↑ CO₂, ↓ O₂) similar to CA.
- **Effective in:** citrus, apple, mango, cucumber.
- **Drawback:** Excessive coating → anaerobic fermentation, off-flavour.

**4. Comparative Summary Table:**

| Strategy | Capital Cost | Effectiveness | Energy Use | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intermittent warming | Low | Moderate–High | Medium | Long-distance transport of mango, papaya |
| CA storage | High | High | High | Premium avocado, apple storage |
| Edible wax | Low | Moderate | Low | Citrus, mango, cucumber at retail |

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for the comparative table)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3]**

**(a)** What is Q₁₀? Derive an expression for Q₁₀ and explain its significance in estimating the shelf life of perishable horticultural produce. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed account of the symptoms of chilling injury in (i) banana, (ii) mango, and (iii) cucumber. Add a note on the Chilling Injury Index (CII). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. Definition:**
Q₁₀ is the **temperature coefficient** — the factor by which the rate of a biological (or chemical) reaction changes for a **10 °C rise in temperature**.

**2. Derivation:**

Let R₁ be the rate at T₁ (°C) and R₂ be the rate at T₂ (°C). Define:

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

**Derivation from the Arrhenius equation:**

The Arrhenius relation gives the rate constant:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

At two temperatures T₁ and T₂:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

Define the fractional temperature change:

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}$$

Then by definition of Q₁₀:

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\alpha}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln Q_{10} = \frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}$$

For typical biochemical reactions, **Eₐ ≈ 50–80 kJ mol⁻¹**, giving **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3**.

**3. Significance in Shelf Life Estimation:**
- **Respiration rate** of produce is the single most important indicator of post-harvest life.
- A typical Q₁₀ = 2.5 means that **lowering storage T by 10 °C reduces respiration ~2.5×**.
- For banana: respiration at 25 °C ≈ 80 mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹; at 15 °C ≈ 32; at 5 °C ≈ 13.
- Commercial cold-chain design (set point, transit duration) is engineered around Q₁₀ calculations.
- **However, Q₁₀ is NOT valid across a phase transition** — it fails in the chilling-injury temperature range for sensitive crops (a "break" in the Arrhenius plot at T_c).

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 3 for derivation, 3 for application)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**1. Banana (T_c = 13 °C):**
- **Peel browning / blackening** ("chilling-induced peel discoloration") — PPO activity on dopamine.
- **Failure to ripen uniformly** — starch-to-sugar conversion is impaired; uneven yellowing.
- **Dull, grey-green peel** (loss of chlorophyll unmasking).
- **Subepidermal vascular browning.**
- **Reduced aroma** (volatile ester synthesis blocked).
- **Increased finger drop and crown rot.**

**2. Mango (T_c = 13 °C):**
- **Skin scald** — irregular sunken, discoloured patches on the peel.
- **Pulp browning / blackening** (redox imbalance, PPO activity).
- **Off-flavour** (ethanol, acetaldehyde accumulation from anaerobic respiration).
- **Failure to develop normal carotenoid pigmentation** during ripening.
- **Increased incidence of anthracnose** (*Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*) and **stem-end rot**.

**3. Cucumber (T_c = 10 °C):**
- **Surface pitting** — most diagnostic symptom.
- **Water-soaked lesions** (intercellular fluid leakage).
- **Accelerated decay** by *Alternaria*, *Botrytis*, and bacterial soft rots.
- **Loss of turgor and glossiness** (cuticle damage).
- **Internal seed browning** (in severe cases).

**4. Chilling Injury Index (CII):**
- A **quantitative scoring scale** (0–4 or 0–5) used to compare treatments.

| Score | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | None | No visible symptoms |
| 1 | Trace | < 10 % surface affected |
| 2 | Mild | 10–25 % surface affected |
| 3 | Moderate | 25–50 % surface affected, slight internal browning |
| 4 | Severe | > 50 % surface affected, deep internal damage, decay |

**Formula:**

$$CII = \frac{\sum (S_i \cdot N_i)}{S_{max} \cdot N_{total}}$$

where Sᵢ = score, Nᵢ = number of fruits at that score, S_max = maximum score (usually 4), N_total = total fruits.

CII allows statistical comparison of treatments (e.g., wax vs. control) and is a **mandatory parameter in KTU lab records**.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 for symptoms, CII respectively)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Mark-Deduction Traps in Chilling Injury Answers (KTU Board Examiner Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CI with Freezing Injury** — *most common error.* CI is **above 0 °C**; freezing injury is **below 0 °C** with ice crystals. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Skipping the membrane mechanism** — Writing only symptoms without explaining the **lipid phase transition** is treated as incomplete. Always draw or describe the bilayer.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the latency period** — A 2-mark question asking "when do CI symptoms appear" loses marks if you say "immediately" — the answer must include "**2–14 days after transfer to ambient temperature**."
>
> 4. **Wrong threshold values** — Mixing up banana (13 °C) and apple (0 °C) is treated as a fundamental error. Memorize the threshold table.
>
> 5. **No Q₁₀ derivation** — A 7-mark question on Q₁₀ expects a **derivation from the Arrhenius equation**, not just a definition. Loss: **3–4 marks**.
>
> 6. **Confusing CII with subjective scoring** — CII is a **formula-based numerical index**, not a feel-based description.
>
> 7. **Missing the trade-off principle** — In any "prevention of CI" answer, the examiner expects the *trade-off* — "**lower T reduces respiration but increases CI risk; the optimum is just above T_c**." Without this, the answer is judged superficial.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chilling Injury — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **CI** = disorder at **0 °C < T < T_c** in chilling-sensitive (tropical/subtropical) produce.
> - **T_c** examples: banana **13 °C**, mango **13 °C**, papaya **7 °C**, cucumber **10 °C**, bell pepper **7 °C**, tomato (green) **13 °C**, pineapple **10 °C**.
> - **Mechanism (mnemonic "M-L-E-R-S"):** **M**embrane phase change → **L**oss of integrity → **E**lectrolyte leakage → **R**OS accumulation → **S**ymptoms + secondary decay.
> - **Symptoms (mnemonic "P-W-B-R-O-D"):** **P**itting, **W**ater-soaking, **B**rowning, **R**ipening failure, **O**ff-flavour, **D**ecay.
> - **Latency:** symptoms appear **2–14 days** AFTER transfer to ambient — *not* during cold storage.
> - **Key equation — Q₁₀:** $Q_{10} = (R_2/R_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$, typically **2–3** for enzymes, **> 3** for CI.
> - **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ — respiration/suppoilage rate vs. absolute temperature.
> - **CII formula:** $CII = \frac{\sum (S_i \cdot N_i)}{S_{max} \cdot N_{total}}$ — quantitative symptom index.
> - **Electrolyte leakage (%):** proxy for membrane damage — measured by conductivity meter.
> - **Membrane composition rule:** Chilling-sensitive = **more saturated** FAs (high T_m); Chilling-resistant = **more unsaturated** FAs (low T_m).
> - **Preventive strategies (mnemonic "CATCH"):** **C**ontrolled atmosphere, **A**void T < T_c, **T**emperature preconditioning, **C**oatings (wax/lecithin), **H**ot air/hot water treatment + Intermittent warming.
> - **Trade-off principle:** Lower T ⇒ less respiration/microbial growth BUT more CI for sensitive crops → optimum is **T just above T_c of the most sensitive commodity in the load**.
> - **Apple/pear are chilling-resistant** (storage at 0 °C for months); **banana/mango are chilling-sensitive** (storage at 13 °C only).
> - **Freezing injury ≠ CI** — freezing forms ice crystals (mechanical), CI forms gel-phase membranes (physico-chemical).
> - **Ethylene** (climacteric fruits) — CI disrupts normal ethylene signalling → uneven or absent ripening.
> - **Antioxidant enzymes** (SOD, CAT, POD, APX) are the cellular defence against CI-induced ROS; their decline marks the **irreversibility threshold** of CI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freezing Injury
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freezing Injury

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Freezing Injury** is defined as the irreversible, structural, and physiological damage sustained by plant tissues (fruits, vegetables, and other horticultural produce) when their cellular water is converted into ice crystals at temperatures below the tissue-specific freezing point, typically ranging from **−0.5 °C to −3.0 °C** depending on solute concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Freezing injury is the *post-freeze* deterioration that occurs because ice crystals — formed either in the extracellular spaces, intracellular spaces, or both — mechanically rupture cell membranes, displace cytoplasmic contents, and cause irreversible loss of compartmentalization, ultimately leading to tissue death, water-soaking, and accelerated microbial spoilage upon thawing.

### 1.2 Critical Distinction: Freezing Injury vs Chilling Injury

| Parameter | Chilling Injury | Freezing Injury |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Temperature Range | Above 0 °C (typically 0–13 °C) | Below 0 °C (typically −0.5 to −3 °C) |
| Phase Change of Water | No ice formation | Ice crystal formation |
| Primary Cause | Metabolic dysfunction | Physical/mechanical disruption |
| Reversibility | Often partially reversible | Generally irreversible |
| Symptom Onset | Hours to days | Immediate post-thaw |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Balloon in a Freezer" Analogy:** Imagine a water balloon (the plant cell) placed in a freezer. When the surrounding temperature drops below zero, the water inside wants to expand as it freezes. Ice occupies ~**9% more volume** than liquid water. If freezing happens *fast* (small crystals everywhere inside) — the balloon shatters into microscopic fragments. If freezing happens *slow* (large ice crystals form outside the balloon first) — water is sucked out of the balloon by osmotic pull, causing the cell to shrivel and collapse. Either way, the balloon is ruined. Plant tissues behave the same way during freezing injury.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Freezing Point of Pure Water:** **0 °C** (273.15 K)
- **Typical Tissue Freezing Point:** **−0.5 °C to −2.5 °C** (depressed by dissolved solutes)
- **Critical Ice Nucleation Temperature for Most Produce:** **−2 °C to −5 °C**
- **Ice Density at 0 °C:** **0.917 g/cm³** (vs water 1.000 g/cm³)
- **Latent Heat of Fusion of Water:** **334 kJ/kg** (≈ 80 cal/g)
- **Specific Heat of Ice:** **2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Specific Heat of Water:** **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Ice:** **2.2 W/(m·K)** (vs water 0.56 W/(m·K))

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic of Freezing Injury

When a fruit or vegetable is subjected to sub-zero temperatures, the following cascade of events occurs:

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat Removal (Above Freezing Point):**
- The produce temperature drops from ambient (e.g., 25 °C) toward the freezing point.
- Energy is removed as *sensible heat* following $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- Cell metabolism slows; respiration rate decreases.

**Step 2 — Supercooling Phase:**
- The tissue cools *below* its equilibrium freezing point without ice forming (typically 2–4 °C below).
- Water remains in a metastable liquid state.
- This phase is unstable — nucleation is imminent.

**Step 3 — Ice Nucleation (Phase Change Begins):**
- Ice nuclei form, predominantly in the **extracellular spaces** first (because cell walls/membranes act as barriers and extracellular fluid has lower solute concentration, hence higher freezing point).
- Latent heat of fusion is released, causing a temporary temperature plateau at the freezing point.

**Step 4 — Crystal Growth & Cellular Dehydration:**
- Extracellular ice grows by drawing water *out* of the cells via the vapor pressure gradient.
- This causes intracellular dehydration, concentration of cytoplasmic solutes, and plasmolysis.
- If cooling is **slow** → large extracellular crystals → cell collapse.

**Step 5 — Intracellular Ice Formation (if cooling is fast enough):**
- If the cooling rate exceeds the rate of water efflux, intracellular ice forms.
- Intracellular ice is **mechanically devastating** — it punctures organelles, the plasma membrane, and tonoplast.

**Step 6 — Membrane Phase Transition & Lipid Damage:**
- Membrane lipids undergo a phase transition from liquid-crystalline to gel state at low temperatures.
- This causes membrane rigidification, loss of selective permeability, and electrolyte leakage.

**Step 7 — Post-Thaw Deterioration:**
- Upon thawing, damaged cells release nutrients and cellular contents → substrate for microbial growth.
- Tissue becomes water-soaked, soft, discolored, and rapidly spoils.

### 2.2 Types of Freezing Injury

1. **Mechanical Injury** — direct rupture by ice crystals
2. **Dehydration/Plasmolysis Injury** — osmotic water loss
3. **Membrane Injury** — lipid phase transition and leakage
4. **Metabolic Injury** — disrupted enzyme systems and respiration
5. **Oxidative Injury** — ROS accumulation post-thaw

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Freezing point depression; $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant of water (**1.86 °C·kg/mol**); $m$ = molality; $i$ = van't Hoff factor | $T_f$ depression per molal concentration |
| 2 | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \exp\left(\dfrac{-\Delta H_f}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_f}\right)\right)$ | Water activity at sub-zero temp; $\Delta H_f$ = enthalpy of fusion (**6.01 kJ/mol** for ice); $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K | Valid for pure water; $T < T_f$ |
| 3 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removal; $c_p$ = specific heat | Pre-freezing zone |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat removal at freezing point; $L_f$ = **334 kJ/kg** | Phase change zone |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{L}{T \cdot \Delta V}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron equation (solid-liquid equilibrium); $L$ = latent heat; $\Delta V$ = volume change | Pressure-temperature relationship at phase boundary |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{water \to ice} = +9\%$ | Volume expansion upon freezing | Mechanical stress factor |
| 7 | $t_{pl} = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot \rho \cdot L_f}{k \cdot \Delta T}$ | Plank's equation — freezing time; $d$ = thickness; $\rho$ = density; $k$ = thermal conductivity | Predicts freezing time of food slab |
| 8 | $E_a(\text{leakage}) = f(T, \text{time})$ | Electrolyte leakage rate as Arrhenius function | Post-thaw quality metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:** Engineers designing cold-chain logistics, refrigerated transport, and cryogenic preservation systems must use these equations to predict product survival, optimize blast-freezer parameters, and avoid the **zone of maximum ice crystal formation (−1 °C to −5 °C)** — often called the *zone of largest damage*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Freezing Point Depression of a Plant Tissue

The freezing point of a plant tissue is depressed below 0 °C because dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, organic acids) lower the chemical potential of water.

**Starting Point — Raoult's Law for Ideal Solutions:**

The vapor pressure of water over a solution is reduced by the mole fraction of solute:

$$P_{solution} = X_{solvent} \cdot P^0_{solvent}$$

At the freezing point, the chemical potential of liquid water equals that of ice:

$$\mu_{water}^{liquid}(T_f) = \mu_{water}^{ice}(T_f)$$

This leads to the integrated form of the freezing point depression:

$$\Delta T_f = T_f^{pure} - T_f^{solution} = K_f \cdot m$$

For water, $K_f = 1.86$ °C·kg/mol. For a typical fruit containing **0.5 molal** dissolved solutes:

$$\Delta T_f = (1.86)(0.5) = 0.93 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_f^{tissue} = 0 - 0.93 = -0.93 \text{ °C}$$

This matches empirical observations that most fruits begin freezing between **−0.5 °C and −2.5 °C**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Water Activity Below Freezing Point

Water activity $a_w$ governs all microbial and enzymatic reactions. Below 0 °C, $a_w$ decreases further due to ice formation.

Starting from the Clausius-Clapeyron relation integrated for the ice-liquid equilibrium:

$$\ln(a_w) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

Let us calculate $a_w$ at **−5 °C (268.15 K)** given $T_f = 273.15$ K and $\Delta H_f = 6010$ J/mol:

$$\ln(a_w) = \frac{6010}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{268.15}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{273.15} = 3.661 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{268.15} = 3.729 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{268.15} = -6.82 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln(a_w) = 722.6 \times (-6.82 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.0493$$

$$a_w = e^{-0.0493} \approx 0.952$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At −5 °C, $a_w \approx 0.95$, meaning ~**5% of water remains unfrozen** as a concentrated sugar-salt solution. This unfrozen fraction is where microbial metabolism (and spoilage enzymes) still operate, even in the frozen state.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plank's Equation for Freezing Time

For engineering design of blast freezers, predicting how long a slab of food takes to freeze is critical.

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pl} &= \frac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot d^2}{k \cdot \Delta T} \\
\text{where:} \quad \rho &= \text{density of food} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3] \\
L_f &= \text{latent heat of fusion} \quad [\text{J/kg}] \\
d &= \text{thickness (half-thickness for slab)} \quad [\text{m}] \\
k &= \text{thermal conductivity} \quad [\text{W/(m·K)}] \\
\Delta T &= T_{initial} - T_{freezing} \quad [\text{K}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Example:** A slab of frozen spinach, thickness **0.04 m**, density **950 kg/m³**, latent heat **280 kJ/kg** (lower than pure water due to solids content), thermal conductivity **1.5 W/(m·K)**, initial temperature **20 °C**, freezer at **−30 °C**.

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 0.04 \text{ m} \\
\Delta T &= 20 - (-30) = 50 \text{ K} \\
t_{pl} &= \frac{(950)(280000)(0.04)^2}{(1.5)(50)} \\
t_{pl} &= \frac{(950)(280000)(0.0016)}{75} \\
t_{pl} &= \frac{425{,}600}{75} \\
t_{pl} &= 5674.7 \text{ seconds} \\
t_{pl} &\approx 1.58 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Note:** The half-thickness formula assumes *one-dimensional* heat transfer through a slab with insulated edges. For irregular geometries (sphere, cylinder), Plank's equation uses different geometric constants (shape factors).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Volume Expansion Stress on Cell Wall

When water converts to ice, volume increases by 9%. The mechanical stress $\sigma$ on the cell wall can be estimated as:

$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} \cdot E_{cell\,wall}$$

Where $E_{cell\,wall}$ is the elastic modulus of the cell wall (typically **0.1–10 MPa** for plant tissues) and $\Delta V/V_0 = 0.09$:

$$\sigma = 0.09 \times E_{cell\,wall}$$

For a typical $E = 5$ MPa:

$$\sigma = 0.09 \times 5 = 0.45 \text{ MPa}$$

This exceeds the tensile strength of many parenchyma cell walls (~**0.2–0.5 MPa**), explaining why freezing causes structural rupture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Freezing Injury Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ambient Temperature Produce"] --> B["Sensible Heat Removal"]
    B --> C{"Reaches Tissue Freezing Point"}
    C --> D["Supercooling Phase 2 to 4 C Below FP"]
    D --> E["Extracellular Ice Nucleation"]
    E --> F["Vapor Pressure Gradient Forms"]
    F --> G["Intracellular Water Migrates Out"]
    G --> H{"Cooling Rate"}
    H -->|Slow| I["Large Extracellular Crystals"]
    H -->|Fast| J["Intracellular Ice Formation"]
    I --> K["Cell Plasmolysis and Collapse"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Membrane Phase Transition Lipid Damage"]
    L --> M["Electrolyte Leakage and ROS Burst"]
    M --> N["Post Thaw Water Soaking and Discoloration"]
    N --> O["Accelerated Microbial Spoilage"]
    O --> P["Complete Tissue Death"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification and Mitigation of Freezing Injury

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Freezing Injury Types"] --> B["Mechanical Injury"]
    A --> C["Dehydration Injury"]
    A --> D["Membrane Injury"]
    A --> E["Metabolic Injury"]
    A --> F["Oxidative Injury"]

    B --> G["Cause Large Ice Crystals"]
    C --> H["Cause Osmotic Water Loss"]
    D --> I["Cause Lipid Rigidity"]
    E --> J["Cause Enzyme Dysfunction"]
    F --> K["Cause ROS Accumulation"]

    G --> L["Mitigation Strategies"]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M["Rapid Freezing Small Crystals"]
    L --> N["Cryoprotectants Sugars Glycerol"]
    L --> O["Temperature Preconditioning"]
    L --> P["Cultivar Selection Cold Tolerant"]
    L --> Q["Controlled Thawing Protocols"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cold Chain Engineering to Prevent Freezing Injury

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Harvest Point"] --> B["Pre cooling to Remove Field Heat"]
    B --> C["Storage Temperature Selection"]
    C --> D{"Temperature Above FP or Below FP"}
    D -->|Above FP| E["Chilled Storage 0 to 13 C"]
    D -->|Below FP| F["Frozen Storage Below 18 C"]
    E --> G["Humidity Control 85 to 95 percent RH"]
    F --> H["Blast Freezing Tunnel"]
    H --> I["Quick Pass Through Zone of Max Ice Formation"]
    I --> J["Long Term Frozen Storage"]
    G --> K["Distribution Cold Chain"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Retail Display"]
    L --> M["Controlled Thawing at End User"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define freezing injury. How does it differ from chilling injury in post-harvest produce?*

**Model Answer:**
Freezing injury is the irreversible damage to plant tissues caused by the formation of ice crystals at temperatures below the tissue freezing point (typically −0.5 °C to −2.5 °C). It involves mechanical rupture by ice, cellular dehydration, and membrane disruption. Chilling injury, in contrast, occurs at temperatures *above* 0 °C (typically 0–13 °C for tropical produce) without ice formation, and is caused by metabolic dysfunction. Chilling injury is often partially reversible upon return to optimum temperature, while freezing injury is generally irreversible. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*State Clausius-Clapeyron equation and explain its relevance to freezing point of food tissues.*

**Model Answer:**
The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{L}{T \Delta V}$, relating pressure and temperature at a phase boundary. Its relevance to freezing injury lies in explaining why the freezing point of foods (containing dissolved solutes) is depressed below 0 °C. It allows prediction of water activity at sub-zero temperatures, which governs the fraction of unfrozen water and hence microbial/enzymatic activity in frozen foods. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the mechanism of freezing injury in fruits and vegetables. Discuss the role of cooling rate in determining the type and extent of cellular damage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The mechanism of freezing injury proceeds through the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Sensible Heat Removal:** As produce temperature drops from ambient to near the freezing point, heat is removed as sensible heat. Cell metabolism slows.

**Stage 2 — Supercooling:** The tissue may supercool 2–4 °C below its equilibrium freezing point without ice forming, due to kinetic barriers to nucleation.

**Stage 3 — Ice Nucleation:** Ice forms preferentially in the **extracellular spaces** because (i) extracellular fluid has lower solute concentration and hence higher freezing point, and (ii) cell membranes act as nucleation barriers.

**Stage 4 — Cellular Dehydration:** Extracellular ice creates a vapor pressure gradient, drawing water out of cells osmotically. Cells undergo plasmolysis.

**Stage 5 — Role of Cooling Rate:**

- **Slow cooling** → Water has time to migrate out → **large extracellular crystals** → cells collapse from dehydration but membranes remain largely intact.
- **Fast cooling** → Water cannot exit fast enough → **intracellular ice forms** → mechanical rupture of organelles and membranes → catastrophic damage.

**Stage 6 — Membrane Damage:** Lipid bilayers undergo liquid-crystalline to gel phase transition, losing selective permeability.

**Stage 7 — Post-thaw:** Damaged cells leak nutrients, enabling rapid microbial colonization. **(7 marks complete)**

**(b)** A fruit pulp has 12% soluble solids (assume equivalent to 12 g sucrose per 100 g water, molecular weight 342 g/mol). Calculate the freezing point depression and discuss its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Soluble solids = 12 g per 100 g water
- Molecular weight of sucrose $M = 342$ g/mol
- $K_f$ for water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**

Molality calculation:

$$m = \frac{12 \text{ g}/342 \text{ g/mol}}{0.1 \text{ kg}} = \frac{0.0351}{0.1} = 0.351 \text{ mol/kg}$$

Freezing point depression (assuming $i = 1$ for sucrose):

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m = 1.86 \times 0.351 = 0.653 \text{ °C}$$

Freezing point of pulp:

$$T_f = 0 - 0.653 = -0.653 \text{ °C}$$

**Engineering Significance:**
- The pulp must be stored **below −0.65 °C** to remain fully frozen.
- Refrigerated storage at 0 °C (above freezing) keeps it liquid but exposed to microbial spoilage.
- This value guides freezer set-point selection; safety margin of **2–3 °C** below this $T_f$ is typically maintained in industrial freezers.
- The depressed freezing point also implies a substantial *unfrozen water fraction* at temperatures just below zero, where microbial activity persists. **(7 marks complete)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the different types of freezing injury. Discuss the biochemical and physiological changes occurring in plant tissues subjected to freezing stress. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Freezing Injury:**

1. **Mechanical Injury:** Direct rupture of cell walls and membranes by expanding ice crystals. The 9% volume expansion on freezing generates stresses exceeding cell wall tensile strength (~0.2–0.5 MPa).

2. **Dehydration/Plasmolysis Injury:** Osmotic loss of intracellular water to extracellular ice. Cells shrivel, lose turgor, and plasmolyze.

3. **Membrane Injury:** Phase transition of membrane lipids from liquid-crystalline to gel state. Membrane-bound enzymes (ATPases, ion pumps) lose function. Electrolyte leakage (measurable via conductivity tests) is a diagnostic indicator.

4. **Metabolic Injury:** Disruption of mitochondrial electron transport, accumulation of toxic intermediates, impaired photosynthesis in leafy tissues.

5. **Oxidative Injury:** Burst of reactive oxygen species (ROS — superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals) upon thawing causes lipid peroxidation, protein oxidation, and DNA damage. **(7 marks complete)**

**(b)** Using Plank's equation, estimate the freezing time for a 6 cm thick slab of fish fillet (density 1050 kg/m³, latent heat 250 kJ/kg, thermal conductivity 1.4 W/(m·K)), initial temperature 15 °C, freezer temperature −25 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Thickness $d = 0.06$ m (half-thickness for Plank's slab = 0.03 m)
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $L_f = 250{,}000$ J/kg
- $k = 1.4$ W/(m·K)
- $\Delta T = 15 - (-25) = 40$ K

Plank's equation:

$$t_{pl} = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot d^2}{k \cdot \Delta T}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pl} &= \frac{(1050)(250000)(0.03)^2}{(1.4)(40)} \\
t_{pl} &= \frac{(1050)(250000)(0.0009)}{56} \\
t_{pl} &= \frac{236{,}250}{56} \\
t_{pl} &= 4218.75 \text{ seconds} \\
t_{pl} &\approx 1.17 \text{ hours} \\
t_{pl} &\approx 70.3 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Note:** This is the *theoretical* minimum freezing time under ideal conditions. In practice, an empirical correction factor of **1.5–2.0** is applied, giving an actual freezing time of ~105–140 minutes. **(7 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **chilling injury** with **freezing injury** — this costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> - When writing Plank's equation, do **NOT** forget to use *half-thickness* for a slab. Many candidates lose 2 marks here.
> - Freezing point depression calculations: always specify the *van't Hoff factor* (i = 1 for sucrose, i = 2 for NaCl). Examiners check this.
> - Diagrams of ice crystal formation (intra vs extracellular) carry bonus marks — always sketch them in 14-mark answers.
> - Numerical units must be consistent (SI). Mark deductions of 0.5 per unit error are common.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Anchor:** Freezing injury = ice crystal damage below tissue freezing point (irreversible).
- **Key Temperature Bands:** Chilling injury (0–13 °C) → Freezing point depression (−0.5 to −3 °C) → Zone of maximum ice damage (−1 to −5 °C) → Safe frozen storage (≤ −18 °C).
- **Volume Expansion on Freezing:** **9%** — this is the root mechanical stress.
- **Latent Heat of Fusion of Water:** **334 kJ/kg** — drives freezing time calculations.
- **Water Activity Below 0 °C:** Follows Clausius-Clapeyron; at −5 °C, $a_w \approx 0.95$.
- **Cooling Rate Matters:** Slow → extracellular crystals; Fast → intracellular crystals (more destructive).
- **Five Injury Types:** Mechanical, Dehydration, Membrane, Metabolic, Oxidative.
- **Key Equations:** $\Delta T_f = K_f m$, Plank's freezing time, Clausius-Clapeyron.
- **Diagnostic Test:** Electrolyte leakage (conductivity) measures membrane damage.
- **Industrial Mitigation:** Rapid freezing, cryoprotectants, temperature preconditioning, cold-tolerant cultivars, controlled thawing.
- **Critical Distinction:** Freezing injury is *physical/structural*; Chilling injury is *physiological/metabolic*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting and Handling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Harvesting and Handling Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Harvesting** in the context of post-harvest technology is defined as the deliberate termination of the biological connection between the plant (or animal) organ intended for consumption and its parent biological system, executed at a precisely determined stage of physiological and/or horticultural maturity to ensure optimum quality, shelf-life, and market value. The harvesting decision is governed by **Maturity Indices** — measurable physical, chemical, physiological, or developmental parameters that signal the transition from growth to senescence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Maturity indices are a set of measurable parameters that indicate the stage at which a commodity is ready for harvest, balancing the competing needs of maximum edible quality against minimum post-harvest deterioration.*

The companion process — **Handling** — encompasses every operation performed on the produce from the moment of detachment from the parent plant up to the point of packaging, including **field handling, curing, cleaning, sorting, grading, pre-cooling, and pre-storage treatments**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Build-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mango Tree Problem:**
> Imagine you have a mango tree bearing 100 fruits. If you pick them when they are rock-hard and green (immature), they will never ripen properly — they will rot, wrinkle, and taste terrible. If you wait until they fall naturally on the ground (over-mature), they will be bruised, fermented, and infested with insects. The *narrow* window of time when the fruit is "just right" — slightly yielding at the stalk end, full yellow colour developing, the shoulder near the stalk turning red — is the **harvest window**. Maturity indices are the "instruments" that tell you *exactly* when to pick. Handling, in turn, is how gently you carry that fragile fruit from the tree to the cold store, and every bump, scratch, or temperature excursion during that journey silently steals days from its marketable life.

## 1.3 Classification of Maturity

| S.No. | Type of Maturity | Definition | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physiological Maturity** | The stage when the organ has completed its development on the plant; maximum growth achieved. | Fruit can ripen normally *after* detachment (climacteric fruits). |
| 2 | **Horticultural Maturity** | The stage when the organ possesses the desired characteristics for its intended use (fresh market, processing, storage). | This is the **harvest maturity** used commercially. |
| 3 | **Commercial / Harvest Maturity** | The stage selected by growers based on market demand, transport distance, and end-use. | May be earlier than horticultural maturity for long-distance shipping. |
| 4 | **Ripening Stage** | The stage when the fruit becomes soft, sweet, aromatic, and ready for consumption. | Climacteric fruits can ripen off-plant; non-climacteric must ripen on-plant. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):** *Physiological maturity* and *Horticultural maturity* are NOT synonymous. A *Cavendish banana* reaches physiological maturity when it is plump and dark green, but its *horticultural maturity* for export is when it is ¾ full (75 % mature, fingers still angular). Picking a banana at full physiological maturity gives a fruit with a shelf life of only 2–3 days.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Recommended Transit Temperature** for most perishables: **0 °C – 13 °C** depending on commodity.
- **Relative Humidity** during handling: **85 % – 95 %** for most fruits and vegetables.
- **Pre-cooling Window** (time from harvest to temperature reduction): target **≤ 6 hours** for highly perishable commodities.
- **Maximum Field Heat Removal Target:** reduce pulp temperature to within **1 °C** of storage temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Maturity Indices

### 2.1.1 Visual / Physical Indices (Non-Destructive)

These are evaluated by the eye or simple hand-held instruments and are the most commonly used indices in the field.

- **Size and Shape:** Commercially fixed by grade standards (e.g., banana bunch fingers ≥ 19 cm for "Extra Class").
- **Surface Colour:** Quantified by comparison with colour charts (e.g., **Hunter L\*a\*b\*** coordinates, or pictorial charts like the **CTIFL Colour Chart** for tomatoes, apples).
- **Surface Morphology:** *Abscission zone* softening at the pedicel (watermelon tendril dries out; apple pedicel separates easily).
- **Firmness / Hardness:** Measured with a **Magness-Taylor Pressure Tester** with an 8 mm (5/16 inch) plunger for apples, pears; reported in **lb-force** or **Newtons (N)**.
- **Specific Gravity:** Used for mango, watermelon, potato.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Visual indices are the "5 Senses Test" — Sight (colour, size, shape), Touch (firmness, weight), Hearing (rattle in watermelon, hollow sound in pumpkin), Smell (aroma of ripe fruit), and Taste (sweetness in peas, chillies).*

### 2.1.2 Chemical Indices (Destructive — Laboratory)

- **Total Soluble Solids (TSS / SSC):** Measured by **refractometer (°Brix)**. Index for grapes (≥ 16 °Brix), mango (≥ 12 °Brix), apple.
- **Titratable Acidity (TA):** Expressed as % citric or malic acid.
- **TSS : TA Ratio (Sugar-Acid Ratio):** Determines flavour balance. Mandarin: 8:1 to 10:1 is harvest-ready.
- **Starch Content:** Determined by the **Starch-Iodine Test** (Lugol's solution). Immature apples stain dark blue-black; mature apples show only a thin ring near the core.
- **Juice Content:** A regulatory index for citrus (e.g., mandarin ≥ 35 %).
- **Oil Content:** Avocado — measured by Soxhlet extraction; harvest at 8–12 % oil.

### 2.1.3 Physiological Indices

- **Respiration Rate:** Measured as **mg CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹** or **mL CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹**. A pre-climacteric minimum indicates harvest readiness for long storage.
- **Ethylene Evolution Rate:** Determined by **Gas Chromatography (GC)**. Pre-climacteric banana: < 0.5 µL C₂H₄ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹.
- **Internal Atmosphere:** O₂ and CO₂ inside the fruit (measured by gas sampling).

### 2.1.4 Morphological / Developmental Indices

- **Days from Flowering / Anthesis (DAA):** Banana — 90–110 DAA; Mango — 110–140 DAA depending on cultivar.
- **Heat Units / Growing Degree Days (GDD):** Cumulative thermal time.
- **Leaf Senescence Patterns:** Onion tops fall over ("tops down"); sugarcane leaves dry.

### 2.1.5 Calculated / Computed Indices

- **Specific Heat Capacity (c):** Heat to remove from field to storage.
- **Heat of Respiration:** Metabolic heat load during transit.

### 2.1.6 Subjective Indices

- *Arabian coffee*: Berries are squeaked between fingers.
- *Durian*: Fallen fruits during midday.

## 2.2 Harvesting Methods — The Decision Matrix

| Method | Principle | Commodities | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Hand-Picking** | Selective, hand-detachment | Apple, mango, citrus, tomato, chilli | Selective maturity, minimal injury | Labour-intensive, slow |
| **Knocking / Shaking** | Mechanical vibration of plant | Nut crops (almond, walnut), olive, coffee | Rapid, low cost | Cannot be selective; injury |
| **Cutting / Pruning** | Shears, knives | Cabbage, lettuce, cauliflower, sugarcane | Clean cut, less tearing | Requires sharp tools |
| **Pulling / Digging** | Manual uprooting | Root crops (carrot, radish, beet) | Complete recovery | Tops break off |
| **Mechanical Harvesters** | Self-propelled straddle harvesters | Tomato, grape, olive, blueberry | High throughput, low cost | High capital, injury-prone |
| **Abscission-Enhancement** | Spraying with *ethephon* (2-chloroethylphosphonic acid) | Cherry, cotton, citrus, olive | Synchronises harvest | Residue concerns |
| **Over-the-Row Harvester** | Straddle type | Berry fruits, dwarf trees | Single-pass operation | Limited to trained orchards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Repeatedly Tested):** The use of *ethephon* is permitted only when the **Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI)** of 7–14 days is strictly observed. Failure to wait results in residue violations under **FSSAI / Codex MRL** standards.

## 2.3 Field Handling — The Critical Operations Chain

1. **Field Heat Removal** (the highest priority immediately after detachment).
2. **Curing** (for root, tuber, and bulb crops — healing of wounds at 28–32 °C and 85–90 % RH for 2–5 days).
3. **Cleaning** (dry brushing, water wash with 50–100 ppm chlorine).
4. **Sorting** (removal of diseased, damaged, undersized produce).
5. **Grading** (Size-grading by weight rings; quality-grading by colour and defect tolerance).
6. **Pre-Cooling** (Room cooling, Forced-air cooling, Hydro-cooling, Vacuum cooling, Ice cooling).
7. **Pre-Storage Treatments** — fungicide (TBZ, Imazalil, Prochloraz), waxing, *curing*, *gum arabic*, *chitosan*, *1-MCP* (1-Methylcyclopropene).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Growing Degree Days (GDD)** | $GDD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{T_{max,i} + T_{min,i}}{2} - T_{base} \right)$ | °C·day | Predict harvest date |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity (SG)** | $SG = \dfrac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$ | dimensionless | Watermelon, mango, potato |
| 3 | **Firmness (Magness-Taylor)** | $F = \dfrac{P_{max}}{\pi r^{2}}$ | N or lb/in² | Apple, pear, kiwi |
| 4 | **Respiratory Heat (RH)** | $RH = R_{CO_2} \times 10.7$ | J kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ | Cooling load calculation |
| 5 | **Pre-cooling Time Constant** | $t = \rho \, c \, V \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{T_{i} - T_{a}}{T_{f} - T_{a}} \right) / (hA)$ | h | Cool-room design |
| 6 | **Bruise Resistance (B)** | $B = k \cdot \sigma^{n}$ (viscoelastic) | J | Mechanical damage |
| 7 | **Chlorine Demand (C)** | $C_{free} = C_{applied} - C_{consumed}$ | ppm | Wash-water sanitation |
| 8 | **Surface Area to Volume (SA:V)** | $SA/V = 3/r$ (sphere) | m⁻¹ | Transpiration rate |
| 9 | **Starch-to-Sugar Conversion (ΔS)** | $\Delta S = S_{mature} - S_{harvest}$ | % | Apple maturity |
| 10 | **Field Heat (Q)** | $Q = m \, c_{p} \, (T_{field} - T_{storage})$ | kJ | Cooling requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference (verifiable):** $T_{base}$ for cool-season crops is typically **4.4 °C (40 °F)**; for warm-season crops **10 °C (50 °F)**. The respiratory quotient 10.7 J per mL CO₂ is derived from the gross heat of combustion of glucose.

## 2.5 Why This System Matters in Food Engineering

The harvesting decision directly determines the **postharvest life (PHL)** of a commodity. Picking one day early can extend the marketable life of a banana by **3–5 days** under optimal storage. Conversely, picking at the wrong stage can result in **20–50 % losses** in transit. From a thermodynamic standpoint, every kilogram of fruit brought in from the field carries **field heat (Q)** that must be removed before storage — failure to do so accelerates respiration and senescence.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Growing Degree Days (GDD) and Harvest Prediction

The concept of GDD integrates temperature over the growth period to predict the day of physiological maturity.

**Step 1 — Define Daily Mean Temperature (T_mean).**
For any day *i*, the mean of the maximum and minimum field temperature is recorded:

$$T_{mean,i} = \frac{T_{max,i} + T_{min,i}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Base Temperature.**
A base (developmental zero) temperature is the minimum at which growth proceeds. For cool-season crops (apple, peach, wheat) $T_{base} = 4.4\ \text{°C}$. For warm-season crops (tomato, mango, rice) $T_{base} = 10\ \text{°C}$.

$$GDD_{i} = T_{mean,i} - T_{base}$$

**Step 3 — Bound the Daily GDD.**
If $GDD_{i} < 0$, set it to zero (no negative heat units accumulated). If $T_{max} > T_{upper}$ (typically 30 °C for cool-season, 35 °C for warm-season), $T_{max}$ is capped at $T_{upper}$ to avoid over-counting on heat-stressed days.

$$GDD_{i}^{bounded} = \max\!\left(0,\; \min\!\left(T_{upper},\, T_{max,i}\right) + T_{min,i} - T_{base}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Sum Daily GDD Values.**

$$GDD_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} GDD_{i}^{bounded}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Cultivar-Specific Requirement.**
Each cultivar has a published GDD requirement from anthesis to harvest. Example: 'Alphonso' mango ≈ 2 500 °C·days, 'Kesar' mango ≈ 2 700 °C·days from flowering.

$$\text{If } GDD_{total} \ge GDD_{required} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Harvest Window Open}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** GDD is the basis of all commercial phenology models in **DSSAT**, **CROPGRO**, and the **Indian Meteorological Department's** agricultural advisories. It allows harvest forecasting **5–10 days in advance**, critical for labour and cold-store booking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Gravity as a Maturity Index

Specific gravity is the ratio of the mass of a body to the mass of an equal volume of water. For fruit, it is measured by **Archimedes' principle**.

**Step 1 — Weigh the Fruit in Air.**

$$W_{air} = m \cdot g \quad (\text{where } m = \text{mass in kg})$$

**Step 2 — Weigh the Fruit Submerged in Water.**

$$W_{water} = m_{sub} \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Apply Archimedes' Principle.**
The buoyant force equals the weight of water displaced. Therefore the *apparent loss of weight in water* equals the weight of water displaced, which equals the weight of an equal volume of water.

$$V_{fruit} = \frac{W_{air} - W_{water}}{\rho_{water} \cdot g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Specific Gravity.**

$$SG = \frac{W_{air}}{W_{air} - W_{water}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Reading.**

| Crop | SG at Harvest | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Watermelon | 0.95 – 0.99 | Ripe, sweet, juicy |
| Mango (Alphonso) | 1.00 – 1.04 | Tree-ripe |
| Potato (mature) | 1.08 – 1.10 | Starch-rich, low sugar |
| Apple | 0.80 – 0.85 | Eating ripe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Pattern):** A mango weighs 250 g in air and 12 g when submerged in water.
> $SG = 250 \div (250 - 12) = 250 \div 238 = 1.050$.
> This is in the tree-ripe range; immediate harvest recommended.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Field Heat and Pre-Cooling Load

When fruit enters the cold store, its pulp temperature $T_{pulp}$ must be lowered to the storage temperature $T_{storage}$. The heat to be extracted is the **Field Heat**.

**Step 1 — Write the Sensible Heat Equation.**

$$Q = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Insert the Values.**
For a 1 tonne batch of mango at $T_{pulp} = 30\ \text{°C}$ to be cooled to $T_{storage} = 13\ \text{°C}$:

$$Q = 1000\ \text{kg} \times 3.6\ \text{kJ/(kg·°C)} \times (30 - 13)$$

$$Q = 1000 \times 3.6 \times 17$$

$$Q = 61\,200\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh for Refrigeration Sizing.**

$$Q = \frac{61\,200}{3600} = 17.0\ \text{kWh of cooling}$$

**Step 4 — Add Respiration Heat.**
Respiration produces additional heat, e.g., for mango at 13 °C: $R_{CO_2} = 40\ \text{mL CO}_2 \text{ kg}^{-1} \text{ h}^{-1}$.

$$RH = 40 \times 10.7 = 428\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

For 24 h, for 1 tonne:

$$RH_{total} = 428 \times 1000 \times 24 / 1000 = 10\,272\ \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Total Cooling Load.**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{field} + RH_{total} = 61\,200 + 10\,272 = 71\,472\ \text{kJ} \approx 19.85\ \text{kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **actual design load** for the cold-room cooling coil. Ignoring respiration heat is the **#1 engineering mistake** in cold-store design for perishables.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Starch-Iodine Index (Apple Maturity)

**Step 1 — Cut the apple transversely at the equator.**
**Step 2 — Dip the cut surface in 1 % Lugol's iodine solution (I₂-KI) for 30 s.**
**Step 3 — Rinse in water; observe the stained pattern.**

The starch that has *not yet* converted to sugar stains **dark blue-black**. Sugar-rich tissue does not stain.

**Step 4 — Compare with the Cornell Starch-Iodine Chart (1–8 scale).**

| Chart Score | Pattern | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 % black (whole surface dark) | Very immature, high starch |
| 4 | 50 % black (clear core, dark cortex) | Climacteric minimum; harvest for long storage |
| 6 | 25 % black (mostly clear) | Tree-ripe, eat immediately |
| 8 | 0 % black (no staining) | Over-mature, mealy, low acid |

**Step 5 — Decide Harvest Action.**
For long-term CA storage, harvest at **Chart 3–4**. For immediate market, **Chart 5–6**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The starch-iodine index is **destructive** — a single apple per batch is consumed. For non-destructive alternatives, the **DA-Meter** (Difference in Absorbance meter) is used, which measures $A_{670} - A_{720}$ to estimate chlorophyll degradation non-destructively.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Matching Method to Commodity

| Commodity | Primary Maturity Index | Secondary Index | Harvest Method | Critical Curing / Pre-Cooling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Apple (Malus)** | Starch-iodine, firmness, background colour | Days from full bloom, GDD | Hand-pick with stem attached | Forced-air cool within 24 h |
| **Banana (Musa)** | Angularity, finger width, days from bunch emergence | TSS, peel colour | Hand-cut whole bunch | Hydro-cool or room-cool to 13 °C |
| **Mango (Mangifera)** | Shoulder colour, specific gravity, lenticel colour | DAA, TSS, latex flow | Hand-pick with 2–3 cm stalk | Forced-air cool to 12–13 °C |
| **Tomato (Solanum)** | Colour chart (green→red), size, firmness | Internal ethylene, pH | Hand-pick or mechanical harvester | Room cool to 12 °C (ripe) |
| **Citrus (Citrus)** | TSS : TA ratio, juice %, rind colour | Size, oil gland appearance | Hand-clip with clippers | No pre-cooling; store at 5–8 °C |
| **Potato (Solanum)** | Vine senescence, specific gravity | Skin-set, dry matter | Mechanical digger | Cure at 15 °C, 95 % RH for 10–14 days |
| **Onion (Allium)** | Tops fall over, neck softening | Skin tightness, pungency | Hand-pull or undercutting | Field-cure 2–3 days in windrow |
| **Cucumber (Cucumis)** | Size (length), firmness, colour | Tendril dryness | Hand-pick | Hydro-cool to 10 °C |

---

## 3.6 Decision Tree: When to Harvest

The decision is **multi-criteria**, never single-parameter. A robust pre-harvest checklist should include:

1. Confirm at least **two** maturity indices are in the *harvest range* simultaneously.
2. Cross-check with **local market price** (commercial override).
3. Verify **weather forecast** — never harvest in rain.
4. Confirm **labour and packing-house readiness** — no fruit should wait in the field.
5. Verify **pre-cooling facility is available and functional**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A *single-index* harvest decision is **3-mark deduction territory** in 14-mark questions. Always justify with multiple indices and explain the underlying biochemistry (e.g., starch hydrolysis, chlorophyll degradation, cell-wall solubilisation).
