# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Maturity Index. Give any four maturity indices used for the harvest of fruits.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1(a)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Maturity Index:** A maturity index is a measurable physical, chemical, physiological, or developmental parameter that indicates the stage at which a fruit or vegetable is ready for harvest, balancing optimum edible quality against minimum post-harvest deterioration.

**Four Major Maturity Indices for Fruits:**

1. **Surface Colour:** Comparison with a standard colour chart (e.g., CTIFL chart for tomato, USDA chart for mango). Reflects the degradation of chlorophyll and synthesis of carotenoids / anthocyanins.
2. **Firmness:** Measured by a Magness-Taylor pressure tester with an 8 mm plunger; reported in Newtons (N) or pounds-force (lb). E.g., apple 70–80 N for long storage.
3. **Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix):** Measured by a hand-held refractometer. E.g., grape ≥ 16 °Brix, mango ≥ 12 °Brix.
4. **Specific Gravity:** Determined by water displacement (Archimedes' principle). E.g., watermelon SG 0.95–0.99 indicates ripe fruit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition 1 mark + 4 indices × 0.5 mark = 2 marks = **3 marks total**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between physiological maturity and horticultural maturity. Give one example.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Q1(b)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Physiological Maturity | Horticultural Maturity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The stage at which an organ has completed its natural growth and development on the parent plant. | The stage at which the organ possesses the desired attributes for its intended use (fresh market, processing, storage). |
| **Timing** | Always earlier or equal to horticultural maturity. | Later than or equal to physiological maturity. |
| **Ripening** | Fruit is *capable* of ripening, but may not be *ready* to eat. | Fruit may be either ready to eat or still firm for transport. |
| **Determination** | Internal markers (seed maturity, dry matter accumulation). | External markers (size, colour, TSS). |
| **Example** | Banana: physiologically mature when fingers are plump and angular but peel is dark green. | Banana: horticulturally mature for export at ¾ full (75 % mature), still firm for sea-shipment. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Use a comparison table for crisp presentation. The 3-mark answer is **Definition 0.5 + 4 rows × 0.5 = 2.5 → 3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each 14-mark question is split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different maturity indices used for the harvest of fruits and vegetables. Classify them with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2 000 kg batch of 'Alphonso' mangoes is harvested at a pulp temperature of 32 °C. The mangoes are to be pre-cooled to a storage temperature of 13 °C using a forced-air cooler. Calculate:
- (i) The total field heat to be removed in kJ and kWh.
- (ii) The additional respiratory heat generated over 24 hours, given the respiration rate of mango at 13 °C is 35 mL CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹.
- (iii) The total refrigeration load in kWh.
Take $c_p$ of mango = 3.6 kJ/(kg·°C); 1 kWh = 3 600 kJ; respiratory quotient = 10.7 J per mL CO₂. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer A(a) — Maturity Indices

Maturity indices are classified into **six major categories**:

1. **Visual / Physical Indices:** Colour, size, shape, surface morphology, abscission zone. *Example:* Tomato colour chart (USDA), apple background colour.

2. **Firmness-Based:** Magness-Taylor test, non-destructive acoustic firmness. *Example:* Apple 70–80 N for CA storage, kiwi 25 N for fresh market.

3. **Chemical Indices:** TSS, TA, TSS:TA ratio, starch (iodine test), juice %, oil content. *Example:* Grape ≥ 16 °Brix; mango oil 8–12 %.

4. **Physiological Indices:** Respiration rate, internal ethylene, internal atmosphere. *Example:* Pre-climacteric banana < 0.5 µL C₂H₄ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹.

5. **Morphological / Developmental:** Days from flowering, leaf senescence, abscission, GDD. *Example:* Banana 90–110 DAA; onion tops falling over.

6. **Calculated / Subjective:** Specific gravity, acoustic resonance (watermelon hollow sound), heat units.

**The Maturity Index Decision:** Harvest decision requires at least **two corroborating indices** to avoid a single-parameter error. Climacteric fruits are picked at *physiological* maturity (firm, green) to allow post-harvest ripening, while non-climacteric fruits are picked at *horticultural* maturity (full ripe on the plant).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Classification table 3 + 6 examples 2 + decision rule 2 = **7 marks**.

### Model Answer A(b) — Engineering Calculation

**Given Data:**
$m = 2\,000\ \text{kg};\; T_{pulp} = 32\ \text{°C};\; T_{storage} = 13\ \text{°C};\; c_p = 3.6\ \text{kJ/(kg·°C)};\; R_{CO_2} = 35\ \text{mL/kg/h};\;$ time = 24 h; $Q_{R} = 10.7\ \text{J/mL CO}_2$.

**Step 1 — Field Heat.**

$$\Delta T = 32 - 13 = 19\ \text{°C}$$

$$Q_{field} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2\,000 \times 3.6 \times 19 = 136\,800\ \text{kJ}$$

Converting to kWh:

$$Q_{field} = \frac{136\,800}{3\,600} = 38.0\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Respiration Heat in kJ per kg per 24 h.**

$$RH = 35 \times 10.7\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg·h}} = 374.5\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg·h}}$$

Over 24 h:

$$RH_{per\ kg} = 374.5 \times 24 = 8\,988\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} = 8.988\ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}$$

For 2 000 kg over 24 h:

$$RH_{total} = 8.988 \times 2\,000 = 17\,976\ \text{kJ}$$

Converting to kWh:

$$RH_{total} = \frac{17\,976}{3\,600} = 4.99\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Total Refrigeration Load.**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{field} + RH_{total} = 38.0 + 4.99 = 42.99\ \text{kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Formula + Q_field 2 + Q_field in kWh 1 + Respiration 2 + Conversion 1 + Total 1 = **7 marks**. Show every step; a missing unit is 0.5-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the various methods of harvesting fruits and vegetables. Compare manual, mechanical, and chemical (ethephon-induced) harvesting with reference to specific commodities. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is curing? Explain the curing process for potato and onion with reference to temperature, relative humidity, duration, and physiological changes during curing. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer B(a) — Harvesting Methods

Harvesting methods are classified into **three principal categories**:

1. **Manual Harvesting:** Hand-picking, hand-cutting, pulling, digging. The most labour-intensive but the most *selective*. Used for apple, mango, tomato, chilli, cabbage, cauliflower, root crops. Equipment: shears, secateurs, ladders, picking bags, picking poles, picking bags with shoulder straps.

2. **Mechanical Harvesting:** Over-the-row harvesters (straddle type), shaking machines (olive, nut, coffee), self-propelled combine harvesters (tomato, grape, blueberry). *Principle:* Mechanical vibration, suction, combing, or straddle-belt. Used when labour is scarce and capital is available.

3. **Chemical Harvesting:** Application of *abscission-enhancing* chemicals such as **Ethephon (2-chloroethylphosphonic acid)**, *cycloheximide*, or *methyl jasmonate*. Ethephon releases ethylene inside the plant tissue, accelerating the formation of the **abscission layer** at the pedicel-fruit junction. Used for cherry, olive, citrus, cotton, and certain grape cultivars.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Manual | Mechanical | Chemical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selectivity | High | Low | Medium |
| Capital cost | Low | High | Low |
| Injury risk | Low | High | Medium |
| Throughput | Low | High | High |
| Residue concern | None | None | Yes (PHI mandatory) |
| Commodities | Apple, mango, tomato | Tomato, grape, olive | Cherry, olive, citrus |

### Model Answer B(b) — Curing of Potato and Onion

**Definition:** Curing is the *controlled, short-duration* post-harvest treatment of root, tuber, and bulb crops at elevated temperature and high relative humidity to promote **wound healing** (suberisation and lignification of damaged cells), thereby reducing water loss and pathogen entry.

**Curing of Potato (Solanum tuberosum):**
- **Temperature:** 15–20 °C
- **Relative Humidity:** 90–95 %
- **Duration:** 10–14 days
- **Physiological changes:** Suberisation of wounded periderm cells; deposition of **suberin** and **lignin**; formation of a *wound periderm* below the damaged layer. Reduces transpiration by 50–80 %.

**Curing of Onion (Allium cepa):**
- **Temperature:** 28–32 °C (field-curing) or 35–45 °C (artificial curing in bulk-storage driers)
- **Relative Humidity:** 70–80 % for field curing; 60 % for artificial curing
- **Duration:** 2–5 days (field) or up to 48 h (artificial)
- **Physiological changes:** Closure of the *neck*; drying of the outer scales; colour development; conversion of pungent precursors (S-alk(en)yl-L-cysteine sulphoxides) to volatile sulphur compounds; formation of protective flavonoid pigments in the outer dry scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Split (B-b):** Definition 1 + Potato 3 + Onion 3 = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Single-index harvest decision** in long answers (–2 marks). Always cite *at least two* indices.
> 2. **Conflating physiological and horticultural maturity** in a 3-mark question (–1 mark).
> 3. **Forgetting units** in cooling-load calculations (–0.5 per missing unit).
> 4. **Skipping the respiration heat** in cold-room design (–2 marks). It is the *most common* engineering error in the paper.
> 5. **Writing "TSS" without specifying °Brix** (–0.5 mark). Always quantify.
> 6. **Not mentioning PHI for ethephon** in chemical harvesting (–1 mark).
> 7. **Ignoring the curing temperature differential** between potato (15–20 °C) and onion (28–32 °C) (–1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Maturity** is multi-parameter. Always use **≥ 2 corroborating indices** before triggering harvest.
- **Physiological maturity ≠ Horticultural maturity.** Climacteric fruits are picked at physiological maturity; non-climacteric at horticultural.
- The **six categories of maturity indices:** Visual, Physical (firmness, SG), Chemical (TSS, TA, starch, oil), Physiological (respiration, ethylene), Morphological (DAA, leaf senescence), Computed (GDD, heat units).
- **Magness-Taylor Pressure Tester** with 8 mm plunger is the standard firmness instrument.
- **Starch-Iodine Test** is the gold standard for apple maturity (Cornell Chart 1–8).
- **°Brix** is measured with a **refractometer** (hand-held or digital).
- **Ethephon** releases ethylene in vivo; PHI of 7–14 days must be observed.
- **Field heat** formula: $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Respiration heat** = $R_{CO_2} \times 10.7$ J (mL CO₂)⁻¹.
- **Curing** is a *wound-healing* process — potato 15–20 °C, onion 28–32 °C — **not** a *drying* process.
- **Pre-cooling window:** ≤ 6 h for highly perishable produce; target within **1 °C** of storage temperature.
- **Five pre-cooling methods:** Room, Forced-air, Hydro, Vacuum, Package icing. *Match method to commodity.*
- **Grading ≠ Sorting.** Sorting = removing defects; Grading = classifying by size/quality.
- **Specific gravity** uses Archimedes' principle: $SG = W_{air} \div (W_{air} - W_{water})$.
- **Watermelon** harvest: hollow sound + tendril dryness + SG 0.95–0.99.
- **Mango** harvest: shoulder colour + SG + 2–3 cm stalk retained.
- **Banana** harvest: ¾ full (angularity preserved) + fingers still angular + cut whole bunch.
- **Citrus** harvest: TSS:TA ratio + juice % + rind colour + clip with secateurs.
- **Potato** harvest: skin-set + vine senescence + SG 1.08–1.10.
- **Onion** harvest: tops fall over + neck softening + field-cure in windrow.
- **1-MCP (1-Methylcyclopropene)** blocks ethylene receptors and extends climacteric fruit shelf life dramatically.
- **Waxing** reduces transpiration, improves gloss, and is a carrier for fungicides (e.g., Imazalil in citrus wax).
- **Chlorine in wash water:** 50–100 ppm free chlorine; pH 6.5–7.5 for maximum HOCl efficacy.
- **Cold chain integrity:** Harvest → Pre-cool → Cold store → Refrigerated truck → Retail chill → Consumer fridge. **No break** in the chain is permitted.
- **FSSAI / Codex MRL** must be respected for every post-harvest chemical application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Final Tip:** When answering any maturity or harvesting question, always use the formula *Maturity Index → Decision Rule → Method → Justification*. Examiners reward **structured, justified, multi-parameter** answers. A one-line answer to a 14-mark question will fetch at most **2–3 marks**. Plan the answer in 3–4 sub-headings, draw a small comparison table, and conclude with a real-world application or a current research reference (e.g., *1-MCP, MAP, nanotechnology-based coatings*). This is the difference between a 7-mark answer and a 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coatings and Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coatings and Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Post-Harvest Coatings and Treatments** refer to the deliberate application of protective materials, chemicals, or physical processes on the surface of fresh fruits, vegetables, and other perishable produce to extend shelf life, reduce physiological deterioration, suppress microbial spoilage, and enhance visual/handling quality after harvest.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (Course PEFTT754), the topic broadly covers:

1. **Edible Surface Coatings** – thin, continuous layers of edible material (waxes, lipids, proteins, polysaccharides, or composites) applied directly on produce surfaces to act as moisture barriers, gas exchange modulators, and carriers of active ingredients.
2. **Curing Treatments** – controlled post-harvest holding under specific temperature and relative humidity conditions to heal wounds, develop a protective periderm, and reduce water loss (typical for root/tuber crops and citrus).
3. **Chemical Treatments** – application of fungicides (e.g., imazalil, thiabendazole), growth regulators, sanitizers (chlorine, ozone), or inhibitors of ethylene action (1-MCP).
4. **Physical Treatments** – hot water treatment (HWT), hot air treatment, vapour heat treatment (VHT), cold treatment, irradiation, UV-C, and edible wax emulsions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 4 of PEFTT754 explicitly tests a student's ability to **justify the choice of a coating or treatment** for a given commodity, **explain barrier properties**, and **interpret mass transfer equations** governing gas/moisture exchange through the coating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine your fresh apple as a tiny **living factory** still breathing after harvest. It loses water through its skin (transpiration), breathes in O₂ and exhales CO₂ (respiration), and is constantly under attack by fungal spores that lie in wait like invaders. A coating is essentially **a tailor-made raincoat + gas-mask combination** wrapped around this factory:

- The **raincoat function** (barrier property) slows water loss so the apple does not shrivel.
- The **gas-mask function** (selective permeability) lets just enough O₂ in and CO₂ out — like a controlled breathing filter — to keep internal atmosphere balanced and slow ripening.
- Some advanced coatings even carry **"bodyguards"** — antimicrobials or antioxidants — embedded inside the film to fight spoilage.

**Curing**, in contrast, is like giving the produce **time to "scab" over a wound** — like when you cut your finger and a protective crust forms before healing. Curing lets the plant build its own natural barrier (suberized periderm) so it can defend itself.

**Chemical/Physical treatments** are like sending the produce through a **disinfection checkpoint** — killing hidden microbes, knocking out insect eggs, or deactivating ripening hormones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Coating thickness: **typically 1–10 µm (micrometers)**
> - Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR): measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**
> - O₂ Permeability: **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**
> - Curing temperature range: **25–35 °C** (commodity dependent)
> - Hot Water Treatment (HWT) range: **46–55 °C** for 3–20 minutes
> - 1-MCP dosage: **0.5–1.0 µL·L⁻¹ (ppm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Coatings (Structured Logic)

### A. Based on Composition

1. **Wax Coatings (Hydrophobic)**
   - Natural waxes: beeswax, carnauba wax, candelilla wax, shellac.
   - Paraffin wax (mineral origin).
   - Mechanism: Creates a thick hydrophobic barrier → drastically reduces transpiration.
   - Commodity examples: apples, citrus, cucumbers, plums, tomatoes.

2. **Lipid-Based Coatings**
   - Fatty acids, fatty alcohols, acylglycerols, sucrose polyesters.
   - Excellent water vapour barrier but poor gas barrier; brittle if used alone.

3. **Resin / Shellac Coatings**
   - Shellac (secreted by lac insect) → glossy coating on apples; high CO₂ retention.

4. **Polysaccharide-Based Coatings (Hydrophilic)**
   - Chitosan, alginate, pectin, starch, cellulose derivatives (CMC, HPMC, MC).
   - Good gas barrier (O₂, CO₂) but poor moisture barrier.
   - Often used in combination with lipids (composite films).

5. **Protein-Based Coatings**
   - Whey protein, casein, soy protein, wheat gluten, gelatin.
   - Excellent O₂ barrier, moderate moisture barrier, good film-forming.

6. **Composite (Multi-Component) Coatings**
   - Combine lipid + polysaccharide/protein → leverage strengths of each.
   - Most commercially successful formulations.

### B. Based on Function

| Coating Type | Primary Function | Typical Commodity |
|---|---|---|
| Wax emulsion | Gloss + water loss reduction | Apple, citrus, plum |
| Chitosan film | Antimicrobial + gas barrier | Strawberry, mango |
| Alginate gel | Moisture retention + firmness | Cut lettuce, peach |
| Shellac | High-gloss + CO₂ retention | Apple |
| Lipid + starch composite | Balanced barrier | Cheese, fresh-cut fruit |

## 2.2 Functional Requirements of an Ideal Coating

A coating must satisfy the following for the KTU-level design analysis:

1. **Good adhesion** to the waxy epicuticle of the produce.
2. **Acceptable water vapour barrier** (low WVTR).
3. **Selective gas permeability** to maintain internal modified atmosphere.
4. **Non-toxic, food-grade, biodegradable.**
5. **Mechanical integrity** (flexible, not brittle).
6. **Carrier capability** for active agents (antimicrobials, antioxidants, nutraceuticals).

## 2.3 Why Coatings Work — The "How" Behind Each Step

1. **Barrier to Water Vapour:** The coating layer adds a tortuous path for water molecules; diffusion lengthens → transpiration rate drops.
2. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) Effect:** The coating alters internal O₂ and CO₂ partial pressures → respiration rate decreases → ethylene production suppressed → ripening slows.
3. **Mechanical Protection:** Resists micro-cracks and punctures that would otherwise open infection routes.
4. **Carrier Function:** Lipophilic antimicrobials (e.g., natamycin) or antioxidants (e.g., BHT, ascorbic acid) can be embedded.
5. **Surface Sterilization Synergy:** Coatings prevent re-contamination after sanitization.

## 2.4 Curing Treatments — Deep Logic

**Curing** is commodity-specific:

- **Potato / Sweet Potato:** 25–30 °C, 85–95% RH for 4–10 days → induces **suberization** (suberin deposition) and **wound periderm** formation. Reduces water loss by 50–70% during subsequent storage.
- **Onion / Garlic:** 30–45 °C, 60–75% RH for 12–48 h → closes neck, seals leaves.
- **Citrus:** Curing at 30 °C, 90–95% RH for 2–3 days after degreening reduces stem-end rind breakdown.
- **Mango (hot water cure):** 46–55 °C immersion for 3–15 min → kills anthracnose spores (Colletotrichum gloeosporioides)潜伏 and forms a chemical barrier on lenticels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Curing vs. Hot Water Treatment — A Subtle Distinction Examiners Love to Test:**
> *Curing* = physiological wound healing, RH-controlled, longer duration.
> *Hot Water Treatment (HWT)* = disinfestation/ pathogen kill, precisely temperature-timed, minutes to hours.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> **Note on Notation:** To preserve markdown table integrity, the symbol $\vert$ is used in place of the vertical pipe character wherever absolute-value expressions or modulus signs appear.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-state water vapour flux through coating | $J_w = \dfrac{P_w \cdot (p_{i} - p_{s})}{L}$ | $J_w$ = flux (g·m⁻²·s⁻¹); $P_w$ = permeability (g·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹); $L$ = coating thickness (m); $p_i, p_s$ = internal/surface partial pressure (Pa) | Fick's First Law analogue |
| 2 | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = molar flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹); $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²·s⁻¹); $C$ = concentration (mol·m⁻³) | Drives gas transport through coating |
| 3 | Respiration Rate (Clausen & Kader) | $r_{O_2} = k \cdot [O_2]^n$ | $r_{O_2}$ = O₂ consumption; $k$ = rate constant; $n$ = order (typically 1) | Empirical kinetic model |
| 4 | Permeability coefficient | $P = D \cdot S$ | $D$ = diffusivity; $S$ = solubility (m³·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹) | $P$ in mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ |
| 5 | Selectivity ratio (ideal coating) | $\alpha = P_{O_2} / P_{CO_2}$ | Dimensionless | Ideal fruit coating: $\alpha \approx 1$ to $5$ |
| 6 | Coating effectiveness | $E = \dfrac{WL_{uncoated} - WL_{coated}}{WL_{uncoated}} \times 100$ | $E$ = % effectiveness; $WL$ = weight loss | Higher $E$ = better moisture barrier |
| 7 | Decimal reduction time (D-value) | $\log(N/N_0) = -t/D$ | $N$ = survivors; $N_0$ = initial; $t$ = time (min); $D$ = D-value (min) | Used in HWT kill kinetics |
| 8 | Arrhenius (treatment lethality) | $k = k_0 \cdot \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | $E_a$ = activation energy (J·mol⁻¹); $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $T$ = K | Predicts rate of microbial/spoilage kinetics |
| 9 | Internal atmosphere steady-state | $r_{O_2} \cdot V = P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (p_{O_2,ext} - p_{O_2,int}) / L$ | $V$ = free volume; $A$ = surface area | Balance of respiration vs. permeation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fresh-Cut Produce Industry:** Alginate + calcium coatings form an instant gel on cut surfaces → prevents leakage, browning, and microbial ingress.
- **Citrus Packing Houses:** Waxing line applies 1–2 % solids carnauba-shellac emulsion → reduces weight loss from **5 % to <1 %** over 4–6 weeks.
- **Export Quarantine:** Vapour heat treatment at **47 °C core temperature for fruit fly disinfestation** is mandatory under many international phytosanitary protocols (e.g., USDA-APHIS, Japan MAFF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Water Vapour Transmission Through an Edible Coating

We model a coated fruit as a sphere of internal water vapour partial pressure $p_i$ surrounded by a coating of uniform thickness $L$, with the external ambient partial pressure $p_s < p_i$. Steady state is assumed.

**Step 1 — Start with Fick's First Law of Diffusion.**

For one-dimensional steady-state diffusion through a thin film of thickness $L$:

$$J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

Here $C$ is the concentration of water vapour (mol·m⁻³), $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, and $x$ is the direction of transport.

**Step 2 — Apply Henry's Law to convert concentration to partial pressure.**

Assuming the coating obeys Henry-type sorption:

$$C = S \cdot p$$

where $S$ is the solubility (mol·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹) and $p$ is the local partial pressure.

**Step 3 — Combine the two relations.**

Substituting $C = S \cdot p$ into Fick's law:

$$J = -D \cdot S \cdot \dfrac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the permeability coefficient $P = D \cdot S$.**

$$J = -P \cdot \dfrac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate across the coating thickness** from $x = 0$ (inner surface, pressure $p_i$) to $x = L$ (outer surface, pressure $p_s$).

$$\int_{p_i}^{p_s} dp = -\dfrac{J}{P} \cdot \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

The left-hand side becomes $(p_s - p_i)$ and the right-hand side becomes $-\dfrac{J \cdot L}{P}$. So:

$$p_s - p_i = -\dfrac{J \cdot L}{P}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $J$.**

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ and rearranging:

$$J = \dfrac{P \cdot (p_i - p_s)}{L}$$

**Step 7 — Express the flux in mass units (water vapour).**

Multiplying by the molar mass of water $M_w = 18.015$ g·mol⁻¹:

$$J_w = \dfrac{P \cdot M_w \cdot (p_i - p_s)}{L}$$

This is the **water vapour transmission rate (WVTR)** in g·m⁻²·s⁻¹ when the units of $P$ are in mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹.

**Physical Interpretation:** As coating thickness $L$ increases or permeability $P$ decreases, the water vapour flux drops — the fruit dehydrates more slowly. This justifies the design rule that **doubling coating thickness halves the steady-state weight loss** (provided the coating remains defect-free).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A wax coating of thickness $L = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m and permeability $P = 1.2 \times 10^{-13}$ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ is applied to an apple. The internal water vapour partial pressure at the surface is $p_i = 3160$ Pa (corresponding to ≈ 25 °C saturated) and the storage room is at $p_s = 1200$ Pa. Calculate the steady-state water vapour flux in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Pressure gradient.**

$$\Delta p = p_i - p_s = 3160 - 1200 = 1960 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Molar flux using derived equation.**

$$J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-13} \times 1960}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$J = \dfrac{2.352 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.704 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass flux.**

$$J_w = J \cdot M_w = 4.704 \times 10^{-5} \times 18.015 = 8.474 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert seconds to days.**

$$J_{w,day} = 8.474 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 = 73.21 \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·day}^{-1}$$

**Answer:** $J_w \approx 73.2$ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Cross-Check (model answer key):** Uncoated apples typically lose water at 200–400 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. The wax coating reduces this by a factor of **3–5×** — consistent with commercial data. Always quote both the *uncoated* baseline and the *coated* value to demonstrate the barrier benefit.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Coating vs. Treatment

| Parameter | Edible Coating | Curing | Hot Water Treatment | Chemical Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary target | Transpiration, gas exchange | Wound healing | Pathogen kill, disinfestation | Microbial load, ripening control |
| Temperature | Ambient | 25–45 °C | 46–55 °C | Ambient |
| Duration | Seconds (dipping) | Days | Minutes | Seconds–minutes |
| Risk of injury | Very low | Low | Moderate (heat injury) | Chemical residue |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Low | Medium | Low–Medium |
| Effect on shelf life | +30–100 % | +50–200 % | +50–150 % | +30–80 % |
| Regulatory status | Food-grade | None | Phytosanitary-approved | MRL-based regulation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Function of a Coating on a Fruit Surface

The Mermaid block below maps the **interactions** between the coating layer, the produce surface, and the surrounding environment. This satisfies the diagram requirement using a clean **block-level functional architecture** rather than a complex physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Produce Surface] --> B[Pre-Treatment: Wash and Sanitize]
    B --> C[Coating Formulation Tank]
    C --> D[Dipping or Spraying Station]
    D --> E[Coated Produce]
    E --> F[Internal Atmosphere Modulation]
    E --> G[Moisture Barrier Function]
    E --> H[Active Agent Release]
    F --> I[Reduced Respiration Rate]
    F --> J[Suppressed Ethylene Synthesis]
    G --> K[Lower Weight Loss]
    H --> L[Antimicrobial Protection]
    I --> M[Extended Shelf Life]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Quality and Marketability]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A → B → C → D** represent the **processing line**; this is the only path by which coating material is delivered.
- The node **E (Coated Produce)** is the central hub from which three parallel **barrier functions** diverge: gas modulation (F), moisture barrier (G), and active agent release (H).
- These three branches feed into the **shelf-life node (M)** and ultimately to the **marketability node (N)**.

## 4.2 Curing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Harvested Commodity] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Healing Chamber 25-35 C, 85-95 percent RH]
    C --> D[Suberin and Lignin Deposition]
    D --> E[Periderm Formation]
    E --> F[Storage at 10-15 C, 85 percent RH]
    F --> G[Reduced Weight Loss in Storage]
    F --> H[Reduced Microbial Entry]
    G --> I[Extended Marketable Life]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selection of Treatment by Commodity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Commodity] --> Q1{Is the produce a citrus or apple?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Wax[Apply Wax Emulsion: Carnauba or Shellac]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a berry or fresh cut?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Chito[Chitosan or Alginate Edible Coating]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it a tuber or root?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Cure[Apply Curing 4-10 days]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it mango or papaya for export?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| HWT[Apply Hot Water Treatment 46-55 C]
    Q4 -->|No| Chem[Apply Chemical Treatment: Fungicide or 1-MCP]
```

## 4.4 Mass Transfer Schematic — Through-Coating Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inside Produce: High Water Vapour Pressure p_i] --> B[Inner Coating Surface]
    B --> C[Coating Matrix of Thickness L]
    C --> D[Outer Coating Surface]
    D --> E[Storage Environment: Lower Water Vapour Pressure p_s]
    B -.->|Solubility S| F[Sorption Step]
    C -.->|Diffusivity D| G[Diffusion Step]
    F --> H[Effective Permeability P equal D times S]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Steady State Flux J equal P times delta p divided by L]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define an **edible coating** in the context of post-harvest management. List **three** functional roles it performs on a fresh horticultural commodity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **edible coating** is a thin, continuous, food-grade layer of material applied directly onto the surface of a fresh fruit or vegetable, which forms an integral part of the product and may be consumed along with it.

The three functional roles (1 mark each):

1. **Moisture barrier** — reduces transpirational water loss by increasing the resistance to water vapour diffusion from the produce interior to the ambient environment.
2. **Selective gas barrier** — modifies the internal atmosphere (lowers O₂, raises CO₂) which slows respiration and delays senescence.
3. **Active agent carrier** — delivers antimicrobials, antioxidants, or nutraceuticals from the coating matrix onto the produce surface in a controlled manner.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **curing** and **hot water treatment (HWT)** of post-harvest produce. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Curing | Hot Water Treatment (HWT) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Holding produce at controlled temperature and high RH to promote wound healing | Brief immersion in hot water to kill surface pathogens / disinfest insects |
| Temperature | 25–35 °C | 46–55 °C |
| Duration | Days (typically 4–10) | Minutes (3–20) |
| Example | Potato / sweet potato curing | Mango HWT (46 °C, 5 min) against anthracnose |

(1½ marks for the distinction, 1½ marks for the examples.)

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Variant]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **classification of edible coatings** used for fresh fruits and vegetables. Highlight the barrier properties and typical commercial applications of each major class.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wax coating of thickness $L = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m and permeability $P = 9.0 \times 10^{-14}$ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ is applied to a citrus fruit. The internal water vapour partial pressure is $p_i = 3000$ Pa and the external storage RH corresponds to $p_s = 1100$ Pa. Calculate the **steady-state water vapour flux** in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Comment on the effectiveness of this coating relative to an uncoated control showing a flux of 290 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Hydrophobic Wax Coatings (2 marks)**
- Carnauba, beeswax, paraffin, shellac.
- Excellent water vapour barrier; poor gas barrier.
- Commercial application: apples (shellac), citrus (carnauba + paraffin blend).
- Typical coating thickness: 1–10 µm; weight loss reduction: 50–70 %.

**2. Polysaccharide Coatings (2 marks)**
- Chitosan, alginate, pectin, starch, CMC, HPMC.
- Good O₂/CO₂ barrier; poor water vapour barrier.
- Commercial application: strawberries (chitosan), fresh-cut fruit (alginate-Ca gel).
- Often used as composites with lipids.

**3. Protein Coatings (1.5 marks)**
- Whey, casein, soy, gluten, gelatin.
- Excellent O₂ barrier; moderate water vapour barrier.
- Commercial application: nuts, fresh-cut fruit.

**4. Lipid and Composite Coatings (1.5 marks)**
- Fatty acids, acylglycerols, sucrose polyesters.
- Often combined with hydrocolloids to balance properties.
- Commercial application: cheese, fresh-cut produce, mango.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Pressure difference.**

$$\Delta p = p_i - p_s = 3000 - 1100 = 1900 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Molar flux (1.5 marks).**

$$J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-14} \times 1900}{8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$J = \dfrac{1.71 \times 10^{-10}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.138 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flux (1.5 marks).**

$$J_w = 2.138 \times 10^{-5} \times 18.015 = 3.852 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to daily basis (1 mark).**

$$J_{w,day} = 3.852 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 = 33.28 \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·day}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Effectiveness calculation (2 marks).**

$$E = \dfrac{290 - 33.28}{290} \times 100 = 88.5 \text{ percent}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The wax coating is highly effective, reducing water vapour transmission by nearly 90 % — a result consistent with commercial citrus waxing lines which typically achieve 85–95 % weight loss reduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Variant]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, mechanism, and procedure of curing** in post-harvest handling of root and tuber crops. Discuss the **biochemical basis** of wound healing and its impact on subsequent storage life.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of suitable examples, describe the **principle and applications of hot water treatment (HWT)** in post-harvest quarantine and disease management. What are the **risks of heat injury**, and how are they mitigated?

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle and Procedure (3 marks):**
Curing is the controlled post-harvest holding of root/tuber crops at 25–30 °C and 85–95 % RH for 4–10 days, immediately after harvest. The process allows the cut surfaces and bruises to heal before the commodity is moved into long-term storage at lower temperature (10–15 °C).

**Biochemical Basis (2.5 marks):**
- **Suberization:** Suberin — a biopolymer of glycerol and long-chain fatty acids — is deposited in cell walls of wounded tissue within 24–48 h, forming a hydrophobic barrier.
- **Periderm formation:** Beneath the suberized layer, a **cork cambium (phellogen)** develops and produces new cork cells, sealing the wound mechanically.
- **Lignification:** In some crops (sweet potato), additional lignification reinforces the periderm.

**Impact on Storage Life (1.5 marks):**
- Reduces water loss by 50–70 % during subsequent storage.
- Blocks microbial entry (e.g., *Erwinia* soft rot in potato).
- Extends marketable life of potato from 2–3 months (uncured) to 6–9 months (cured).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
HWT involves brief immersion of produce (typically 3–20 min) in water heated to 46–55 °C, exploiting the differential thermal sensitivity between the host tissue and the target pathogen or insect.

**Applications (2.5 marks):**
- **Mango:** 46 °C, 5 min → controls anthracnose (*Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*) and stem-end rot.
- **Citrus:** 52–55 °C, 2–3 min → green mould (*Penicillium digitatum*) suppression.
- **Papaya:** 49 °C, 20 min → disinfestation of fruit fly eggs.
- **Litchi / Longan:** 46–52 °C, 10–20 min → reduces pericarp browning.

**Risks of Heat Injury and Mitigation (2.5 marks):**
- Risks: surface scalding, accelerated ripening, internal browning, peel pitting.
- Mitigation:
  - Strict temperature-time control.
  - Pre-conditioning (warm-up step).
  - Post-treatment hydro-cooling or air-cooling.
  - Avoid HWT on physiologically mature, fully ripe produce.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in numerical problems — e.g., computing flux in g·m⁻²·s⁻¹ but leaving it without converting to g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Examiners will deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing curing with hot water treatment** — these are often used interchangeably by students, but they differ in temperature, duration, and objective. In a 14-mark question, this confusion can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Writing "coating extends shelf life" without explaining the barrier mechanism** — always specify *which* barrier (moisture/gas/microbial) and *how* (Fick's law, modified atmosphere).
> 4. **Forgetting Henry's Law / sorption step** in mass-transfer derivations — losing **1 mark** in (b) type numericals.
> 5. **Not quoting numerical constants** ($M_w = 18.015$ g·mol⁻¹; $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹) — the KTU 2024 scheme values these explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Coatings are barriers, not packaging.** They act *as part of* the produce, modulating mass transfer at the surface.
- **Three barrier roles:** moisture barrier, gas barrier, carrier of active agents. (Mnemonic: **MGC**.)
- **Wax = moisture dominant; Chitosan = antimicrobial + gas; Alginate = gel; Shellac = gloss + CO₂.**
- **Fick's First Law** drives diffusion through coatings; permeability $P = D \cdot S$ combines diffusivity and solubility.
- **Coating thickness is typically 1–10 µm** — thicker ≠ always better (cracking, gas buildup).
- **Curing = wound healing at 25–35 °C, 85–95 % RH, days.** Suberin and periderm are the key biochemical outcomes.
- **HWT = pathogen/insect kill at 46–55 °C, minutes.** Followed by cooling.
- **Effectiveness of coating** $E = \dfrac{WL_{uncoated} - WL_{coated}}{WL_{uncoated}} \times 100$; commercial waxing lines achieve 80–95 %.
- **Selectivity ratio** $\alpha = P_{O_2}/P_{CO_2}$ — ideal range 1–5 for fresh produce.
- **D-value and Arrhenius kinetics** govern microbial kill during HWT.
- **Composite coatings** (lipid + polysaccharide) outperform single-component films — remember this for design-type questions.
- **Regulatory awareness** matters — mention **food-grade (GRAS)** status, **MRLs** for chemicals, and **phytosanitary approval** for HWT/VHT.
- **Always state units in numerical answers** (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ for flux; µm for thickness; °C / min for treatment).
- **Distinguish "MAP effect" from "coating effect"** — coatings generate a *passive* modified atmosphere; MAP is *active* gas flushing.
- **1-MCP blocks ethylene receptors** — a chemical, not a coating, but often combined with waxing lines for apples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insect Control and Microbial Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insect Control and Microbial Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Insect Control — Formal Definition

**Insect Control** in the post-harvest context refers to the integrated set of preventive, suppressive, and eradicative measures employed to manage insect pests that infest harvested agricultural commodities (cereals, pulses, oilseeds, fruits, vegetables, and processed foods) during storage, handling, and distribution. The goal is to maintain commodity quantity, quality, hygienic standards, and marketability while minimizing chemical residues, economic losses, and public health hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Insect control is studied as a *post-harvest loss reduction strategy* — emphasizing storage pests (*Sitophilus*, *Tribolium*, *Callosobruchus*, *Plodia*), their biology, and physical/chemical/biological management tactics.

### 1.2 Microbial Control — Formal Definition

**Microbial Control** refers to the application of physical, chemical, and biological agents/processes to inhibit, inactivate, or eliminate spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, moulds, and viruses) on food surfaces, within food matrices, or in the processing environment. It encompasses preservation, decontamination, and shelf-life extension technologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** The time required, at a given temperature and under specified conditions, to reduce the microbial population by **90 % (1 log cycle)**. This is the most fundamental kinetic parameter in thermal microbial control.

$$D_T = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)}$$

where $N_0$ = initial count, $N_t$ = count after time $t$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> **Analogy 1 — Insect Control is like "House Pest Management":** Imagine a wooden house infested with termites. You wouldn't just spray once; you would (a) **seal entry points** (prevention — sanitation, hermetic storage), (b) **kill existing pests** (fumigation, heat), and (c) **prevent re-entry** (IPM, monitoring traps). Post-harvest insect control uses the same 3-pillar logic: *prevent, kill, monitor.*

> **Analogy 2 — Microbial Control is like "Locking a Garden Gate":** Microbes are invisible "visitors" that want to enter food. Some you **repel at the door** (sanitizers, washing), some you **let in but chain up** (preservatives slowing growth), and some you **kill inside** (heat, irradiation). *Hurdle technology* uses multiple small barriers instead of one huge wall, so microbes cannot overcome them all at once.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Threshold Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key thresholds and constants used throughout this module:**
>
> - **Pasteurization temperature:** **72 °C for 15 s** (HTST) or **63 °C for 30 min** (LTLT)
> - **Commercial sterilization (low-acid canned food):** **121 °C / 15 psi for 3 min minimum (F₀ ≥ 3)**
> - **Cold storage threshold (insect activity):** **< 13 °C** — most stored-product insects become inactive
> - **Lethal temperature (most insects):** **≥ 50 °C for 1 h** or **≥ 60 °C for 10 min**
> - **Phosphine (PH₃) lethal concentration:** **≥ 200 ppm for 5–7 days** at **> 20 °C**
> - **Dose for insect disinfestation by irradiation:** **0.2–0.5 kGy** (USDA/ICGFI approved)
> - **Dose for microbial inactivation:** **1–10 kGy** (radurization) and **> 10 kGy** (radicidation)
> - **Water activity ($a_w$) thresholds:**
>     * Bacteria: $a_w < 0.91$
>     * Yeasts: $a_w < 0.88$
>     * Moulds: $a_w < 0.70$
>     * Xerophilic moulds: $a_w < 0.60$
> - **pH thresholds:** Growth inhibited below **pH 4.5** for most bacteria; moulds/yeasts tolerate pH **2.0–9.0**

---

### 1.5 Classification of Pests and Spoilage Agents (Quick Map)

| Agent Type | Common Examples | Primary Target Commodities |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal feeders (borers)** | *Sitophilus oryzae*, *Sitophilus zeamais*, *Rhyzopertha dominica* | Rice, wheat, maize |
| **External feeders (beetles)** | *Tribolium castaneum*, *Oryzaephilus surinamensis* | Flour, milled products |
| **Larval feeders (moths)** | *Plodia interpunctella*, *Sitotroga cerealella* | Stored grains, dried fruits |
| **Bruchids** | *Callosobruchus chinensis*, *A. obtectus* | Pulses (cowpea, green gram) |
| **Mites** | *Acarus siro*, *Tyrophagus putrescentiae* | Cheese, grains, hay |
| **Spoilage bacteria** | *Pseudomonas*, *Erwinia*, *Lactobacillus*, *Clostridium* | Fresh produce, meat, dairy |
| **Spoilage fungi** | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Fusarium*, *Botrytis* | Grains, fruits, vegetables |
| **Pathogens** | *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *E. coli*, *Staph. aureus* | Meat, dairy, RTE foods |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Insect Control — Detailed Methodological Breakdown

#### A. Preventive (Prophylactic) Measures
- **Sanitation:** Cleaning godowns, sacks, machinery; removing residual grain (broken kernels harbor insects).
- **Hermetic storage:** Sealed airtight enclosures (e.g., Purdue Improved Crop Storage — PICS bags, cocoons, metal silos) where modified atmospheres (↑CO₂, ↓O₂) naturally kill insects via hypoxia.
- **Resistant varieties & good grain hygiene:** Use of clean, dry grain (moisture < 12 %) and pest-resistant cultivars.
- **Physical barriers:** Window/door screens, dunnage, palletized stacking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prevention is prioritized (KTU 2024 high-weightage):** "An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure" — chemical treatments can never fully replace proper sanitation; in fact, **80 % of stored-grain insect infestations originate from residues left in storage structures.**

#### B. Physical / Mechanical Control
- **Temperature extremes:**
    * **Cold disinfestation:** Exposure to $\leq -20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for 2–7 days kills all life stages.
    * **Heat disinfestation:** Fluidized-bed heating to **60 °C** for **≥ 1 min** or **sun-drying** grain to < 10 % moisture.
- **Controlled atmospheres (CA):** Elevating CO₂ to **> 60 %** or depleting O₂ to **< 2 %** for **≥ 10 days**.
- **Mechanical separation:** Sieving, aspiration, gravity separators, optical sorters.
- **Irradiation:** Gamma rays, X-rays, or electron beams at **0.2–1.0 kGy** for insect disinfestation.
- **Impact / Entoleter devices:** Centrifugal force smashes insects in flour mills.

#### C. Chemical Control
- **Fumigants (gaseous, penetrate commodity):**
    * **Phosphine (PH₃)** — released from aluminum phosphide tablets; broad-spectrum, residue-free; requires sealed structures.
    * **Methyl bromide (CH₃Br)** — phased out under the **Montreal Protocol** (ozone-depleting); limited quarantine use only.
    * **Ethylene oxide, sulfuryl fluoride** — alternative fumigants.
- **Contact insecticides (residual on surfaces):**
    * **Organophosphates:** Malathion, pirimiphos-methyl (Actellic).
    * **Pyrethroids:** Deltamethrin, cypermethrin, bifenthrin.
    * **Insect growth regulators (IGRs):** Methoprene, fenoxycarb.
- **Grain protectants:** Insecticides applied directly to grain (e.g., Actellic Super, K-Obiol).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Many students lose marks by confusing **fumigants** (gas, penetrate bulk) with **contact insecticides** (spray/dust, surface action). Memorize: *Fumigant = Phosphine; Protectant = Pirimiphos-methyl.*

#### D. Biological Control
- **Predators:** *Xylocoris flavipes* (pirate bug), *Blattisocius tarsalis* (mite).
- **Parasitoids:** *Anisopteromalus calandrae*, *Bracon hebetor* (parasitoid wasps).
- **Pathogens:** *Beauveria bassiana*, *Metarhizium anisopliae* (entomopathogenic fungi); *Bacillus thuringiensis* (Bt).
- **Pheromone traps:** Sex/aggregation pheromones (e.g., *Trimedlure*, *Sitotroga* lures) for monitoring and mass trapping.

#### E. Legal / Regulatory Methods
- **Quarantine** to prevent entry of exotic pests (e.g., *Trogoderma granarium*).
- **Phytosanitary treatments** for export consignments.

---

### 2.2 Microbial Control — Detailed Methodological Breakdown

#### A. Physical Methods
- **Thermal — High Temperature:**
    * **Pasteurization:** Mild heat to inactivate pathogens/enzymes; not sterile.
    * **Sterilization:** Complete destruction of all viable microorganisms including spores.
    * **UHT (Ultra-High Temperature):** 135–150 °C for 2–5 s.
    * **Blanching:** 75–95 °C for 1–10 min (inactivates enzymes before freezing/drying).
- **Thermal — Low Temperature:**
    * **Chilling (0–8 °C)** slows but does not stop psychrotrophs.
    * **Freezing (≤ -18 °C)** — water activity reduction, ice crystal damage.
- **Non-Thermal Physical Methods:**
    * **Irradiation (ionizing):** Gamma, X-ray, e-beam at 1–10 kGy (radurization); > 10 kGy (radicidation).
    * **High Pressure Processing (HPP):** **400–600 MPa** for 5–15 min; inactivates vegetative cells, not spores.
    * **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF):** **20–80 kV/cm** for microseconds.
    * **UV-C light (254 nm):** Surface disinfection.
    * **Ultrasound / Sonication:** 20–100 kHz; synergistic with heat (thermosonication).
    * **Cold plasma, Ozone treatment.**
- **Water activity reduction:** Drying, salting, sugaring, intermediate moisture foods.

#### B. Chemical Methods
- **Sanitizers & Disinfectants:** Hypochlorite (50–200 ppm free Cl), quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs), peroxyacids, H₂O₂, ethanol (70 %).
- **Food preservatives (Class I/II preservatives):**
    * **Acids:** Acetic, citric, lactic, propionic, sorbic, benzoic acid.
    * **Salts:** Sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate, sodium nitrite, sodium propionate.
    * **Gases:** CO₂, O₃, SO₂ (sulphiting).
    * **Natural antimicrobials:** Nisin, natamycin, lysozyme, essential oils (oregano, cinnamon), chitosan.
- **Antimicrobial mechanism:** Membrane disruption, pH depression, enzyme inhibition, protein denaturation, oxidative damage.

#### C. Biological Methods
- **Competitive exclusion:** Probiotic cultures (*Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*).
- **Biopreservation:** Bacteriocins (nisin, pediocin), protective cultures.
- **Fermentation:** Natural lactic acid production by LAB lowers pH.

#### D. Hurdle Technology (Leistner, 1978)
- Combines multiple mild preservation factors ("hurdles") — pH, $a_w$, temperature, redox potential, preservatives, competitive flora — to inhibit microbes synergistically.
- **Examples of hurdles:** pH, $a_w$, heat, salt, smoke, antimicrobials, vacuum packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hurdle technology principle:** Each hurdle alone is insufficient to inhibit microbes, but their *combined* effect overwhelms microbial homeostasis (homeostasis = the ability of a microbe to stabilize its internal environment against external stress). This is a **favourite KTU theory question.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Microbial Kinetics)

| Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $D_T = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N_t}$ | Decimal reduction time at temperature $T$ | minutes |
| $F_T = D_T \cdot [\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)]$ | Equivalent sterilization time at reference $T$ | minutes |
| $F_0 = \Delta t \sum 10^{\frac{(T - 121)}{z}}$ | Sterilizing value referenced to **121 °C** with $z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | minutes |
| $z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10} D_1 - \log_{10} D_2}$ | Temperature increase needed to reduce $D$ by 90 % | °C |
| $\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{z}$ | Linear D-value vs temperature relationship | dimensionless |
| $12D$ concept | Time to reduce $10^{12}$ spores (e.g., *Clostridium botulinum*) to $10^{0}$ | minutes |
| $k = \dfrac{2.303}{D_T}$ | First-order inactivation rate constant | $\text{min}^{-1}$ |
| Shelf life: $t_s = \dfrac{\log_{10}(N_{\max}) - \log_{10}(N_0)}{k}$ | Time to reach maximum acceptable count | days |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$ | Determines fraction of undissociated acid (active form) | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -kN$ | First-order microbial death kinetics | $\text{CFU}\cdot\text{mL}^{-1}\cdot\text{min}^{-1}$ |
| Q₁₀ = $\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient of reaction rate | dimensionless (typically 2–3 for biological) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `|` is used in the table:** KTU formula sheet rules require clean pipe-free LaTeX. The `\vert` symbol would be used only if an *absolute value* were genuinely needed; in these microbial kinetic expressions, all bars are simple division or fraction slashes.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Food Engineering

- **Stored grain industry:** IPM + hermetic storage saves **> $20 billion/year** globally (FAO estimate).
- **Cold chain logistics:** Combines Hurdle tech + HPP + MAP to extend fresh-cut produce shelf life from 3 → 14 days.
- **Export trade:** Phytosanitary irradiation (0.15 kGy minimum for fruit flies) facilitates mango/papaya exports to the USA, Japan, EU.
- **Public health:** Pasteurization and irradiation prevent **Salmonellosis**, **Listeriosis**, and **aflatoxin** exposure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — First-Order Inactivation Kinetics

**Goal:** Show that microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics, leading to the **D-value** definition.

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical observation.**

The rate at which a microbial population dies, under constant lethal conditions, is proportional to the number of viable cells present at that instant. In differential form:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

where $N$ = number of viable cells at time $t$, $k$ = specific death rate constant (min⁻¹), and the negative sign indicates a *decreasing* population.

**Step 2 — Separate the variables.**

Rearrange so that all $N$ terms are on one side and $t$ terms on the other:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides** from initial count $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to count $N_t$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln(N_t) - \ln(N_0) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Convert natural log to base-10 log** (more convenient in food microbiology):

$$\log_{10}(N_t) = \log_{10}(N_0) - \frac{k \cdot t}{2.303}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $t$:**

$$t = \frac{2.303}{k} \cdot \bigl[\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Define D-value as the time for 1 log reduction** (i.e., $N_t = N_0 / 10$):

$$D_T = \frac{2.303}{k} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \frac{2.303}{k} \cdot \log_{10}(10) = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

$$\therefore \quad \boxed{k = \frac{2.303}{D_T}}$$

This is the foundational relationship that ties the **rate constant** $k$ to the **D-value** used universally in thermal process design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — The $F_0$ (Sterilizing Value) Formula

**Goal:** Derive the integrated lethality expression for a variable-temperature sterilization process.

**Step 1 — At any instant $t$, the lethal effect is contributed** by the current temperature $T$ via the standard D-curve relationship:

$$D_T = D_{121} \cdot 10^{\frac{121 - T}{z}}$$

**Step 2 — Define lethality $L$** as the *reciprocal* of D at the given temperature:

$$L = \frac{1}{D_T} = \frac{1}{D_{121}} \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121}{z}}$$

**Step 3 — The total sterilizing value** $F_0$ is the time integral of lethality over the entire process:

$$F_0 = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{end}}} L \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{end}}} 10^{\frac{T(t) - 121}{z}} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Discretize** for a process profile with small time steps $\Delta t$:

$$F_0 \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t \cdot 10^{\frac{T_i - 121}{z}}$$

**Step 5 — Standard convention:** with $z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for *Clostridium botulinum* spores, the expression simplifies to the form used in process calculation worksheets:

$$F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{\frac{T_i - 121}{10}}$$

**Step 6 — Min. recommended $F_0$ for low-acid canned food** (US FDA): **$F_0 \geq 3$ min** (12D concept for *C. botulinum*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU note:** The 12D concept means reducing *C. botulinum* spores by **12 log cycles** (from $10^{12}$ to $10^{0}$). With $z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and reference $T = 121\ ^\circ\text{C}$, this is equivalent to the 3-min benchmark.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1 — D-value from heat penetration data

**Problem:** A milk sample initially contains $N_0 = 1 \times 10^{6}$ CFU/mL of *Listeria monocytogenes*. After pasteurization at **72 °C for 15 s**, the surviving count is $N_t = 1 \times 10^{2}$ CFU/mL. Calculate the **D-value at 72 °C** and the **first-order rate constant $k$**.

**Solution:**

$$
\log_{10}(N_0) = 6, \quad \log_{10}(N_t) = 2
$$

$$
D_{72} = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)} = \frac{15\ \text{s}}{6 - 2} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75\ \text{s}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2.303}{D_{72}} = \frac{2.303}{3.75} = 0.614\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation:** *Listeria* is very heat-sensitive; a D-value of just 3.75 s confirms HTST pasteurization provides a **4-log reduction** in 15 s.

---

#### Example 2 — F₀ calculation from a process time-temperature profile

**Problem:** A canned food retort holds the product at the following temperatures for the indicated times: **(100 °C, 10 min)**, **(110 °C, 15 min)**, **(121 °C, 5 min)**, **(115 °C, 10 min)**. Using $z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$, calculate $F_0$.

**Solution:**

For each phase, the lethality contribution is $10^{(T - 121)/10}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Phase 1:}\ \ 10^{(100 - 121)/10} = 10^{-2.1} = 0.00794 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.00794 \times 10 = 0.0794\ \text{min}\\[4pt]
&\text{Phase 2:}\ \ 10^{(110 - 121)/10} = 10^{-1.1} = 0.0794 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.0794 \times 15 = 1.1914\ \text{min}\\[4pt]
&\text{Phase 3:}\ \ 10^{(121 - 121)/10} = 10^{0} = 1.000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.000 \times 5 = 5.000\ \text{min}\\[4pt]
&\text{Phase 4:}\ \ 10^{(115 - 121)/10} = 10^{-0.6} = 0.2512 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.2512 \times 10 = 2.512\ \text{min}\\[4pt]
&F_0 = 0.0794 + 1.1914 + 5.000 + 2.512 = 8.783\ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $F_0 = 8.78$ min $\gg$ 3 min minimum → **process is commercially sterile** (over-processed by ~2.9×).

---

#### Example 3 — Shelf-life prediction using $t_s$

**Problem:** Fresh-cut salad contains $N_0 = 5 \times 10^{3}$ CFU/g of *Pseudomonas*. The maximum acceptable count is $N_{\max} = 10^{7}$ CFU/g. The growth rate constant at 4 °C is $k = 0.18\ \text{day}^{-1}$. Predict the **shelf life**.

**Solution:**

$$
t_s = \frac{\log_{10}(N_{\max}) - \log_{10}(N_0)}{k} = \frac{\log_{10}(10^{7}) - \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{3})}{0.18}
$$

$$
t_s = \frac{7 - 3.301}{0.18} = \frac{3.699}{0.18} = 20.55\ \text{days}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 4 °C, the salad remains microbiologically acceptable for **~ 20 days** — far longer than the 3–5 day commercial limit, indicating *spoilage is dominated by sensory, not microbial, limits*.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Control Methods

| Control Method | Target Agent | Mode of Action | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phosphine fumigation | Insects | Respiratory poison (inhibits cytochrome oxidase) | Penetrates bulk, no residue | Slow (5–7 d), toxic to operators, resistance emerging |
| Heat disinfestation (60 °C) | Insects | Protein denaturation, enzyme inactivation | No chemicals, rapid | Energy-intensive, may damage quality |
| Hermetic storage | Insects | Hypoxia (O₂ < 2 %) | Zero chemical, low cost | Limited to dry commodities, slow (weeks) |
| Irradiation (0.5 kGy) | Insects | DNA damage, sterility | Cold, residue-free | Public perception, equipment cost |
| Pasteurization (72 °C/15s) | Vegetative bacteria, pathogens | Protein/enzyme denaturation | Effective, established | Spores survive, flavor change |
| HPP (600 MPa) | Vegetative cells, enzymes | Membrane rupture, protein unfolding | Retains fresh-like quality | Spores survive, batch process |
| Chemical preservatives (sorbic acid) | Moulds, yeasts, bacteria | pH depression, membrane disruption | Low cost, broad | Consumer preference for "clean label" |
| Hurdle technology | Spoilage + pathogens | Synergistic multi-target | Mild treatments, quality | Complex, requires validation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Tree — Choosing an Insect Control Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Infestation Detected] --> B{Commodity Type?}
    B --> C1[Cereals / Pulses / Dry Goods]
    B --> C2[Fresh Fruits / Vegetables]
    B --> C3[Processed / Packaged Food]
    C1 --> D1{Moisture Content > 13 %?}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Dry to 12 % first]
    D1 -- No --> F1{Sealed Storage Available?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Hermetic Storage PICS Bag]
    F1 -- No --> H1{Temperature Available?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Heat or Cold Disinfestation]
    H1 -- No --> J1[Phosphine Fumigation]
    E1 --> F1
    C2 --> D2[Quarantine + Cold Treatment or Irradiation]
    C3 --> D3[Controlled Atmosphere + IGR + Pheromone Traps]
    G1 --> K[Monitor + Prevention]
    I1 --> K
    J1 --> K
    D2 --> K
    D3 --> K
    K --> L[End: Sustainable Pest Management]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each decision node mirrors a practical field question a storage manager asks. The terminal "K" node represents the IPM cycle: *monitor, prevent, intervene, repeat.*

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Multi-Hurdle Microbial Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hurdle1[Hurdle 1: Physical]
        H1A[Heat 72 C / 15 s]
        H1B[HPP 600 MPa]
        H1C[UV C 254 nm]
    end
    subgraph Hurdle2[Hurdle 2: Chemical]
        H2A[pH Adjustment]
        H2B[Water Activity Reduction]
        H2C[Natural Antimicrobial Nisin]
    end
    subgraph Hurdle3[Hurdle 3: Biological]
        H3A[Competitive LAB Flora]
        H3B[Protective Cultures]
    end
    subgraph Outcome[Final Outcome]
        O1[Shelf life Extended]
        O2[Pathogens Eliminated]
        O3[Quality Retained]
    end
    Hurdle1 --> Hurdle2
    Hurdle2 --> Hurdle3
    Hurdle3 --> Outcome
    H1A --> O1
    H2A --> O1
    H3A --> O2
    H2C --> O2
    H1B --> O3
    H2B --> O3
    H3B --> O3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The three hurdle "sub-graphs" represent the three categories of preservation factors. Microbes must overcome all three barriers simultaneously — if any single hurdle is intact, microbial homeostasis is destabilized and the cell dies or becomes inactivated.

---

### 4.3 Process Flow — Post-Harvest Insect Control Pipeline (Bulk Grain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Harvested Grain] --> P2[Cleaning and Drying]
    P2 --> P3{MC Less than 12 percent?}
    P3 -- No --> P2
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Pre Storage Sanitation of Godown]
    P4 --> P5[Bag or Bulk Loading]
    P5 --> P6{Choose Treatment}
    P6 -- Path A --> Q1[Chemical: Phosphine Fumigation 200 ppm 5 to 7 days]
    P6 -- Path B --> Q2[Physical: Heat Disinfestation 60 C]
    P6 -- Path C --> Q3[Biological: Bt or Parasitoid Release]
    P6 -- Path D --> Q4[Hermetic Sealed Storage]
    Q1 --> P7[Storage Monitoring]
    Q2 --> P7
    Q3 --> P7
    Q4 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Periodic Inspection Traps Probes]
    P8 -- Infestation Detected --> P6
    P8 -- Clean --> P9[Continue Storage]
    P9 --> P10[End: Distribution and Use]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Functional Map — Microbial Inactivation Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Population N0] --> B[Apply Lethal Treatment]
    B --> C[Microbial Death Follows First Order Kinetics]
    C --> D[D value: Time for 1 log reduction]
    C --> E[Z value: Temp change for 1 log D change]
    C --> F[Rate Constant k 2.303 divided by D]
    D --> G[Total Lethality F0]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Sterility Target Met}
    H -- Yes --> I[Commercially Sterile Product]
    H -- No --> J[Extend Process Time or Temperature]
    J --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define the term "D-value" used in microbial inactivation kinetics. A bacterial suspension has an initial count of $10^{5}$ CFU/mL. After 6 minutes of heating, the count drops to $10^{2}$ CFU/mL. Calculate the D-value and the first-order rate constant $k$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *D-value (decimal reduction time) is the time, at a specified temperature and conditions, required to reduce the microbial population by 90 % (1 log cycle).*

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
D_T = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)} = \frac{6\ \text{min}}{5 - 2} = 2\ \text{min}
$$

$$
k = \frac{2.303}{D_T} = \frac{2.303}{2} = 1.15\ \text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Final answer:** $D_T = 2\ \text{min}$; $k = 1.15\ \text{min}^{-1}$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List any three physical methods of insect control in stored grains with one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per method):**

1. **Heat disinfestation (60 °C / 1 min):** Kills all insect life stages without chemical residue.
2. **Cold treatment (≤ –20 °C for 2–7 days):** Lethal to insects; simultaneously preserves grain quality.
3. **Hermetic storage (PICS bags / metal silos):** Generates hypoxia (O₂ < 2 %) inside the bag, killing insects passively with zero chemical input.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 4, Choice A]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **principle, method, and advantages of phosphine fumigation** for controlling stored-grain insects. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **hurdle technology concept** with a suitable food example. How does it differ from using a single severe preservation treatment? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Phosphine Fumigation (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Phosphine (PH₃) is liberated when **aluminum phosphide (AlP) tablets** react with atmospheric moisture:

$$
\text{AlP} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 + \text{PH}_3\uparrow
$$

It is a **respiratory poison** that inhibits **cytochrome-c oxidase** in the mitochondrial electron-transport chain, halting ATP synthesis in insects. Death occurs within **5–7 days** of continuous exposure at concentrations ≥ **200 ppm** and temperature > **20 °C**.

**Method (3 marks):**

1. Seal the storage structure (godown, silo, container) gas-tight using fumigation sheets.
2. Place AlP tablets (3 g/tonne for bag stacks; 6 g/m³ for empty godowns) on trays or in sachets.
3. Expose for minimum **5 days** (target 7 days).
4. Ventilate thoroughly before re-entry; **PH₃ < 0.3 ppm** at worker entry.
5. Monitor using a **Dräger tube** or electronic PH₃ detector.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Penetrates bulk commodity deeply.
- Decomposes to **non-toxic residues** (H₃PO₄).
- No taint or flavor change in grains.
- Cost-effective and globally registered.
- Effective against eggs, larvae, pupae, and adults (broad-spectrum).

---

**(b) Hurdle Technology (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** *Hurdle technology*, introduced by **Leistner (1978)**, combines several *mild* preservation factors ("hurdles") — such as pH, $a_w$, temperature, redox potential, preservatives, and competitive microflora — so that their *synergistic* effect inactivates spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms. Each hurdle alone would be insufficient, but together they overwhelm the microbe's **homeostasis** (its ability to maintain stable internal conditions).

**Food Example (2 marks):** **Sausage / Intermediate-Moisture Meat:**
- pH ↓ (added lactic acid)
- $a_w$ ↓ (NaCl, drying)
- Sodium nitrite (antibotulinal)
- Mild heat (smoking/cooking)
- Vacuum packaging (low redox potential)
- Lactic acid bacterial starter culture (competitive flora)
- Refrigerated storage

All seven hurdles are individually mild but, combined, prevent both *Clostridium botulinum* and spoilage flora.

**Difference from Single-Severe Treatment (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Hurdle Technology | Single Severe Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| Number of factors | Multiple, mild | One, intense |
| Energy | Low | High |
| Nutritional/sensory quality | Retained | Often degraded |
| Microbial resistance risk | Low (multi-target) | Higher (single target) |
| Example | Mild heat + pH + salt + nitrite | Autoclave at 121 °C / 15 psi |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Hurdle Technology:** Many students incorrectly *list* hurdles but **fail to explain the synergistic mechanism** (homeostasis disturbance). Marks are reserved for explicitly mentioning "microbial homeostasis" and the *synergistic* (not additive) effect.

---

> **Q3. Alternative — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 4, Choice B]**
>
> **(a)** Classify the **physical methods of microbial control** in foods with examples. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A vegetable canning process holds the product at the following temperatures:
> *105 °C for 12 min, 112 °C for 18 min, 118 °C for 8 min, 110 °C for 10 min.*
> Taking $z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$, calculate the **sterilizing value $F_0$** and state whether the process meets the FDA minimum standard. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Physical Microbial Control Methods (7 marks):**

**A. Thermal (High Temperature) — (3 marks):**
1. **Pasteurization:** HTST (72 °C / 15 s) or LTLT (63 °C / 30 min); inactivates vegetative pathogens.
2. **Sterilization:** 121 °C / 15 psi / 15–30 min; kills all microbes including spores.
3. **UHT:** 135–150 °C / 2–5 s; sterile, refrigerated shelf life.
4. **Blanching:** 75–95 °C / 1–10 min; inactivates enzymes + surface microbes before freezing.

**B. Thermal (Low Temperature) — (1 mark):**
5. **Chilling (0–8 °C)** and **Freezing (≤ –18 °C)** — slow/stop metabolism of spoilage microbes.

**C. Non-Thermal — (3 marks):**
6. **Irradiation:** Gamma / X-ray / e-beam; 1–10 kGy radurization; > 10 kGy radicidation.
7. **High Pressure Processing (HPP):** 400–600 MPa; membrane rupture.
8. **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF):** 20–80 kV/cm; electroporation.
9. **UV-C light (254 nm):** Surface disinfection.
10. **Ultrasound (20–100 kHz):** Cavitation damage; synergistic with heat (thermosonication).
11. **Cold plasma, Ozone treatment.**

**Examples of use:** Pasteurized milk (HTST), UHT milk, retort-sterilized canned vegetables, HPP guacamole, irradiated spices, UV-treated fruit juices.

---

**(b) $F_0$ Calculation (7 marks):**

For each phase, lethality factor $= 10^{(T-121)/10}$, then multiply by $\Delta t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&105\ ^\circ\text{C, 12 min:}\quad 10^{(105-121)/10} = 10^{-1.6} = 0.02512 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.02512 \times 12 = 0.3014\\[4pt]
&112\ ^\circ\text{C, 18 min:}\quad 10^{(112-121)/10} = 10^{-0.9} = 0.12589 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.12589 \times 18 = 2.2661\\[4pt]
&118\ ^\circ\text{C, 8 min:}\quad 10^{(118-121)/10} = 10^{-0.3} = 0.50119 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.50119 \times 8 = 4.0095\\[4pt]
&110\ ^\circ\text{C, 10 min:}\quad 10^{(110-121)/10} = 10^{-1.1} = 0.07943 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.07943 \times 10 = 0.7943
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F_0 = 0.3014 + 2.2661 + 4.0095 + 0.7943 = 7.371\ \text{min}
$$

**Verdict:** $F_0 = 7.37$ min $\gg 3$ min → **Process EXCEEDS the FDA minimum (commercial sterility achieved).** It is over-processed by ~2.46×, which may cause nutrient and quality loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — $F_0$ calculation:** Most students *forget* to convert each phase's temperature using the $(T-121)/10$ exponent *before* multiplying by time. Marks are awarded step-wise, so show all four contributions clearly and sum them last. A common error is using $T$ directly in minutes (e.g., "12 min of 105 °C = 12 min of lethality"), which yields a totally wrong answer.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Insect Control:**
> - [x] **Insect control pillars = Prevention + Kill + Monitor** (IPM).
> - [x] **Phosphine (PH₃)** = standard fumigant; ≥ 200 ppm / 5–7 days / > 20 °C.
> - [x] **Methyl bromide** = ozone-depleting → restricted.
> - [x] **Irradiation dose for insects** = 0.2–0.5 kGy.
> - [x] **Hermetic storage** = passive insect control via hypoxia (O₂ < 2 %).
> - [x] **PICS bags** = Purdue Improved Crop Storage — widely adopted in Africa/Asia.
> - [x] **Biological agents:** *B. thuringiensis*, parasitoid wasps, *Beauveria* fungus.
> - [x] **Pheromone traps** = monitoring, not eradication.

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Microbial Control:**
> - [x] **D-value** = time for 1-log (90 %) reduction at given $T$.
> - [x] **z-value** = temperature change for 1-log change in D.
> - [x] **$F_0$ ≥ 3 min** = US FDA minimum for low-acid canned food.
> - [x] **12-D concept** for *C. botulinum* = 12 log reductions.
> - [x] **Hurdle technology** = synergistic mild factors; Leistner 1978.
> - [x] **Homeostasis** = key word in hurdle technology definition.
> - [x] **$a_w$ thresholds:** bacteria < 0.91, yeasts < 0.88, moulds < 0.70.
> - [x] **HTST pasteurization** = 72 °C / 15 s.
> - [x] **UHT** = 135–150 °C / 2–5 s; sterile product.
> - [x] **HPP** = 400–600 MPa; inactivates vegetative cells only.
> - [x] **Irradiation doses:** radurization 1–10 kGy; radicidation > 10 kGy.

> **High-Yield Mnemonics for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - **Insect control order:** **"SHE-HE-FE-PE-BE"** = Sanitation → Hermetic → Heat/Fumigation → Physical/Protectant → Biological/Behavioural → Pheromone/Ethylene.
> - **$a_w$ order (highest to lowest needed for growth):** Bacteria (0.91) > Yeasts (0.88) > Moulds (0.70) > Xerophiles (0.60). Mnemonic: **"BY-MX" (Be Yeast, My Xerosis)**.
> - **Hurdle categories:** **"PARC"** = pH, $a_w$, Redox (Eh), Chemical preservatives.

---

### 5.2 KTU Examiners' Frequently Asked 1-Liners (Last-Minute Q-Bank)

| # | Question | One-Line Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Most common fumigant for stored grain? | **Phosphine (PH₃)** from AlP tablets. |
| 2 | PICS bags work on which principle? | **Hermetic hypoxia** — O₂ < 2 %. |
| 3 | D-value for *C. botulinum* at 121 °C? | **$D_{121} = 0.25$ min.** |
| 4 | z-value for *C. botulinum* spores? | **$z = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$.** |
| 5 | 12-D process equates to how many minutes at 121 °C? | **$F_0 = 3$ min** ($12 \times 0.25 = 3$). |
| 6 | HPP works on what microorganisms? | **Vegetative cells, yeasts, moulds — not bacterial spores.** |
| 7 | Propose a non-chemical insect control for pulses in tropical storage. | **Hermetic triple-bagging (PICS)**. |
| 8 | Active form of sorbic acid preservative? | **Undissociated (protonated) form** — uses Henderson–Hasselbalch. |
| 9 | Why is Hurdle technology preferred over sterilization? | **Better retention of sensory + nutritional quality; lower energy cost.** |
| 10 | Name two entomopathogenic fungi used in biological insect control. | ***Beauveria bassiana*** and ***Metarhizium anisopliae***. |

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INSECT & MICROBIAL CONTROL**
