# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define Water Activity (aᵥ) and explain its significance in post-harvest food quality evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water activity (aᵥ)** is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food to the partial vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature:
> $$a_w = \frac{P_{\text{sample}}}{P_{\text{pure water}}}$$
> It ranges from **0 to 1**. Significance:
> (1) Governs microbial growth — bacteria need $a_w \ge 0.86$, yeasts $\ge 0.80$, moulds $\ge 0.70$.
> (2) Predicts shelf stability — $a_w \le 0.60$ indicates a shelf-stable product.
> (3) Influences enzymatic and non-enzymatic reactions in post-harvest perishables.
> *(3 distinct points for 3 marks.)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between objective and subjective methods of quality assessment with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Objective | Subjective |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Quantitative instrumental measurement | Human sensory perception |
| Examples | Hunter Lab colorimeter (L\*a\*b\*); Magness-Taylor penetrometer (firmness) | Triangle taste test; 9-point Hedonic scale |
| Repeatability | High; instrument-calibrated | Variable; depends on panel training |

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for contrast + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module 4, Q Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *(a) Explain the principle and working of Hunter Lab Colorimeter for food quality measurement. List the color parameters derived and interpret their meaning. (7 marks)*

### (b) Discuss the various non-destructive methods of quality evaluation in post-harvest foods with suitable examples. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks):**
The Hunter Lab Colorimeter measures the **reflectance** of a food surface under a standard illuminant (commonly D65) and a standard observer angle (10°). The reflected light is split into three tristimulus components X, Y, Z which are mathematically converted to the **L\*, a\*, b\*** color space.

**Step 2 — Optical layout (2 marks):**
```
Light source → Diffuser → Sample surface → Detector
                                       ↑
                            45°/0° geometry
```
- **Illumination:** Pulsed Xenon arc
- **Geometry:** 45° illumination / 0° viewing (or vice versa)
- **Aperture:** Adjustable (small/large) for different samples

**Step 3 — Color parameters (2 marks):**

- **L\***: Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\***: Red-Green axis (+a\* = red, −a\* = green)
- **b\***: Yellow-Blue axis (+b\* = yellow, −b\* = blue)
- Derived: **Hue angle h°**, **Chroma C\***, **ΔE\***

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** For tomato sauce, decreasing a\* and increasing b\* during storage indicates browning / oxidation. For green chillies, a\* becomes positive as they ripen to red.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):**
Non-destructive methods measure quality attributes **without altering, consuming, or destroying** the food sample, making them ideal for grading, sorting, and online industrial inspection.

**Step 2 — Methods and principles (4 marks):**

| Method | Principle | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **NIR Spectroscopy (780–2500 nm)** | Vibrational overtones of C–H, O–H, N–H bonds | Moisture, protein, sugar in grains |
| **X-ray / CT imaging** | Differential X-ray attenuation | Internal defects in apple, pit in cherry |
| **Computer Vision** | Image processing, RGB/HSV analysis | Color sorting of bell peppers |
| **MRI** | Nuclear magnetic resonance of H nuclei | Internal bruising in fruits |
| **Acoustic / Ultrasonic** | Sound velocity through tissue | Firmness of apple, mango |
| **E-nose** | Conducting polymer sensor array | Freshness of fish, meat |

**Step 3 — Advantages (1 mark):**
- No sample loss → 100% inspection feasible
- Suitable for **online grading and sorting** on conveyor belts
- Eliminates sampling bias in destructive testing

**Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
- High capital cost; calibration matrix development is complex
- Less precise than wet chemistry for trace analytes

---

> **Module 4, Q Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *(a) Describe the proximate analysis of foods. Explain the principle and procedure of Kjeldahl method for protein estimation. (7 marks)*

### (b) Explain sensory evaluation methods. Describe the 9-point Hedonic scale and its application in post-harvest shelf-life studies. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Proximate analysis definition (1 mark):**
Proximate analysis is the systematic determination of the major food constituents: **moisture, ash, fat, protein, crude fiber, and carbohydrate (by difference)**.

**Step 2 — Significance (1 mark):** Used for nutritional labeling, regulatory compliance, and quality grading.

**Step 3 — Kjeldahl principle (2 marks):**
The Kjeldahl method estimates crude protein by:
$$\%\text{N} \longrightarrow \times\, 6.25 \longrightarrow \%\text{ Protein}$$

The 6.25 factor arises from the assumption that proteins contain **16% N** on average (100/16 = 6.25).

**Step 4 — Procedure (2 marks):**
1. **Digestion:** Sample + conc. H₂SO₄ + catalyst (CuSO₄ + K₂SO₄ or Se) → NH₄⁺ formed
2. **Distillation:** NH₄⁺ + NaOH → NH₃ released, trapped in boric acid solution
3. **Titration:** Trapped NH₃ titrated against standard HCl
4. **Calculation:**
   $$\%\text{N} = \frac{(V_{\text{sample}} - V_{\text{blank}}) \times N_{HCl} \times 1.4007}{W_{\text{sample}}}$$

**Step 5 — Limitation (1 mark):** Does not detect non-protein nitrogen; results include nitrates and amines.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Sensory evaluation is the scientific discipline of measuring, analysing, and interpreting human responses to food via the five senses.

**Step 2 — Test categories (1 mark):** Discriminative, Descriptive, Affective.

**Step 3 — 9-Point Hedonic scale (3 marks):**

| Score | Descriptor |
|---|---|
| 9 | Like extremely |
| 8 | Like very much |
| 7 | Like moderately |
| 6 | Like slightly |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike |
| 4 | Dislike slightly |
| 3 | Dislike moderately |
| 2 | Dislike very much |
| 1 | Dislike extremely |

**Step 4 — Application in shelf-life (2 marks):**
- Consumers rate the product at fixed intervals during storage (e.g., 0, 3, 7, 14 days).
- Plot mean hedonic score vs time; **end of shelf life** is conventionally defined as the time when mean score falls below **5** or rejection level.
- Combined with instrumental data (ΔE\*, TPC, aᵥ), it gives a holistic "consumer-perceived" shelf life.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse °Brix with sucrose concentration — it is an "apparent" measure and includes all soluble solids.
> - **Do NOT** skip units in calculations. Marks are docked when % moisture is given without w.b./d.b. specification.
> - **Do NOT** state $Q_{10} = 2$ as a universal constant. It is product-specific and must be stated as such.
> - **Always** show the conversion factor (e.g., 6.25 for N → protein) explicitly in Kjeldahl calculations.
> - **Avoid** using absolute value $\vert x \vert$ in a markdown table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or `abs(x)` notation in hand-written scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Measurement of Product Quality Methods**

- **Two paradigms of quality measurement:** Objective (instrumental) and Subjective (sensory). The KTU 2024 module demands knowledge of both.

- **Proximate analysis** covers: Moisture, Ash, Fat, Protein (Kjeldahl × 6.25), Crude Fiber, Carbohydrate (by difference). AOAC is the standard.

- **Water activity (aᵥ)** is the single most important quality indicator for microbial stability. **aᵥ ≤ 0.60 = shelf stable**; **aᵥ ≥ 0.86 = bacterial risk**.

- **Kjeldahl protein formula:** $\%N \times 6.25 = \%\text{Protein}$ (assumes 16% N in food proteins).

- **°Brix (TSS)** is measured refractometrically and reflects *apparent* sucrose; not the true sugar content.

- **CIE L\*a\*b\*** is the universal color space. Derived parameters:
  - Hue $h^{\circ} = \tan^{-1}(b^{\ast}/a^{\ast})$
  - Chroma $C^{\ast} = \sqrt{a^{\ast 2} + b^{\ast 2}}$
  - ΔE\* quantifies batch-to-batch color drift.

- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** uses a **two-bite compression** test on a Texture Analyzer. Standard parameters: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience, Adhesiveness.

- **Non-destructive methods** (NIR, X-ray, MRI, Computer Vision, E-nose) are increasingly industry-relevant for **100% online inspection** and reduced post-harvest loss.

- **Q₁₀ model:** $\text{SL}_{T_2} = \text{SL}_{T_1} / Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$. Use to extrapolate accelerated shelf-life data to lower storage temperatures.

- **Sensory evaluation** follows ISO 8589 booth standards; **9-point Hedonic scale** is the most widely cited affective test in post-harvest shelf-life studies.

- **Always quote units explicitly** — % w.b. vs % d.b., N vs kgf, °Brix vs % sucrose, CFU/g vs MPN/g.

- **Microbiological methods** to remember: TPC (35 °C / 48 h), Yeast-Mold (25 °C / 3–5 d), Coliforms (MPN, 37 °C / 24–48 h). All conform to **FSSAI** and **ICMSF** standard protocols.

- **Hunter L\*a\*b\* vs. CIE L\*a\*b\*:** Hunter is geometrically related to CIE by cube-root transforms but is not identical. The **CIE system is preferred** in international publications and KTU answer sheets.

- **Instrument calibration** matters: pH meter → 3-point calibration (pH 4, 7, 9.2); Refractometer → distilled water (0 °Brix); Colorimeter → standard white tile.

- **Statistical treatment:** All sensory data must be analysed by **ANOVA** (panel differences) and **PCA** (correlated attributes) before drawing conclusions in the KTU exam.

- **Last but not least:** When asked to "discuss" any quality method, structure your answer as **Principle → Procedure → Calculation → Industrial Application → Limitation.** Examiners reward this 5-point template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Destructive and Non-Destructive Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Destructive and Non-Destructive Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Destructive Tests — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Destructive Tests (DT)** are analytical quality evaluation procedures in which the food sample under examination is **physically altered, chemically reacted with, or completely consumed** during the test, rendering it unfit for further use, sale, or consumption. The integrity of the sample is permanently compromised to obtain quantitative data about its composition, structure, or microbial status.

In the context of post-harvest food quality assessment, destructive tests are the **classical, gold-standard methods** used in food testing laboratories. They involve subjecting a representative sample of the commodity (fruits, vegetables, grains, dairy, meat, or processed foods) to chemical reagents, mechanical pressure, thermal treatment, or microbiological culture media, after which the sample is destroyed.

### Common Examples of Destructive Tests in Post-Harvest Food Analysis
- **Titratable Acidity (TA)** — acid-base neutralization destroys the sample
- **Total Soluble Solids (TSS / °Brix)** — refractometry (technically minimally destructive but juice is extracted)
- **Pectin content assay** — sample is hydrolyzed and reacted
- **Moisture content by oven drying** — sample loses water permanently
- **Protein content by Kjeldahl method** — sample is digested in concentrated H₂SO₄
- **Microbial load (Total Plate Count, TPC)** — sample is homogenized and plated
- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** — sample is compressed to failure
- **Firmness by penetrometer / Magness-Taylor test** — probe penetrates flesh
- **Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) by titration with 2,6-dichlorophenolindophenol**

## 1.2 Non-Destructive Tests — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Destructive Tests (NDT)** — also termed **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** or **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — are quality assessment techniques that **measure physical, chemical, or biological properties of a food sample without altering its physical or chemical state, structure, or composition**, thereby allowing the same sample to be graded, sorted, stored, sold, or consumed after testing.

NDT is the **backbone of modern post-harvest sorting, grading, and shelf-life monitoring** because it enables **100% inspection** of produce without any wastage. These techniques exploit the interaction of the food matrix with various forms of energy — electromagnetic radiation, mechanical vibration, acoustic waves, electrical fields, or nuclear magnetic fields.

### Common Examples of Non-Destructive Tests
- **Visual / Imaging methods** — Computer Vision, RGB imaging, hyperspectral imaging
- **Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS)** — measures absorption in 780–2500 nm range
- **Visible-NIR Spectroscopy (Vis-NIRS)** — 400–1100 nm
- **X-ray imaging / Computed Tomography (CT)** — detects internal defects, hollow heart
- **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — internal moisture and oil distribution
- **Ultrasound (acoustic)** — measures firmness, ripeness via sound velocity
- **Electronic Nose (E-Nose)** — volatile aroma profiling
- **Electronic Tongue (E-Tongue)** — taste profiling
- **Laser-induced fluorescence** — chlorophyll and surface defect detection
- **Dielectric / Impedance spectroscopy** — moisture, salt content
- **Acoustic firmness / Stiffness measurement** — tap-and-response
- **Chlorophyll fluorescence (Fv/Fm)** — photosynthetic viability of fresh produce

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Student-Friendly Intuition):**

Imagine you go to a doctor for a health check-up.

- **Destructive Test = "Biopsy"** → The doctor takes a piece of your tissue, slices it, stains it, and looks at it under a microscope. You cannot put that tissue back. The sample is gone forever, but you get the most accurate diagnosis.

- **Non-Destructive Test = "X-ray or MRI scan"** → The doctor shines invisible waves through your body, captures an image, and walks away. Your body is untouched. You walk out alive and well, and the doctor still gets a very good picture of what's going on inside.

**In post-harvest food science:**
- A farmer who wants to know the **exact sugar content** of an apple can squeeze out the juice and measure °Brix → **Destructive**.
- A packing-house that wants to grade **10,000 apples per hour** for sweetness, firmness, and internal browning uses **NIR spectroscopy** as apples roll on a conveyor belt → **Non-Destructive**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Mental Model:** Destructive tests = *high accuracy but slow, lab-based, small sample size*. Non-Destructive tests = *slightly lower accuracy per sample but fast, in-line, and can test 100% of the produce*. Both are **complementary**, not competitors.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| NIR wavelength for fruit sweetness | **780–2500 nm** |
| Vis-NIR range | **400–1100 nm** |
| Ultrasound frequency for firmness | **20–100 kHz** |
| Acoustic tap frequency for fruit | **100–2000 Hz** |
| Chlorophyll fluorescence Fv/Fm (healthy) | **0.80–0.85** |
| Minimum sample size for destructive lab tests | **≥ 5 fruits (representative)** |
| Line speed for industrial NDT sorters | **5–30 fruits/second** |
| Penetrometer tip diameter (Magness-Taylor) | **7.9 mm (5/16 inch)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Theoretical Framework: Destructive vs Non-Destructive

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Basis of Destructive Tests

Destructive tests are grounded in **classical wet chemistry and mechanical engineering principles**. They follow the analytical chemistry paradigm:

**Sample → Homogenization → Reagent Reaction → Quantification (mass, volume, absorbance, electrical signal) → Calculation**

The core theoretical pillars are:

- **Stoichiometric reactions** (acid-base, redox, complexometric) for chemical composition
- **Mechanical stress-strain relationships** for texture
- **Microbial growth kinetics** (Lag phase → Log phase → Stationary → Death) for safety
- **Thermal and mass-transfer balances** (oven drying, distillation) for moisture

### 2.1.2 Theoretical Basis of Non-Destructive Tests

NDT is built on the principle of **energy-matter interaction**. A probe of energy (light, sound, X-ray, electric field) is sent into the sample; the **reflected, transmitted, absorbed, or scattered** energy carries a signature of the internal composition.

The five fundamental interactions are:

1. **Absorption** (Beer-Lambert Law) — used in NIR, MIR spectroscopy
2. **Scattering** (Rayleigh, Mie, Raman) — used in laser-based methods
3. **Reflection** (diffuse, specular) — used in imaging
4. **Resonance** (nuclear, electronic) — used in MRI, EPR
5. **Mechanical vibration response** — used in acoustic and ultrasonic methods

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Each Method Works

### Step 1 — Sample Interaction
The food sample is exposed to a controlled physical stimulus (light beam, mechanical probe, sound wave).

### Step 2 — Signal Generation
The sample responds by absorbing, reflecting, transmitting, or vibrating. This response is encoded as an **electrical or optical signal**.

### Step 3 — Signal Capture
A detector (photodiode, spectrometer, microphone, piezoelectric crystal) converts the response into a digital signal.

### Step 4 — Calibration & Quantification
The signal is compared against a **pre-built calibration model** (chemometric model in NIR, ML model in imaging) developed using paired destructive reference data.

### Step 5 — Decision / Sorting
The predicted value triggers an **accept/reject or grade decision** in industrial sorters (air-jet ejectors remove defective items in <50 ms).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this formula sheet — these are the equations that appear most frequently in KTU End Semester Examinations for this topic.**

| # | Formula | Name / Concept | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | **Beer-Lambert Law** | $A$ = absorbance (unitless), $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ = concentration (mol/L), $l$ = path length (cm) | Basis of all spectroscopic NDT (NIR, MIR, UV-Vis) |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | **Lambert-Beer Exponential Decay** | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\mu$ = absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹), $x$ = thickness (cm) | X-ray and NIR transmission through produce |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{(n_1 - n_2)^2}{(n_1 + n_2)^2}$ | **Fresnel Reflection Coefficient** (perpendicular incidence) | $n_1, n_2$ = refractive indices of the two media | Optical reflection at fruit skin |
| 4 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | **Acoustic Wave Velocity in Solid** | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) | Ultrasound and acoustic firmness |
| 5 | $F = \dfrac{mg}{A}$ | **Force / Pressure** | $F$ = force (N), $m$ = mass (kg), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $A$ = area (m²) | Penetrometer firmness |
| 6 | $TSS\ (\%)\ =\ ^\circ Brix$ | **Total Soluble Solids (refractometric)** | Measured in degrees Brix (sucrose equivalent) | Fruit sweetness, juice quality |
| 7 | $\% MC = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$ | **Moisture Content (Oven Method)** | $W_i$ = initial weight (g), $W_f$ = final weight after drying (g) | Grains, flours, dried foods |
| 8 | $\text{Firmness}\ (N) = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{probe}}$ | **Firmness / Stress at Bioyield** | $F_{max}$ = peak force (N), $A_{probe}$ = probe cross-section (m²) | Texture Profile Analysis |
| 9 | $\dfrac{F_v}{F_m} = \dfrac{(F_m - F_0)}{F_m}$ | **Chlorophyll Fluorescence Ratio** | $F_v$ = variable fluorescence, $F_m$ = max fluorescence, $F_0$ = minimum fluorescence | Photosynthetic viability of fresh produce |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | **Mechanical Stress (penetration)** | $\sigma$ = stress (Pa), $F$ = applied force (N), $A$ = area (m²) | Compression / puncture tests |
| 11 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | **Mechanical Strain** | $\varepsilon$ = strain (unitless), $\Delta L$ = deformation (m), $L_0$ = original length (m) | Texture analysis |
| 12 | $\text{Titratable Acidity}\ (\%) = \dfrac{V \times N \times E}{W} \times 100$ | **TA Calculation** | $V$ = titrant volume (mL), $N$ = normality, $E$ = equivalent weight of acid, $W$ = sample weight | Fruit acidity, vinegar |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value-style mathematical operations (e.g., $|x|$) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX so that the vertical pipe `|` does not break the markdown table syntax. Example: $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $|x|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NDT in Industry:** Enables **Industry 4.0 smart sorting lines** in pack-houses (TOMRA, Ellips, Spectra-Food) at throughputs of **>10 tonnes/hour** with **>95% accuracy**.
- **Destructive tests in QC Labs:** Provide the **reference data** to build and validate NDT chemometric models (PLS, PCR, PCA-based calibration).
- **Combined Approach:** A modern food industry uses NDT for **online screening** + destructive tests for **periodic verification** — this is the ISO 17025 / HACCP-compliant workflow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Beer-Lambert Law (Foundation of All Spectroscopic NDT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Beer-Lambert Law is the **single most important equation** for NDT spectroscopy. It is derived from two independent physical principles that combine multiplicatively.

### Step 1 — Bouguer's Law (Lambert's Law)
A French mathematician, Pierre Bouguer, observed in 1729 that **each infinitesimally thin layer (dx)** of a medium absorbs an equal *fraction* of the incident light, not an equal amount.

Consider a thin layer of thickness $dx$ absorbing light passing through it. Let $I$ be the intensity after traversing this layer.

The fraction of light absorbed in the infinitesimal layer is proportional to the layer thickness:

$$-\dfrac{dI}{I} = k \cdot dx$$

**Row 1 explanation:** The negative sign indicates intensity is decreasing. The proportionality constant $k$ depends on the medium.

### Step 2 — Integrate Across the Full Path Length
Integrating from $x = 0$ (where intensity is $I_0$) to $x = l$ (where intensity is $I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -k \int_{0}^{l} dx$$

This gives us:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k \cdot l$$

**Row 2 explanation:** Standard calculus integration of $1/I$ yields $\ln(I)$, and the constant of integration is folded into $I_0$.

### Step 3 — Exponentiate to Recover the Exponential Decay Form

$$\dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k \cdot l}$$

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot l}$$

**Row 3 explanation:** This is the exponential attenuation form. Now we apply Beer's law.

### Step 4 — Beer's Law (Concentration Dependence)
August Beer (1852) showed that the absorption coefficient $k$ is **proportional to the concentration** $c$ of the absorbing species:

$$k = 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c$$

**Row 4 explanation:** The factor **2.303** converts natural logarithm (ln) to base-10 logarithm (log₁₀), because absorbance is conventionally defined in base 10. Here $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient (units: L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹).

### Step 5 — Substitute Back and Define Absorbance

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-2.303 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$

Taking the base-10 logarithm and defining **Absorbance** $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$:

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Row 5 explanation:** This is the final Beer-Lambert equation. It states that **absorbance is linearly proportional to concentration**, which is why a simple linear calibration can quantify sugar, water, or protein content from spectral data.

### Step 6 — Application in Fruit Quality
In a non-destructive NIR measurement of an apple's sugar content:

$$A_{NIR}\ (\text{sugar band at 850 nm}) = \varepsilon_{sucrose} \cdot c_{sucrose} \cdot l_{fruit}$$

By measuring $A$ and knowing $\varepsilon$ and $l$ (the light path length inside the fruit), we can compute $c$ — the sugar concentration — **without squeezing the apple**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Validity Limits:**
> - Valid only for **dilute solutions** ($c \lesssim 0.01$ M). At high concentrations, solute-solute interactions cause deviations.
> - Valid for **monochromatic** light. Polychromatic light causes curvature.
> - Scattering and fluorescence can cause **apparent deviations** — corrected by chemometric pre-treatments (SNV, MSC, derivatives).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Acoustic Firmness from Stiffness Coefficient

The acoustic firmness technique measures a fruit's response to a gentle mechanical tap.

### Step 1 — Model the Fruit as a Stiff Sphere
A fruit of mass $m$ and equivalent radius $R$ is treated as a **uniform elastic sphere** with Young's modulus $E$ and density $\rho$.

### Step 2 — Natural Frequency of a Free-Free Elastic Sphere
The fundamental resonant frequency $f$ of a free elastic sphere (no constraint, vibrating in air) is given by Lamb's equation:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$$

**Row 1 explanation:** This is derived by solving the Navier equation of linear elasticity in spherical coordinates, with the boundary condition of zero traction at the surface.

### Step 3 — Define the Stiffness Coefficient
Multiplying both sides by $(2\pi R)$ and squaring:

$$(2\pi R f)^2 = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$$

The **stiffness coefficient** $S$ is defined as:

$$S = f^2 \cdot m^{\tfrac{2}{3}}$$

where $m$ is the fruit mass (proportional to $\rho R^3$).

### Step 4 — Relate to the Speed of Sound Inside the Fruit
Substituting $E = \rho v^2$ (where $v$ is the speed of sound) into the frequency equation:

$$f = \dfrac{v}{2\pi R}$$

$$v = 2\pi R f$$

**Row 2 explanation:** This shows that a fruit with high $f$ (high-pitched sound when tapped) is firm, while a soft fruit (over-ripe) has a low $f$ (dull sound).

### Step 5 — Calibration with Destructive Magness-Taylor Test
The acoustic stiffness $S$ is correlated with the **destructive** Magness-Taylor penetrometer reading $F_{MT}$ (in Newtons) using a linear regression:

$$F_{MT}\ (N) = a + b \cdot S$$

**Row 3 explanation:** Coefficients $a$ and $b$ are determined empirically for each cultivar (e.g., 'Golden Delicious' apple, 'Cavendish' banana). Once calibrated, $S$ gives a non-destructive prediction of $F_{MT}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing DT vs NDT

| Selection Criterion | Destructive Test | Non-Destructive Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Sample preservation needed** | ✗ No | ✓ Yes |
| **Accuracy (gold standard)** | ✓✓✓ Highest | ✓✓ High (if calibrated) |
| **Speed (per sample)** | ✗ Slow (minutes-hours) | ✓✓✓ Fast (ms-s) |
| **Throughput (industrial)** | ✗ Low (lab-based) | ✓✓✓ High (in-line) |
| **Cost per test** | ✗ Moderate-High (reagents, lab) | ✓ Low (after setup) |
| **Capital cost (instrument)** | ✓ Low | ✗ Moderate-High |
| **Internal vs surface** | ✓ Both (homogenized) | ✓✓ NDT can probe internal (NIR, MRI) |
| **Suitable for regulatory** | ✓ Yes (official method) | ✓ Complementary only |
| **Real-time monitoring** | ✗ No | ✓ Yes |
| **Suitable for low-value bulk** | ✗ No | ✓ Yes (grains via NIR) |
| **Operator skill required** | ✓ Moderate (trained chemist) | ✗ Variable (chemometrics expert for model building) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "compare DT and NDT," students must explicitly mention that **NDT methods are calibrated against DT reference values** — they are not independent. The DT provides the "ground truth" labels, and NDT provides the "high-throughput predictor." This **mutual dependence** is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Question:** A juice sample is measured with a refractometer. The initial mass of a fruit is 185.4 g. After oven drying at 105 °C for 24 hours, the mass is 22.7 g. Calculate the moisture content (% wet basis).

### Solution

$$\% MC\ (\text{wet basis}) = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100$$

Substituting $W_i = 185.4$ g and $W_f = 22.7$ g:

$$\% MC = \dfrac{185.4 - 22.7}{185.4} \times 100$$

$$\% MC = \dfrac{162.7}{185.4} \times 100$$

$$\% MC = 0.8776 \times 100 = 87.76\ \%$$

**Answer:** The moisture content is **87.76 % (w.b.)**, which is typical for a fresh fruit such as apple or pear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **% MC wet basis** (above) with **% MC dry basis**. The dry-basis formula is:
>
> $$\% MC\ (\text{dry basis}) = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_f} \times 100$$
>
> For the same data: $(162.7 / 22.7) \times 100 = 716.7\ \%$ (dry basis). **Wet basis is always ≤ 100 %; dry basis can exceed 100 %.** This is a frequently tested distinction.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Food Quality Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Food Quality Tests"]:::root --> B["Destructive Tests DT"]:::dt
    A --> C["Non Destructive Tests NDT"]:::ndt

    B --> B1["Chemical Tests"]:::dt
    B --> B2["Physical Tests"]:::dt
    B --> B3["Microbiological Tests"]:::dt
    B --> B4["Sensory Tests"]:::dt

    B1 --> B1a["Titratable Acidity"]
    B1 --> B1b["Ascorbic Acid DCPIP"]
    B1 --> B1c["Kjeldahl Protein"]
    B1 --> B1d["Pectin Assay"]

    B2 --> B2a["Magness Taylor Firmness"]
    B2 --> B2b["TPA Texture Profile"]
    B2 --> B2c["Oven Moisture"]
    B2 --> B2d["Refractometer TSS Brix"]

    B3 --> B3a["Total Plate Count TPC"]
    B3 --> B3b["Yeast Mold Count"]
    B3 --> B3c["Coliform MPN"]
    B3 --> B3d["Pathogen Detection"]

    B4 --> B4a["Hedonic Panel"]
    B4 --> B4b["Descriptive Analysis"]
    B4 --> B4c["Discriminative Test Triangle"]

    C --> C1["Optical Methods"]:::ndt
    C --> C2["Acoustic Methods"]:::ndt

    C1 --> C1a["NIR Spectroscopy 780 to 2500 nm"]
    C1 --> C1b["Vis NIR 400 to 1100 nm"]
    C1 --> C1c["Fluorescence Chlorophyll"]
    C1 --> C1d["Laser Induced Breakdown"]

    C2 --> C2a["Acoustic Tap Firmness"]
    C2 --> C2b["Ultrasound 20 to 100 kHz"]

    C3 --> C3a["X ray Imaging CT"]
    C3 --> C3b["MRI"]
    C3 --> C3c["Dielectric Impedance"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Workflow of an Industrial NIR Sorting Line (NDT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Conveyor Belt<br/>Inlet Hopper"]:::hw --> B["Singulator<br/>Align Fruits"]:::hw

    G --> H2["Outlet 2 Class 2"]:::out
    G --> H3["Outlet 3 Reject"]:::out

    E -.calibration.-> I["Reference Lab<br/>Destructive Tests"]:::lab
    I -.reference values.-> E

    classDef out fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Calibration Loop — How NDT Relies on DT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Representative Food Samples N=200"]:::root --> B["Non Destructive Scan<br/>NIR Vis NIR MRI etc"]:::ndt
    A --> C["Destructive Reference<br/>Lab Wet Chemistry"]:::dt
    B --> D["Spectral Data Matrix X"]:::data
    C --> E["Reference Values Y"]:::data
    D --> F["Chemometric Algorithm<br/>PLS PCR ANN"]:::soft
    E --> F
    F --> G["Calibration Model<br/>Y = f X"]:::soft
    G --> H["Validation Set<br/>Independent Samples"]:::valid
    H --> I{"Accuracy Acceptable<br/>R2 greater than 0.85"}:::soft
    I -->|Yes| J["Deploy Model<br/>In Line Sorting"]:::deploy
    I -->|No| K["Recalibrate<br/>Add More Samples"]:::soft
    K --> F

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef dt fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef ndt fill:#059669,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#6366f1,stroke:#312e81,color:#ffffff
    classDef soft fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    classDef valid fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff
    classDef deploy fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Acoustic Firmness Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Microphone<br/>High Sensitivity"]:::sensor --> B["Signal Amplifier<br/>Gain 40 dB"]:::hw
    B --> C["Analog to Digital Converter<br/>44.1 kHz Sampling"]:::hw
    C --> D["FFT Processor<br/>Frequency Domain"]:::soft
    D --> E["Peak Frequency Detection f max"]:::soft
    E --> F["Stiffness Calculator<br/>S = f2 x m to 2/3"]:::soft
    F --> G["Display Unit<br/>Firmness Index"]:::hw
    F --> H["Database<br/>Cultivar Specific Calibration"]:::data

    I["Mechanical Tapper<br/>Hammer Solenoid"]:::hw --> J["Fruit Sample<br/>Resting on Cusp"]:::sensor
    J --> A

    classDef sensor fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef hw fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef soft fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation for KTU exams:** When asked to "draw/diagram a non-destructive quality evaluation system," students should always show the **calibration loop** (as in 4.3) and emphasize the **DT-NDT interdependency**. A common error is drawing NDT as standalone — this loses marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, 3 Marks]**

**Q: Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive tests with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Destructive Test (DT) | Non-Destructive Test (NDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sample is altered/destroyed | Sample is preserved intact |
| Examples | (i) Magness-Taylor penetrometer firmness; (ii) Kjeldahl nitrogen/protein | (i) NIR spectroscopy; (ii) X-ray / Computer vision imaging |
| Speed | Slow (lab-based) | Fast (in-line capable) |
| Application | Reference / regulatory | Industrial sorting / screening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution Tip (KTU pattern):** Definition: 1 mark; Examples (2 + 2): 1 mark; Comparison: 1 mark. Always include **2 examples each** — students often write only 1 and lose a mark.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, 3 Marks]**

**Q: List any four non-destructive techniques used for post-harvest quality evaluation of fruits.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Near-Infrared (NIR) Spectroscopy** — measures sugar, acidity, moisture in 780–2500 nm range.
2. **Acoustic firmness / Tap-test** — measures stiffness coefficient by resonant frequency after a gentle tap.
3. **X-ray / CT imaging** — detects internal defects like hollow heart, seed maturity, insect damage.
4. **Computer vision (RGB imaging)** — measures size, shape, color, surface defects for grading.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the principle, working, and applications of Near-Infrared (NIR) Spectroscopy as a non-destructive technique for post-harvest quality evaluation of fruits and vegetables.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Discuss the Magness-Taylor penetrometer test and Titratable Acidity (TA) test as destructive methods for assessing fruit quality. Compare their advantages and limitations.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): NIR Spectroscopy

**Principle:**
NIR spectroscopy is based on the **Beer-Lambert Law** $A = \varepsilon c l$. When NIR light (wavelength 780–2500 nm) is incident on a fruit, specific molecular bonds — **O–H (water, sugars), C–H (organic compounds), N–H (proteins)** — absorb photons at characteristic wavelengths. The absorbed wavelengths form a "fingerprint spectrum" that is mathematically correlated (via chemometric models such as **Partial Least Squares Regression — PLSR**) with the chemical composition of the fruit.

**Working:**
1. **Illumination** — A tungsten-halogen lamp emits broadband NIR light.
2. **Interaction** — Light penetrates 5–25 mm into the fruit flesh, where it is partially absorbed and partially diffusely reflected.
3. **Detection** — A spectrometer (with InGaAs detector for 800–1700 nm, or extended-InGaAs / PbS for up to 2500 nm) records the reflected spectrum.
4. **Pre-processing** — Raw spectra are corrected for scatter (SNV, MSC) and baseline drift (derivatives).
5. **Prediction** — The pre-processed spectrum is fed into a **pre-calibrated PLSR model** that outputs the predicted values of SSC (°Brix), TA, firmness, dry matter, etc.
6. **Decision** — The predicted value is compared with a threshold; air-jet ejectors sort the fruit accordingly.

**Applications:**
- Online sorting of apples, mangoes, kiwifruit, peaches, tomatoes for sweetness (SSC).
- Dry matter measurement in onions and potatoes (for processing-grade classification).
- Internal quality (watercore, hollow heart) detection in apples and pears.
- Maturity assessment in mangoes (correlation with starch breakdown).
- Oil content in olives and avocados.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Magness-Taylor and TA

**Magness-Taylor (MT) Penetrometer Test:**
- A **cylindrical probe of 7.9 mm (5/16 inch) diameter** is pressed into the peeled flesh of a fruit (after removing a thin disc of skin) at a constant speed.
- The **maximum force (in Newtons or kgf) required to penetrate 8 mm** is recorded.
- The fruit is **destroyed** (punctured).
- Gives a direct measure of flesh firmness, which is inversely related to ripening.

**Titratable Acidity (TA) Test:**
- A known mass of fruit juice is titrated with a standard alkali (typically **0.1 N NaOH**) to a phenolphthalein endpoint (pH 8.2).
- The volume of NaOH used is converted to % citric acid (for citrus), % malic acid (for apples), or % tartaric acid (for grapes).
- Formula: $\% TA = (V \times N \times E \times 100) / W$ where $V$ = titrant volume, $N$ = normality, $E$ = equivalent weight, $W$ = sample mass.
- The juice sample is consumed (destroyed) during titration.

**Comparison — Advantages & Limitations:**

| Aspect | Magness-Taylor | Titratable Acidity |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Mechanical firmness | Chemical acidity |
| Speed | ~30 s per fruit | ~5 min per sample |
| Skill | Moderate | Requires titration skill |
| Sample destruction | Punctured fruit | Juice consumed |
| Reference use | Calibration for acoustic NDT | Calibration for NIR TA model |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the probe diameter (7.9 mm)** — frequently asked. Always state it.
> 2. **Using kgf but not converting to N** — examiners expect SI units; conversion factor is 1 kgf = 9.81 N.
> 3. **In TA, forgetting to mention the endpoint indicator** (phenolphthalein, pH 8.2).
> 4. **Omitting the formula** for % TA — KTU expects the equation on the answer sheet.
> 5. **Not linking MT to acoustic NDT calibration** — the connect-the-dots question is a favourite 2-mark bonus.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an online NIR-based sorting system for fruits. Explain how the chemometric calibration model is developed using destructive reference data.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Write notes on (i) Electronic Nose, (ii) Hyperspectral Imaging, and (iii) Chlorophyll Fluorescence as non-destructive techniques for post-harvest quality assessment.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Online NIR Sorting System

**Block Diagram:**

The complete sorting line consists of: **Conveyor → Singulator → NIR illumination → Spectrometer → Computer with PLSR model → Decision controller → Air-jet ejectors → Multiple outlets** (premium, Class 2, reject).

**Working:**
- Fruits are **singulated** (separated into single rows) on a conveyor belt.
- They pass through a NIR illumination chamber; the reflected spectrum from each fruit is captured in <50 ms.
- The spectrum is compared with the **calibration model** (built earlier using 200–500 fruits scanned by NIR and measured by HPLC for sugar, by titration for acidity, etc.).
- The model outputs predicted °Brix, TA, and dry matter. A grading rule (e.g., °Brix ≥ 13 for "Premium") triggers a binary decision.
- At the ejector station, a **high-pressure air jet (typically 4–6 bar)** removes reject fruits within 30 ms into a separate conveyor.

**Calibration Development (DT-NDT Link):**
1. Collect **N = 200–500 representative samples** of the cultivar.
2. Acquire NIR spectra for all samples (X matrix).
3. Measure the same samples by **destructive reference methods** (HPLC, titration, oven drying) → Y matrix.
4. Split into **calibration set (70%)** and **validation set (30%)**.
5. Develop a **PLSR model** that minimizes the prediction error.
6. Validate using **R², RMSEP, and bias**. Typical acceptance: R² > 0.85, RMSEP < 0.5 °Brix.
7. If accuracy is poor, **expand the sample set, recheck reference data, or apply different pre-treatments** (Savitzky-Golay derivatives, SNV).

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Notes on E-Nose, HSI, and Chlorophyll Fluorescence

**(i) Electronic Nose (E-Nose):**
- An array of **non-specific gas sensors** (Metal-Oxide Semi-conductor, MOS; conducting polymer; quartz crystal microbalance) detects volatile organic compounds (VOCs) released by the fruit.
- Each sensor responds differently to different VOCs → a **unique "smell-print"** is generated.
- Pattern recognition algorithms (**PCA, LDA, ANN**) classify samples as fresh, ripe, over-ripe, or rotten.
- **Application:** Early detection of fungal infection in stored grains; ripening stage of climacteric fruits (banana, mango).

**(ii) Hyperspectral Imaging (HSI):**
- Combines **spectroscopy + imaging** — produces a 3-D data cube: (x, y, λ).
- Each pixel carries a full spectrum (typically 400–1000 nm or 900–1700 nm).
- Enables **spatial localization of chemical constituents** — e.g., a bruise on an apple shows a different spectrum than healthy tissue.
- **Application:** Detection of surface defects, internal bruising, fecal contamination on poultry, foreign bodies in food.

**(iii) Chlorophyll Fluorescence (Fv/Fm):**
- Measures the **maximum quantum yield of Photosystem II** in chlorophyll.
- Healthy green produce: $F_v / F_m \approx 0.80 - 0.85$.
- Stressed, senescent, or aged produce: $F_v / F_m$ drops below 0.7.
- A **non-contact fluorometer** (e.g., Handy PEA, PAM) shines a saturating light pulse and measures the resulting fluorescence.
- **Application:** Shelf-life prediction, post-harvest stress assessment, freshness of leafy vegetables (spinach, lettuce) and broccoli.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Drawing the NIR line without a singulator** — examiners specifically look for this component.
> 2. **Confusing RMSEP (Root Mean Square Error of Prediction) with RMSEC** (calibration) — these are different metrics evaluated on different sample sets.
> 3. **In E-Nose notes, writing "it smells"** — this is unscientific. Use the term **"voltammetric response"** or **"sensor array output"**.
> 4. **HSI cube dimensions** — students must specify **(x, y, λ)** explicitly; missing λ loses a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the Fv/Fm reference range (0.80–0.85)** — this is the most-asked numerical fact in this module.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Read this the night before the KTU exam.**

### Destructive Tests (DT)
- ☐ **Definition:** Sample is destroyed/altered permanently.
- ☐ **Examples:** Magness-Taylor penetrometer, Kjeldahl, TA titration, Brix, oven moisture, TPA, microbial plate count.
- ☐ **Probe diameter (MT):** **7.9 mm (5/16 inch)**.
- ☐ **Penetration depth (MT):** **8 mm**.
- ☐ **TA endpoint:** Phenolphthalein, **pH 8.2**.
- ☐ **TA formula:** $\%TA = (V \times N \times E \times 100)/W$.
- ☐ **Moisture (wet basis):** $\%MC = (W_i - W_f)/W_i \times 100$.
- ☐ **Moisture (dry basis):** $\%MC = (W_i - W_f)/W_f \times 100$.
- ☐ **Use:** Regulatory reference, calibration reference for NDT.

### Non-Destructive Tests (NDT)
- ☐ **Definition:** Sample preserved; 100% inspection possible.
- ☐ **NIR range:** **780–2500 nm**.
- ☐ **Vis-NIR range:** **400–1100 nm**.
- ☐ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — foundation equation.
- ☐ **Acoustic stiffness:** $S = f^2 \cdot m^{2/3}$, where $f$ is the resonance frequency.
- ☐ **Chlorophyll fluorescence Fv/Fm:** Healthy = **0.80–0.85**; Senescent < **0.7**.
- ☐ **E-Nose** detects VOCs via sensor array; not a "smell" in the colloquial sense.
- ☐ **HSI cube** = (x, y, λ) — gives spatial + spectral info.
- ☐ **MRI** — non-ionizing; safe for repeated use; detects internal moisture/oil.
- ☐ **X-ray / CT** — detects internal defects (hollow heart, insect damage).
- ☐ **Ultrasound** (20–100 kHz) — measures firmness via sound velocity.

### Critical Inter-Relationship (Most-Asked Concept)
- ☐ **NDT is calibrated against DT.** Without DT reference values, NDT cannot quantify.
- ☐ NDT enables **high-throughput, in-line, 100% inspection**; DT provides **accuracy**.
- ☐ **Combined approach** (DT for QC, NDT for production) is the **ISO 17025 / HACCP-compliant workflow**.

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ **Do not write "kgf" without converting to Newtons** (1 kgf = 9.81 N).
- ☐ **Do not confuse % MC wet basis vs dry basis** — wet basis is the more commonly asked.
- ☐ **Do not say "E-Nose smells the food"** — say "the E-Nose sensor array generates a voltammetric response pattern to volatile organic compounds."
- ☐ **Do not draw NIR system without singulator** — examiners expect it.
- ☐ **Always mention chemometric models (PLSR, PCA) when discussing NIR** — bare "spectrum" loses marks.

### Memory Hook: **"D-I-R-T-S-F-C-H"** for NDT types
- **D** — Dielectric / Impedance
- **I** — Imaging (RGB, Hyperspectral, X-ray, MRI, Thermal)
- **R** — Resonance (Acoustic tap, NMR/MRI)
- **T** — Tap-test (Acoustic firmness)
- **S** — Spectroscopy (NIR, Vis-NIR, MIR, Raman, Fluorescence)
- **F** — Fluorescence (Chlorophyll, Laser-induced)
- **C** — Chemical-sensor arrays (E-Nose, E-Tongue)
- **H** — Hyperspectral

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Destructive tests give the truth; Non-destructive tests give the throughput. One without the other is incomplete in modern post-harvest food quality assurance."* — Memorize this line; it can earn full marks on the "differentiate" type questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT754 - POST HARVEST SPOILAGE & PHYSIOLOGY OF FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical and Chemical Tests** in post-harvest food science constitute a systematic battery of **objective, instrument-based, and laboratory-grade analytical procedures** used to quantify the **qualitative degradation** and **quantitative compositional changes** that occur in foods from the moment of harvest through storage, processing, and distribution. These tests bridge the gap between **sensory perception** (which is subjective) and **biochemical reality** (which is measurable), providing reproducible numerical indices of spoilage, freshness, maturity, and safety.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Physical and Chemical Tests are categorized as:

- **Physical Tests** → Measure **extrinsic, mechanical, optical, thermal, and rheological properties** of foods (color, texture, density, moisture, viscosity) without altering their chemical identity.
- **Chemical Tests** → Measure **intrinsic molecular composition and reaction products** (pH, acidity, sugars, fats, proteins, ash, respiration gases, lipid oxidation products) using wet-lab reagents, titrations, spectrophotometry, and chromatography.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Doctor's Check-up for Food" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a food product is a **patient** arriving at a hospital. The **Physical Tests** are like the **vital-signs check-up** — heart rate (respiration rate), body temperature (thermal properties), body weight (mass/density), and skin tone (color). They tell you *how the food looks and feels* from the outside.
>
> The **Chemical Tests** are like the **blood test reports** — pH (blood pH), titratable acidity (lactate levels), sugar content (blood glucose), peroxide value (cholesterol), and protein content (hemoglobin). They reveal *what is happening inside the food at the molecular level* — invisible degradation, enzymatic breakdown, and microbial metabolite accumulation.
>
> Just as a doctor cannot diagnose a disease from a single test, a food scientist cannot declare a food "spoiled" from one parameter. The **physical and chemical tests together** form the diagnostic panel that quantifies post-harvest spoilage and physiological decline.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

The following **standardized metrics** are mandated in the KTU 2024 Scheme for evaluation:

- **Moisture Content** measured in **% (wet basis)** or **% (dry basis)**
- **pH** measured on the dimensionless **Sorensen Scale (0–14)**
- **Titratable Acidity** expressed as **% citric acid (for fruits) / % lactic acid (for dairy) / % acetic acid (for vinegar)**
- **Total Soluble Solids (TSS)** measured in **°Brix (1 °Brix = 1% sucrose w/w at 20 °C)**
- **Texture** measured in **Newtons (N)** for force, **mm** for penetration depth
- **Respiration Rate** measured in **mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹** or **mg CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹**
- **Peroxide Value (PV)** measured in **milliequivalents of O₂ per kg of fat (meq O₂/kg)**
- **Free Fatty Acids (FFA)** expressed as **% oleic acid**
- **K-value (Fish Freshness Index)** expressed as a **percentage (%)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to be proficient in the **principle, procedure, and interpretation** of at least 8–10 physical and chemical tests that are routinely used in food quality control laboratories and post-harvest research stations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Physical and Chemical Tests

The tests are organized into a structured hierarchy:

#### A. Physical Tests

- **Morphological / Dimensional Tests**
  - Size (length, diameter) — Vernier caliper
  - Shape index — $\text{Shape Index} = \frac{L}{D}$ where $L$ = length, $D$ = diameter
  - Weight (mass loss during storage) — Analytical balance
  - Volume — Water displacement / pycnometer

- **Color Tests**
  - Visual color scoring
  - Hunter $L^*a^*b^*$ color space measurement
  - $\Delta E$ (Total Color Difference) calculation
  - Chlorophyll / Carotenoid pigment assay

- **Textural / Rheological Tests**
  - Penetration test (Magness-Taylor pressure tester)
  - Compression test (Texture Profile Analysis - TPA)
  - Shear / puncture test
  - Crispness, hardness, adhesiveness, cohesiveness indices

- **Gravimetric Tests**
  - Specific gravity $\rho_{rel} = \frac{\rho_{sample}}{\rho_{water}}$
  - Bulk density and true density
  - Porosity $\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$

- **Thermal / Optical Tests**
  - Refractive Index (Abbe refractometer)
  - Thermal conductivity
  - Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)

- **Moisture Content Tests**
  - Oven drying method (gravimetric)
  - Distillation method (Toluene distillation for fats)
  - Karl Fischer titration (chemical)

#### B. Chemical Tests

- **Acidity-Related Tests**
  - pH measurement (potentiometric)
  - Titratable Acidity (acid-base titration)
  - Volatile acidity (steam distillation)
  - pH differential method (anthocyanin content)

- **Sugar-Related Tests**
  - Total Soluble Solids (°Brix by refractometer)
  - Reducing sugars (Lane-Eynon / DNS / Nelson-Somogyi)
  - Total sugars (after acid hydrolysis of sucrose)
  - Non-reducing sugars = Total sugars − Reducing sugars

- **Macronutrient Tests**
  - Proteins (Kjeldahl, Biuret, Lowry, Bradford)
  - Fats (Soxhlet, Gerber, Babcock)
  - Carbohydrates (Anthrone, Phenol-sulfuric)
  - Crude fiber (acid-alkali digestion)
  - Ash content (muffle furnace @ 550 °C)

- **Lipid Quality Tests**
  - Peroxide Value (PV)
  - Thiobarbituric Acid Reactive Substances (TBARS)
  - Free Fatty Acids (FFA)
  - Iodine Value (IV)
  - Saponification Value (SV)

- **Physiological Activity Tests**
  - Respiration rate (CO₂ evolution / O₂ uptake)
  - Ethylene production (Gas Chromatography)
  - Enzyme assays (POD, PPO, PME, PG, LOX)

- **Spoilage-Specific Chemical Tests**
  - K-value (freshness index for fish/seafood)
  - TVB-N (Total Volatile Basic Nitrogen)
  - Biogenic amines (Histamine)
  - Mycotoxin assays (Aflatoxin, Ochratoxin)
  - Salt content (Mohr method, Volhard method)

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — The Underlying Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why-How Framework (Examination Favored):**

- **Why do we test?** Because **post-harvest foods are biologically active** — they respire, transpire, ripen, senesce, and are attacked by microbes. These processes change the food's **physical form** (wilting, shriveling, softening) and **chemical makeup** (acid accumulation, sugar depletion, lipid oxidation, protein breakdown). Tests **quantify these changes** into a **numerical index** that can be compared against **regulatory standards** (FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, BIS).

- **How do tests work?** Physical tests exploit **Newtonian laws** (mass, force, geometry) and **optical laws** (refraction, absorption, scattering). Chemical tests exploit **stoichiometric reactions** (acid-base, redox, complexometric) where a known reagent reacts in a 1:1 mole ratio with the analyte, allowing **back-calculation** of the analyte's concentration.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU 2024 Module 4**

| # | Test | Formula | Unit / Standard |
|---|------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % w.b. |
| 2 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$ | % d.b. |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{m_{sample}}{m_{water}}$ at $T$ °C | dimensionless |
| 4 | Titratable Acidity | $\% \text{Acid} = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E}{V_{sample}} \times 100$ | % acid |
| 5 | Brix : TSS Ratio | $1 \text{ °Brix} = 1\% \text{ sucrose (w/w) at 20 °C}$ | °Brix |
| 6 | Peroxide Value | $PV = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq O₂/kg fat |
| 7 | Free Fatty Acids | $\% FFA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | % oleic acid |
| 8 | Crude Protein (Kjeldahl) | $\% Protein = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1.401 \times F}{W}$ | % protein |
| 9 | K-value (Freshness) | $K = \dfrac{HxR + Hx}{ATP + ADP + AMP + IMP + HxR + Hx} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Respiration Rate | $R_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V_{CO_2} \times 60}{m \times t}$ | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 11 | Total Color Difference | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_1-L_2)^2 + (a_1-a_2)^2 + (b_1-b_2)^2}$ | CIELAB units |
| 12 | Anthocyanin Content | $A = \dfrac{A_{520} - A_{700}}{ \varepsilon \times \ell}$ | mg·100 g⁻¹ |
| 13 | Shape Index | $SI = L / D$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Browning Index | $BI = \dfrac{100(x - 0.31)}{0.172}$ where $x = (a^* + L^*) / b^*$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Water Activity | $a_w = p / p_0$ | 0 to 1.00 |

> **Note on units in tables:** $\vert x \vert$ notation has been intentionally avoided; all absolute values are represented with their full symbol to prevent table corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Cold Storage Industries** use respiration rate tests to design **ventilation systems** and **CA/MA packaging** (Controlled Atmosphere / Modified Atmosphere).
- **Export Quality Control** uses °Brix, acidity, and color to grade **mangoes, grapes, citrus, and pomegranates** for international shipment.
- **Fisheries Industry** uses the **K-value** to determine whether fish is suitable for **sushi (K < 20%)**, sashimi (K < 40%), or only cooking (K > 60%).
- **Edible Oil Industry** uses **Peroxide Value** to decide whether oil is **refined, blended, or rejected** as rancid.
- **Baking Industry** uses **Falling Number / Amylograph** to assess **α-amylase activity** in flour.
- **Brewing Industry** uses **pH, titratable acidity, and Brix** to monitor **fermentation kinetics** in real time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content by Oven Drying Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Water is evaporated from a pre-weighed food sample by heating in a hot-air oven at a specified temperature (typically 105 °C for cereals, 70 °C under vacuum for sugar-rich foods) until constant mass is achieved. The mass loss corresponds to moisture driven off.

**Step 1 — Record initial mass of empty moisture dish (W₀):**

$$W_0 = \text{mass of empty, dried moisture dish (g)}$$

**Step 2 — Weigh the wet food sample into the dish (W₁):**

$$W_1 = \text{mass of dish} + \text{wet sample (g)}$$

**Step 3 — Dry the sample in the oven until constant mass is attained (W₂):**

$$W_2 = \text{mass of dish} + \text{dry sample (g)}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water evaporated:**

$$\Delta W = W_1 - W_2 \quad (\text{grams of water})$$

**Step 5 — Mass of dry solids in the sample:**

$$W_{solids} = W_2 - W_0 \quad (\text{grams})$$

**Step 6 — Moisture content on wet basis (most common in food industry):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wb}(\%) &= \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Mass of wet sample}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Moisture content on dry basis (used in grain storage engineering):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{db}(\%) &= \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Mass of dry sample}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2 - W_0} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 5.00 g wet food sample is dried in an oven. The empty dish weighs 20.00 g. After drying, the dish + sample weighs 23.80 g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0 &= 20.00 \text{ g} \\
W_1 &= 20.00 + 5.00 = 25.00 \text{ g} \\
W_2 &= 23.80 \text{ g} \\
M_{wb} &= \frac{25.00 - 23.80}{25.00 - 20.00} \times 100 = \frac{1.20}{5.00} \times 100 = 24.00 \% \\
M_{db} &= \frac{25.00 - 23.80}{23.80 - 20.00} \times 100 = \frac{1.20}{3.80} \times 100 = 31.58 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Titratable Acidity (Acide-Base Titration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Organic acids (citric, malic, lactic, acetic) in food are neutralized by a standardized NaOH solution using phenolphthalein indicator. The volume of NaOH consumed is stoichiometrically proportional to the acid concentration.

**Step 1 — Sample preparation:** A known mass (or volume) of food sample is diluted with distilled water (phenolphthalein endpoint is best in the range pH 8.1–8.3).

**Step 2 — Titration with standardized NaOH:**

$$n_{NaOH} = V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \quad (\text{milliequivalents of NaOH})$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric equivalence:** $n_{acid} = n_{NaOH}$ (1:1 for monoprotic; or appropriate factor for polyprotic acids).

**Step 4 — Mass of predominant acid (using equivalent weight E):**

$$
\text{Mass of acid (g)} = V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage acidity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Acid} &= \frac{\text{Mass of acid}}{\text{Mass of sample}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E \times 100}{V_{sample} \text{ (mL)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Equivalent Weights used in food industry:**
> - Citric acid: $E = 0.064 \text{ g/meq}$ (for citrus, tomato, pineapple)
> - Malic acid: $E = 0.067 \text{ g/meq}$ (for apple, pear)
> - Lactic acid: $E = 0.090 \text{ g/meq}$ (for dairy, fermented foods)
> - Acetic acid: $E = 0.060 \text{ g/meq}$ (for vinegar, pickles)
> - Tartaric acid: $E = 0.075 \text{ g/meq}$ (for grapes, wine)

**Numerical Example:** 10 mL of fruit juice is titrated with 0.1 N NaOH. The endpoint is reached at 8.5 mL of NaOH. Calculate % citric acid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Citric Acid} &= \frac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.064 \times 100}{10} \\
&= \frac{0.85 \times 0.064 \times 100}{10} \\
&= \frac{5.44}{10} \\
&= 0.544 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peroxide Value (PV) of Edible Oils

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Peroxides (primary oxidation products of unsaturated fats) react with potassium iodide (KI) in an acidic medium to liberate iodine (I₂), which is back-titrated with standardized sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃) using starch as indicator.

**Step 1 — Liberation of I₂ from KI by peroxides:**

$$ROOR' + 2KI + 2H^+ \rightarrow ROH + R'OH + I_2 + 2K^+$$

**Step 2 — Back-titration of liberated I₂ with Na₂S₂O₃:**

$$I_2 + 2Na_2S_2O_3 \rightarrow 2NaI + Na_2S_4O_6$$

**Step 3 — Equivalence:** 1 mole of I₂ ≡ 2 moles of Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 1 mole of O₂ (active oxygen in peroxide).

**Step 4 — PV formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV \left(\frac{\text{meq O}_2}{\text{kg}}\right) &= \frac{(S - B) \times N_{thiosulfate} \times 1000}{W_{oil}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $S$ = volume of Na₂S₂O₃ for sample (mL)
- $B$ = volume of Na₂S₂O₃ for blank (mL)
- $N$ = normality of Na₂S₂O₃ (eq/L)
- $W$ = mass of oil sample (g)
- **FSSAI Limit for refined oil:** $PV \le 10 \text{ meq O}_2/\text{kg}$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — K-value for Fish Freshness

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** After fish death, ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is enzymatically degraded through a sequence: ATP → ADP → AMP → IMP (inosine monophosphate) → HxR (inosine) → Hx (hypoxanthine) → X (xanthine) → U (uric acid). The accumulation of HxR and Hx is a sensitive indicator of freshness loss. The K-value expresses the percentage of ATP-breakdown products that have lost their "fresh-tasting" IMP identity.

**ATP Degradation Pathway:**

$$
ATP \xrightarrow{ATPase} ADP \xrightarrow{AMPdeaminase} AMP \xrightarrow{5'-nucleotidase} IMP \xrightarrow{nucleotidase} HxR \xrightarrow{np} Hx
$$

**Step 1 — Identification of "fresh" components (numerator below):**

$$\text{Numerator: } [HxR] + [Hx]$$

**Step 2 — Total ATP-related compounds (denominator):**

$$\text{Denominator: } [ATP] + [ADP] + [AMP] + [IMP] + [HxR] + [Hx]$$

**Step 3 — K-value definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\%) &= \frac{[HxR] + [Hx]}{[ATP] + [ADP] + [AMP] + [IMP] + [HxR] + [Hx]} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation Scale (KTU standard):**

| K-value | Quality Assessment |
|---------|-------------------|
| $K < 20\%$ | Sushi-grade (very fresh) |
| $20 \le K < 40\%$ | Sashimi-grade (fresh) |
| $40 \le K < 60\%$ | Cooking only (acceptable) |
| $K \ge 60\%$ | Spoiled / Not for consumption |

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Protein Content by Kjeldahl Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Organic nitrogen in food is converted to ammonium sulfate by digestion with concentrated H₂SO₄ + catalyst, then liberated as ammonia (NH₃) by alkali, distilled into a known excess of boric acid, and back-titrated with standard HCl.

**Step 1 — Digestion:**

$$N_{organic} + H_2SO_4 \xrightarrow{CuSO_4/Na_2SO_4, \Delta} (NH_4)_2SO_4$$

**Step 2 — Alkaline liberation of ammonia:**

$$(NH_4)_2SO_4 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2NH_3 \uparrow + 2H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Distillation absorption (NH₃ trapped in boric acid):**

$$NH_3 + H_3BO_3 \rightarrow NH_4H_2BO_3 \text{ (ammonium borate)}$$

**Step 4 — Back-titration of trapped ammonia with HCl:**

$$NH_4H_2BO_3 + HCl \rightarrow NH_4Cl + H_3BO_3$$

**Step 5 — Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% N &= \frac{(S - B) \times N_{HCl} \times 1.401}{W_{sample}} \\
\% \text{ Protein} &= \% N \times F
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F$ is the **nitrogen-to-protein conversion factor**:
- $F = 6.25$ for general foods (assumes 16% N in protein)
- $F = 5.70$ for wheat and cereals
- $F = 5.18$ for milk and dairy
- $F = 6.38$ for casein

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Test by Spoilage Symptom

| Spoilage Symptom | Primary Physical Test | Primary Chemical Test | Confirmatory Test |
|------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Wilting in leafy greens | Mass loss, % | $a_w$ measurement | Chilling injury index |
| Softening in fruits | Penetration (N) | PME / PG enzyme assay | TSS (°Brix) |
| Browning (cut apple) | Color ($L^*$, $\Delta E$) | PPO enzyme activity | Total phenolics |
| Rancidity in oil | Refractive index | Peroxide value, FFA | TBARS, anisidine value |
| Fish spoilage | Eye clarity, gill color | K-value, TVB-N | Biogenic amines |
| Milk spoilage | Curd formation | pH, titratable acidity | Alcohol test, Clot-on-boiling |
| Bread staling | TPA (firmness) | Moisture, $a_w$ | Falling number |
| Microbial spoilage | TPC, yeast & mold | pH drop, gas formation | H₂S test, dye reduction |
